For introductory material, see the :doc:`settings topic\n    22\tguide </topics/settings>`.\n    23\t\n    24\t.. setting:: ABSOLUTE_URL_OVERRIDES\n    25\t\n    26\t``ABSOLUTE_URL_OVERRIDES``\n    27\t--------------------------\n    28\t\n    29\tDefault: ``{}`` (Empty dictionary)\n    30\t\n    31\tA dictionary mapping ``\"app_label.model_name\"`` strings to functions that take\n    32\ta model object and return its URL. This is a way of inserting or overriding\n    33\t``get_absolute_url()`` methods on a per-installation basis. Example::\n    34\t\n    35\t    ABSOLUTE_URL_OVERRIDES = {\n    36\t        'blogs.blog': lambda o: \"/blogs/%s/\" % o.slug,\n    37\t        'news.story': lambda o: \"/stories/%s/%s/\" % (o.pub_year, o.slug),\n    38\t    }\n    39\t\n    40\tThe model name used in this setting should be all lowercase, regardless of the\n    41\tcase of the actual model class name.\n    42\t\n    43\t.. setting:: ADMINS\n    44\t\n    45\t``ADMINS``\n    46\t----------\n    47\t\n    48\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n    49\t\n    50\tA list of all the people who get code error notifications. When\n    51\t:setting:`DEBUG=False <DEBUG>` and :class:`~django.utils.log.AdminEmailHandler`\n    52\tis configured in :setting:`LOGGING` (done by default), Django emails these\n    53\tpeople the details of exceptions raised in the request/response cycle.\n    54\t\n    55\tEach item in the list should be a tuple of (Full name, email address). Example::\n    56\t\n    57\t    [('John', 'john@example.com'), ('Mary', 'mary@example.com')]\n    58\t\n    59\t.. setting:: ALLOWED_HOSTS\n    60\t\n    61\t``ALLOWED_HOSTS``\n    62\t-----------------\n    63\t\n    64\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n    65\t\n    66\tA list of strings representing the host/domain names that this Django site can\n    67\tserve. This is a security measure to prevent :ref:`HTTP Host header attacks\n    68\t<host-headers-virtual-hosting>`, which are possible even under many\n    69\tseemingly-safe web server configurations.\n    70\t\n    71\tValues in this list can be fully qualified names (e.g. ``'www.example.com'``),\n    72\tin which case they will be matched against the request's ``Host`` header\n    73\texactly (case-insensitive, not including port). A value beginning with a period\n    74\tcan be used as a subdomain wildcard: ``'.example.com'`` will match\n    75\t``example.com``, ``www.example.com``, and any other subdomain of\n    76\t``example.com``. A value of ``'*'`` will match anything; in this case you are\n    77\tresponsible to provide your own validation of the ``Host`` header (perhaps in a\n    78\tmiddleware; if so this middleware must be listed first in\n    79\t:setting:`MIDDLEWARE`).\n    80\t\n    81\tDjango also allows the `fully qualified domain name (FQDN)`_ of any entries.\n    82\tSome browsers include a trailing dot in the ``Host`` header which Django\n    83\tstrips when performing host validation.\n    84\t\n    85\t.. _`fully qualified domain name (FQDN)`: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fully_qualified_domain_name\n    86\t\n    87\tIf the ``Host`` header (or ``X-Forwarded-Host`` if\n    88\t:setting:`USE_X_FORWARDED_HOST` is enabled) does not match any value in this\n    89\tlist, the :meth:`django.http.HttpRequest.get_host()` method will raise\n    90\t:exc:`~django.core.exceptions.SuspiciousOperation`.\n    91\t\n    92\tWhen :setting:`DEBUG` is ``True`` and ``ALLOWED_HOSTS`` is empty, the host\n    93\tis validated against ``['.localhost', '127.0.0.1', '[::1]']``.\n    94\t\n    95\t``ALLOWED_HOSTS`` is also :ref:`checked when running tests\n    96\t<topics-testing-advanced-multiple-hosts>`.\n    97\t\n    98\tThis validation only applies via :meth:`~django.http.HttpRequest.get_host()`;\n    99\tif your code accesses the ``Host`` header directly from ``request.META`` you\n   100\tare bypassing this security protection.\n   101\t\n   102\t.. setting:: APPEND_SLASH\n   103\t\n   104\t``APPEND_SLASH``\n   105\t----------------\n   106\t\n   107\tDefault: ``True``\n   108\t\n   109\tWhen set to ``True``, if the request URL does not match any of the patterns\n   110\tin the URLconf and it doesn't end in a slash, an HTTP redirect is issued to the\n   111\tsame URL with a slash appended. Note that the redirect may cause any data\n   112\tsubmitted in a POST request to be lost.\n   113\t\n   114\tThe :setting:`APPEND_SLASH` setting is only used if\n   115\t:class:`~django.middleware.common.CommonMiddleware` is installed\n   116\t(see :doc:`/topics/http/middleware`). See also :setting:`PREPEND_WWW`.\n   117\t\n   118\t.. setting:: CACHES\n   119\t\n   120\t``CACHES``\n   121\t----------\n   122\t\n   123\tDefault::\n   124\t\n   125\t    {\n   126\t        'default': {\n   127\t            'BACKEND': 'django.core.cache.backends.locmem.LocMemCache',\n   128\t        }\n   129\t    }\n   130\t\n   131\tA dictionary containing the settings for all caches to be used with\n   132\tDjango. It is a nested dictionary whose contents maps cache aliases\n   133\tto a dictionary containing the options for an individual cache.\n   134\t\n   135\tThe :setting:`CACHES` setting must configure a ``default`` cache;\n   136\tany number of additional caches may also be specified. If you\n   137\tare using a cache backend other than the local memory cache, or\n   138\tyou need to define multiple caches, other options will be required.\n   139\tThe following cache options are available.\n   140\t\n   141\t.. setting:: CACHES-BACKEND\n   142\t\n   143\t``BACKEND``\n   144\t~~~~~~~~~~~\n   145\t\n   146\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   147\t\n   148\tThe cache backend to use. The built-in cache backends are:\n   149\t\n   150\t* ``'django.core.cache.backends.db.DatabaseCache'``\n   151\t* ``'django.core.cache.backends.dummy.DummyCache'``\n   152\t* ``'django.core.cache.backends.filebased.FileBasedCache'``\n   153\t* ``'django.core.cache.backends.locmem.LocMemCache'``\n   154\t* ``'django.core.cache.backends.memcached.PyMemcacheCache'``\n   155\t* ``'django.core.cache.backends.memcached.PyLibMCCache'``\n   156\t* ``'django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache'``\n   157\t\n   158\tYou can use a cache backend that doesn't ship with Django by setting\n   159\t:setting:`BACKEND <CACHES-BACKEND>` to a fully-qualified path of a cache\n   160\tbackend class (i.e. ``mypackage.backends.whatever.WhateverCache``).\n   161\t\n   162\t.. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   163\t\n   164\t    The ``PyMemcacheCache`` backend was added.\n   165\t\n   166\t.. versionchanged:: 4.0\n   167\t\n   168\t    The ``RedisCache`` backend was added.\n   169\t\n   170\t.. setting:: CACHES-KEY_FUNCTION\n   171\t\n   172\t``KEY_FUNCTION``\n   173\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   174\t\n   175\tA string containing a dotted path to a function (or any callable) that defines how to\n   176\tcompose a prefix, version and key into a final cache key. The default\n   177\timplementation is equivalent to the function::\n   178\t\n   179\t    def make_key(key, key_prefix, version):\n   180\t        return ':'.join([key_prefix, str(version), key])\n   181\t\n   182\tYou may use any key function you want, as long as it has the same\n   183\targument signature.\n   184\t\n   185\tSee the :ref:`cache documentation <cache_key_transformation>` for more\n   186\tinformation.\n   187\t\n   188\t.. setting:: CACHES-KEY_PREFIX\n   189\t\n   190\t``KEY_PREFIX``\n   191\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   192\t\n   193\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   194\t\n   195\tA string that will be automatically included (prepended by default) to\n   196\tall cache keys used by the Django server.\n   197\t\n   198\tSee the :ref:`cache documentation <cache_key_prefixing>` for more information.\n   199\t\n   200\t.. setting:: CACHES-LOCATION\n   201\t\n   202\t``LOCATION``\n   203\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   204\t\n   205\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   206\t\n   207\tThe location of the cache to use. This might be the directory for a\n   208\tfile system cache, a host and port for a memcache server, or an identifying\n   209\tname for a local memory cache. e.g.::\n   210\t\n   211\t    CACHES = {\n   212\t        'default': {\n   213\t            'BACKEND': 'django.core.cache.backends.filebased.FileBasedCache',\n   214\t            'LOCATION': '/var/tmp/django_cache',\n   215\t        }\n   216\t    }\n   217\t\n   218\t.. setting:: CACHES-OPTIONS\n   219\t\n   220\t``OPTIONS``\n   221\t~~~~~~~~~~~\n   222\t\n   223\tDefault: ``None``\n   224\t\n   225\tExtra parameters to pass to the cache backend. Available parameters\n   226\tvary depending on your cache backend.\n   227\t\n   228\tSome information on available parameters can be found in the\n   229\t:ref:`cache arguments <cache_arguments>` documentation. For more information,\n   230\tconsult your backend module's own documentation.\n   231\t\n   232\t.. setting:: CACHES-TIMEOUT\n   233\t\n   234\t``TIMEOUT``\n   235\t~~~~~~~~~~~\n   236\t\n   237\tDefault: ``300``\n   238\t\n   239\tThe number of seconds before a cache entry is considered stale. If the value of\n   240\tthis setting is ``None``, cache entries will not expire. A value of ``0``\n   241\tcauses keys to immediately expire (effectively \"don't cache\").\n   242\t\n   243\t.. setting:: CACHES-VERSION\n   244\t\n   245\t``VERSION``\n   246\t~~~~~~~~~~~\n   247\t\n   248\tDefault: ``1``\n   249\t\n   250\tThe default version number for cache keys generated by the Django server.\n   251\t\n   252\tSee the :ref:`cache documentation <cache_versioning>` for more information.\n   253\t\n   254\t.. setting:: CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_ALIAS\n   255\t\n   256\t``CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_ALIAS``\n   257\t--------------------------\n   258\t\n   259\tDefault: ``'default'``\n   260\t\n   261\tThe cache connection to use for the :ref:`cache middleware\n   262\t<the-per-site-cache>`.\n   263\t\n   264\t.. setting:: CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_KEY_PREFIX\n   265\t\n   266\t``CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_KEY_PREFIX``\n   267\t-------------------------------\n   268\t\n   269\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   270\t\n   271\tA string which will be prefixed to the cache keys generated by the :ref:`cache\n   272\tmiddleware <the-per-site-cache>`. This prefix is combined with the\n   273\t:setting:`KEY_PREFIX <CACHES-KEY_PREFIX>` setting; it does not replace it.\n   274\t\n   275\tSee :doc:`/topics/cache`.\n   276\t\n   277\t.. setting:: CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_SECONDS\n   278\t\n   279\t``CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_SECONDS``\n   280\t----------------------------\n   281\t\n   282\tDefault: ``600``\n   283\t\n   284\tThe default number of seconds to cache a page for the :ref:`cache middleware\n   285\t<the-per-site-cache>`.\n   286\t\n   287\tSee :doc:`/topics/cache`.\n   288\t\n   289\t.. _settings-csrf:\n   290\t\n   291\t.. setting:: CSRF_COOKIE_AGE\n   292\t\n   293\t``CSRF_COOKIE_AGE``\n   294\t-------------------\n   295\t\n   296\tDefault: ``31449600`` (approximately 1 year, in seconds)\n   297\t\n   298\tThe age of CSRF cookies, in seconds.\n   299\t\n   300\tThe reason for setting a long-lived expiration time is to avoid problems in\n   301\tthe case of a user closing a browser or bookmarking a page and then loading\n   302\tthat page from a browser cache. Without persistent cookies, the form submission\n   303\twould fail in this case.\n   304\t\n   305\tSome browsers (specifically Internet Explorer) can disallow the use of\n   306\tpersistent cookies or can have the indexes to the cookie jar corrupted on disk,\n   307\tthereby causing CSRF protection checks to (sometimes intermittently) fail.\n   308\tChange this setting to ``None`` to use session-based CSRF cookies, which\n   309\tkeep the cookies in-memory instead of on persistent storage.\n   310\t\n   311\t.. setting:: CSRF_COOKIE_DOMAIN\n   312\t\n   313\t``CSRF_COOKIE_DOMAIN``\n   314\t----------------------\n   315\t\n   316\tDefault: ``None``\n   317\t\n   318\tThe domain to be used when setting the CSRF cookie.  This can be useful for\n   319\teasily allowing cross-subdomain requests to be excluded from the normal cross\n   320\tsite request forgery protection.  It should be set to a string such as\n   321\t``\".example.com\"`` to allow a POST request from a form on one subdomain to be\n   322\taccepted by a view served from another subdomain.\n   323\t\n   324\tPlease note that the presence of this setting does not imply that Django's CSRF\n   325\tprotection is safe from cross-subdomain attacks by default - please see the\n   326\t:ref:`CSRF limitations <csrf-limitations>` section.\n   327\t\n   328\t.. setting:: CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY\n   329\t\n   330\t``CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY``\n   331\t------------------------\n   332\t\n   333\tDefault: ``False``\n   334\t\n   335\tWhether to use ``HttpOnly`` flag on the CSRF cookie. If this is set to\n   336\t``True``, client-side JavaScript will not be able to access the CSRF cookie.\n   337\t\n   338\tDesignating the CSRF cookie as ``HttpOnly`` doesn't offer any practical\n   339\tprotection because CSRF is only to protect against cross-domain attacks. If an\n   340\tattacker can read the cookie via JavaScript, they're already on the same domain\n   341\tas far as the browser knows, so they can do anything they like anyway. (XSS is\n   342\ta much bigger hole than CSRF.)\n   343\t\n   344\tAlthough the setting offers little practical benefit, it's sometimes required\n   345\tby security auditors.\n   346\t\n   347\tIf you enable this and need to send the value of the CSRF token with an AJAX\n   348\trequest, your JavaScript must pull the value :ref:`from a hidden CSRF token\n   349\tform input <acquiring-csrf-token-from-html>` instead of :ref:`from the cookie\n   350\t<acquiring-csrf-token-from-cookie>`.\n   351\t\n   352\tSee :setting:`SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY` for details on ``HttpOnly``.\n   353\t\n   354\t.. setting:: CSRF_COOKIE_NAME\n   355\t\n   356\t``CSRF_COOKIE_NAME``\n   357\t--------------------\n   358\t\n   359\tDefault: ``'csrftoken'``\n   360\t\n   361\tThe name of the cookie to use for the CSRF authentication token. This can be\n   362\twhatever you want (as long as it's different from the other cookie names in\n   363\tyour application). See :doc:`/ref/csrf`.\n   364\t\n   365\t.. setting:: CSRF_COOKIE_PATH\n   366\t\n   367\t``CSRF_COOKIE_PATH``\n   368\t--------------------\n   369\t\n   370\tDefault: ``'/'``\n   371\t\n   372\tThe path set on the CSRF cookie. This should either match the URL path of your\n   373\tDjango installation or be a parent of that path.\n   374\t\n   375\tThis is useful if you have multiple Django instances running under the same\n   376\thostname. They can use different cookie paths, and each instance will only see\n   377\tits own CSRF cookie.\n   378\t\n   379\t.. setting:: CSRF_COOKIE_SAMESITE\n   380\t\n   381\t``CSRF_COOKIE_SAMESITE``\n   382\t------------------------\n   383\t\n   384\tDefault: ``'Lax'``\n   385\t\n   386\tThe value of the `SameSite`_ flag on the CSRF cookie. This flag prevents the\n   387\tcookie from being sent in cross-site requests.\n   388\t\n   389\tSee :setting:`SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE` for details about ``SameSite``.\n   390\t\n   391\t.. setting:: CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE\n   392\t\n   393\t``CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE``\n   394\t----------------------\n   395\t\n   396\tDefault: ``False``\n   397\t\n   398\tWhether to use a secure cookie for the CSRF cookie. If this is set to ``True``,\n   399\tthe cookie will be marked as \"secure\", which means browsers may ensure that the\n   400\tcookie is only sent with an HTTPS connection.\n   401\t\n   402\t.. setting:: CSRF_USE_SESSIONS\n   403\t\n   404\t``CSRF_USE_SESSIONS``\n   405\t---------------------\n   406\t\n   407\tDefault: ``False``\n   408\t\n   409\tWhether to store the CSRF token in the user's session instead of in a cookie.\n   410\tIt requires the use of :mod:`django.contrib.sessions`.\n   411\t\n   412\tStoring the CSRF token in a cookie (Django's default) is safe, but storing it\n   413\tin the session is common practice in other web frameworks and therefore\n   414\tsometimes demanded by security auditors.\n   415\t\n   416\tSince the :ref:`default error views <error-views>` require the CSRF token,\n   417\t:class:`~django.contrib.sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware` must appear in\n   418\t:setting:`MIDDLEWARE` before any middleware that may raise an exception to\n   419\ttrigger an error view (such as :exc:`~django.core.exceptions.PermissionDenied`)\n   420\tif you're using ``CSRF_USE_SESSIONS``. See :ref:`middleware-ordering`.\n   421\t\n   422\t.. setting:: CSRF_FAILURE_VIEW\n   423\t\n   424\t``CSRF_FAILURE_VIEW``\n   425\t---------------------\n   426\t\n   427\tDefault: ``'django.views.csrf.csrf_failure'``\n   428\t\n   429\tA dotted path to the view function to be used when an incoming request is\n   430\trejected by the :doc:`CSRF protection </ref/csrf>`. The function should have\n   431\tthis signature::\n   432\t\n   433\t    def csrf_failure(request, reason=\"\"):\n   434\t        ...\n   435\t\n   436\twhere ``reason`` is a short message (intended for developers or logging, not\n   437\tfor end users) indicating the reason the request was rejected. It should return\n   438\tan :class:`~django.http.HttpResponseForbidden`.\n   439\t\n   440\t``django.views.csrf.csrf_failure()`` accepts an additional ``template_name``\n   441\tparameter that defaults to ``'403_csrf.html'``. If a template with that name\n   442\texists, it will be used to render the page.\n   443\t\n   444\t.. setting:: CSRF_HEADER_NAME\n   445\t\n   446\t``CSRF_HEADER_NAME``\n   447\t--------------------\n   448\t\n   449\tDefault: ``'HTTP_X_CSRFTOKEN'``\n   450\t\n   451\tThe name of the request header used for CSRF authentication.\n   452\t\n   453\tAs with other HTTP headers in ``request.META``, the header name received from\n   454\tthe server is normalized by converting all characters to uppercase, replacing\n   455\tany hyphens with underscores, and adding an ``'HTTP_'`` prefix to the name.\n   456\tFor example, if your client sends a ``'X-XSRF-TOKEN'`` header, the setting\n   457\tshould be ``'HTTP_X_XSRF_TOKEN'``.\n   458\t\n   459\t.. setting:: CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS\n   460\t\n   461\t``CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS``\n   462\t------------------------\n   463\t\n   464\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n   465\t\n   466\tA list of trusted origins for unsafe requests (e.g. ``POST``).\n   467\t\n   468\tFor requests that include the ``Origin`` header, Django's CSRF protection\n   469\trequires that header match the origin present in the ``Host`` header.\n   470\t\n   471\tFor a :meth:`secure <django.http.HttpRequest.is_secure>` unsafe\n   472\trequest that doesn't include the ``Origin`` header, the request must have a\n   473\t``Referer`` header that matches the origin present in the ``Host`` header.\n   474\t\n   475\tThese checks prevent, for example, a ``POST`` request from\n   476\t``subdomain.example.com`` from succeeding against ``api.example.com``. If you\n   477\tneed cross-origin unsafe requests, continuing the example, add\n   478\t``'https://subdomain.example.com'`` to this list (and/or ``http://...`` if\n   479\trequests originate from an insecure page).\n   480\t\n   481\tThe setting also supports subdomains, so you could add\n   482\t``'https://*.example.com'``, for example, to allow access from all subdomains\n   483\tof ``example.com``.\n   484\t\n   485\t.. versionchanged:: 4.0\n   486\t\n   487\t    The values in older versions must only include the hostname (possibly with\n   488\t    a leading dot) and not the scheme or an asterisk.\n   489\t\n   490\t    Also, ``Origin`` header checking isn't performed in older versions.\n   491\t\n   492\t.. setting:: DATABASES\n   493\t\n   494\t``DATABASES``\n   495\t-------------\n   496\t\n   497\tDefault: ``{}`` (Empty dictionary)\n   498\t\n   499\tA dictionary containing the settings for all databases to be used with\n   500\tDjango. It is a nested dictionary whose contents map a database alias\n   501\tto a dictionary containing the options for an individual database.\n   502\t\n   503\tThe :setting:`DATABASES` setting must configure a ``default`` database;\n   504\tany number of additional databases may also be specified.\n   505\t\n   506\tThe simplest possible settings file is for a single-database setup using\n   507\tSQLite. This can be configured using the following::\n   508\t\n   509\t    DATABASES = {\n   510\t        'default': {\n   511\t            'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3',\n   512\t            'NAME': 'mydatabase',\n   513\t        }\n   514\t    }\n   515\t\n   516\tWhen connecting to other database backends, such as MariaDB, MySQL, Oracle, or\n   517\tPostgreSQL, additional connection parameters will be required. See\n   518\tthe :setting:`ENGINE <DATABASE-ENGINE>` setting below on how to specify\n   519\tother database types. This example is for PostgreSQL::\n   520\t\n   521\t    DATABASES = {\n   522\t        'default': {\n   523\t            'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',\n   524\t            'NAME': 'mydatabase',\n   525\t            'USER': 'mydatabaseuser',\n   526\t            'PASSWORD': 'mypassword',\n   527\t            'HOST': '127.0.0.1',\n   528\t            'PORT': '5432',\n   529\t        }\n   530\t    }\n   531\t\n   532\tThe following inner options that may be required for more complex\n   533\tconfigurations are available:\n   534\t\n   535\t.. setting:: DATABASE-ATOMIC_REQUESTS\n   536\t\n   537\t``ATOMIC_REQUESTS``\n   538\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   539\t\n   540\tDefault: ``False``\n   541\t\n   542\tSet this to ``True`` to wrap each view in a transaction on this database. See\n   543\t:ref:`tying-transactions-to-http-requests`.\n   544\t\n   545\t.. setting:: DATABASE-AUTOCOMMIT\n   546\t\n   547\t``AUTOCOMMIT``\n   548\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   549\t\n   550\tDefault: ``True``\n   551\t\n   552\tSet this to ``False`` if you want to :ref:`disable Django's transaction\n   553\tmanagement <deactivate-transaction-management>` and implement your own.\n   554\t\n   555\t.. setting:: DATABASE-ENGINE\n   556\t\n   557\t``ENGINE``\n   558\t~~~~~~~~~~\n   559\t\n   560\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   561\t\n   562\tThe database backend to use. The built-in database backends are:\n   563\t\n   564\t* ``'django.db.backends.postgresql'``\n   565\t* ``'django.db.backends.mysql'``\n   566\t* ``'django.db.backends.sqlite3'``\n   567\t* ``'django.db.backends.oracle'``\n   568\t\n   569\tYou can use a database backend that doesn't ship with Django by setting\n   570\t``ENGINE`` to a fully-qualified path (i.e. ``mypackage.backends.whatever``).\n   571\t\n   572\t.. setting:: HOST\n   573\t\n   574\t``HOST``\n   575\t~~~~~~~~\n   576\t\n   577\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   578\t\n   579\tWhich host to use when connecting to the database. An empty string means\n   580\tlocalhost. Not used with SQLite.\n   581\t\n   582\tIf this value starts with a forward slash (``'/'``) and you're using MySQL,\n   583\tMySQL will connect via a Unix socket to the specified socket. For example::\n   584\t\n   585\t    \"HOST\": '/var/run/mysql'\n   586\t\n   587\tIf you're using MySQL and this value *doesn't* start with a forward slash, then\n   588\tthis value is assumed to be the host.\n   589\t\n   590\tIf you're using PostgreSQL, by default (empty :setting:`HOST`), the connection\n   591\tto the database is done through UNIX domain sockets ('local' lines in\n   592\t``pg_hba.conf``). If your UNIX domain socket is not in the standard location,\n   593\tuse the same value of ``unix_socket_directory`` from ``postgresql.conf``.\n   594\tIf you want to connect through TCP sockets, set :setting:`HOST` to 'localhost'\n   595\tor '127.0.0.1' ('host' lines in ``pg_hba.conf``).\n   596\tOn Windows, you should always define :setting:`HOST`, as UNIX domain sockets\n   597\tare not available.\n   598\t\n   599\t.. setting:: NAME\n   600\t\n   601\t``NAME``\n   602\t~~~~~~~~\n   603\t\n   604\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   605\t\n   606\tThe name of the database to use. For SQLite, it's the full path to the database\n   607\tfile. When specifying the path, always use forward slashes, even on Windows\n   608\t(e.g. ``C:/homes/user/mysite/sqlite3.db``).\n   609\t\n   610\t.. setting:: CONN_MAX_AGE\n   611\t\n   612\t``CONN_MAX_AGE``\n   613\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   614\t\n   615\tDefault: ``0``\n   616\t\n   617\tThe lifetime of a database connection, as an integer of seconds. Use ``0`` to\n   618\tclose database connections at the end of each request — Django's historical\n   619\tbehavior — and ``None`` for unlimited persistent connections.\n   620\t\n   621\t.. setting:: OPTIONS\n   622\t\n   623\t``OPTIONS``\n   624\t~~~~~~~~~~~\n   625\t\n   626\tDefault: ``{}`` (Empty dictionary)\n   627\t\n   628\tExtra parameters to use when connecting to the database. Available parameters\n   629\tvary depending on your database backend.\n   630\t\n   631\tSome information on available parameters can be found in the\n   632\t:doc:`Database Backends </ref/databases>` documentation. For more information,\n   633\tconsult your backend module's own documentation.\n   634\t\n   635\t.. setting:: PASSWORD\n   636\t\n   637\t``PASSWORD``\n   638\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   639\t\n   640\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   641\t\n   642\tThe password to use when connecting to the database. Not used with SQLite.\n   643\t\n   644\t.. setting:: PORT\n   645\t\n   646\t``PORT``\n   647\t~~~~~~~~\n   648\t\n   649\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   650\t\n   651\tThe port to use when connecting to the database. An empty string means the\n   652\tdefault port. Not used with SQLite.\n   653\t\n   654\t.. setting:: DATABASE-TIME_ZONE\n   655\t\n   656\t``TIME_ZONE``\n   657\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   658\t\n   659\tDefault: ``None``\n   660\t\n   661\tA string representing the time zone for this database connection or ``None``.\n   662\tThis inner option of the :setting:`DATABASES` setting accepts the same values\n   663\tas the general :setting:`TIME_ZONE` setting.\n   664\t\n   665\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True`` and this option is set, reading datetimes\n   666\tfrom the database returns aware datetimes in this time zone instead of UTC.\n   667\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``False``, it is an error to set this option.\n   668\t\n   669\t* If the database backend doesn't support time zones (e.g. SQLite, MySQL,\n   670\t  Oracle), Django reads and writes datetimes in local time according to this\n   671\t  option if it is set and in UTC if it isn't.\n   672\t\n   673\t  Changing the connection time zone changes how datetimes are read from and\n   674\t  written to the database.\n   675\t\n   676\t  * If Django manages the database and you don't have a strong reason to do\n   677\t    otherwise, you should leave this option unset. It's best to store datetimes\n   678\t    in UTC because it avoids ambiguous or nonexistent datetimes during daylight\n   679\t    saving time changes. Also, receiving datetimes in UTC keeps datetime\n   680\t    arithmetic simple — there's no need for the ``normalize()`` method provided\n   681\t    by pytz.\n   682\t\n   683\t  * If you're connecting to a third-party database that stores datetimes in a\n   684\t    local time rather than UTC, then you must set this option to the\n   685\t    appropriate time zone. Likewise, if Django manages the database but\n   686\t    third-party systems connect to the same database and expect to find\n   687\t    datetimes in local time, then you must set this option.\n   688\t\n   689\t* If the database backend supports time zones (e.g. PostgreSQL), the\n   690\t  ``TIME_ZONE`` option is very rarely needed. It can be changed at any time;\n   691\t  the database takes care of converting datetimes to the desired time zone.\n   692\t\n   693\t  Setting the time zone of the database connection may be useful for running\n   694\t  raw SQL queries involving date/time functions provided by the database, such\n   695\t  as ``date_trunc``, because their results depend on the time zone.\n   696\t\n   697\t  However, this has a downside: receiving all datetimes in local time makes\n   698\t  datetime arithmetic more tricky — you must call the ``normalize()`` method\n   699\t  provided by pytz after each operation.\n   700\t\n   701\t  Consider converting to local time explicitly with ``AT TIME ZONE`` in raw SQL\n   702\t  queries instead of setting the ``TIME_ZONE`` option.\n   703\t\n   704\t.. setting:: DATABASE-DISABLE_SERVER_SIDE_CURSORS\n   705\t\n   706\t``DISABLE_SERVER_SIDE_CURSORS``\n   707\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   708\t\n   709\tDefault: ``False``\n   710\t\n   711\tSet this to ``True`` if you want to disable the use of server-side cursors with\n   712\t:meth:`.QuerySet.iterator`. :ref:`transaction-pooling-server-side-cursors`\n   713\tdescribes the use case.\n   714\t\n   715\tThis is a PostgreSQL-specific setting.\n   716\t\n   717\t.. setting:: USER\n   718\t\n   719\t``USER``\n   720\t~~~~~~~~\n   721\t\n   722\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n   723\t\n   724\tThe username to use when connecting to the database. Not used with SQLite.\n   725\t\n   726\t.. setting:: DATABASE-TEST\n   727\t\n   728\t``TEST``\n   729\t~~~~~~~~\n   730\t\n   731\tDefault: ``{}`` (Empty dictionary)\n   732\t\n   733\tA dictionary of settings for test databases; for more details about the\n   734\tcreation and use of test databases, see :ref:`the-test-database`.\n   735\t\n   736\tHere's an example with a test database configuration::\n   737\t\n   738\t    DATABASES = {\n   739\t        'default': {\n   740\t            'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',\n   741\t            'USER': 'mydatabaseuser',\n   742\t            'NAME': 'mydatabase',\n   743\t            'TEST': {\n   744\t                'NAME': 'mytestdatabase',\n   745\t            },\n   746\t        },\n   747\t    }\n   748\t\n   749\tThe following keys in the ``TEST`` dictionary are available:\n   750\t\n   751\t.. setting:: TEST_CHARSET\n   752\t\n   753\t``CHARSET``\n   754\t^^^^^^^^^^^\n   755\t\n   756\tDefault: ``None``\n   757\t\n   758\tThe character set encoding used to create the test database. The value of this\n   759\tstring is passed directly through to the database, so its format is\n   760\tbackend-specific.\n   761\t\n   762\tSupported by the PostgreSQL_ (``postgresql``) and MySQL_ (``mysql``) backends.\n   763\t\n   764\t.. _PostgreSQL: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/multibyte.html\n   765\t.. _MySQL: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/en/charset-charsets.html\n   766\t\n   767\t.. setting:: TEST_COLLATION\n   768\t\n   769\t``COLLATION``\n   770\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   771\t\n   772\tDefault: ``None``\n   773\t\n   774\tThe collation order to use when creating the test database. This value is\n   775\tpassed directly to the backend, so its format is backend-specific.\n   776\t\n   777\tOnly supported for the ``mysql`` backend (see the `MySQL manual`_ for details).\n   778\t\n   779\t.. _MySQL manual: MySQL_\n   780\t\n   781\t.. setting:: TEST_DEPENDENCIES\n   782\t\n   783\t``DEPENDENCIES``\n   784\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   785\t\n   786\tDefault: ``['default']``, for all databases other than ``default``,\n   787\twhich has no dependencies.\n   788\t\n   789\tThe creation-order dependencies of the database. See the documentation\n   790\ton :ref:`controlling the creation order of test databases\n   791\t<topics-testing-creation-dependencies>` for details.\n   792\t\n   793\t.. setting:: TEST_MIGRATE\n   794\t\n   795\t``MIGRATE``\n   796\t^^^^^^^^^^^\n   797\t\n   798\tDefault: ``True``\n   799\t\n   800\tWhen set to ``False``, migrations won't run when creating the test database.\n   801\tThis is similar to setting ``None`` as a value in :setting:`MIGRATION_MODULES`,\n   802\tbut for all apps.\n   803\t\n   804\t.. setting:: TEST_MIRROR\n   805\t\n   806\t``MIRROR``\n   807\t^^^^^^^^^^\n   808\t\n   809\tDefault: ``None``\n   810\t\n   811\tThe alias of the database that this database should mirror during\n   812\ttesting.\n   813\t\n   814\tThis setting exists to allow for testing of primary/replica\n   815\t(referred to as master/slave by some databases)\n   816\tconfigurations of multiple databases. See the documentation on\n   817\t:ref:`testing primary/replica configurations\n   818\t<topics-testing-primaryreplica>` for details.\n   819\t\n   820\t.. setting:: TEST_NAME\n   821\t\n   822\t``NAME``\n   823\t^^^^^^^^\n   824\t\n   825\tDefault: ``None``\n   826\t\n   827\tThe name of database to use when running the test suite.\n   828\t\n   829\tIf the default value (``None``) is used with the SQLite database engine, the\n   830\ttests will use a memory resident database. For all other database engines the\n   831\ttest database will use the name ``'test_' + DATABASE_NAME``.\n   832\t\n   833\tSee :ref:`the-test-database`.\n   834\t\n   835\t.. setting:: TEST_SERIALIZE\n   836\t\n   837\t``SERIALIZE``\n   838\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   839\t\n   840\tBoolean value to control whether or not the default test runner serializes the\n   841\tdatabase into an in-memory JSON string before running tests (used to restore\n   842\tthe database state between tests if you don't have transactions). You can set\n   843\tthis to ``False`` to speed up creation time if you don't have any test classes\n   844\twith :ref:`serialized_rollback=True <test-case-serialized-rollback>`.\n   845\t\n   846\t.. deprecated:: 4.0\n   847\t\n   848\t   This setting is deprecated as it can be inferred from the\n   849\t   :attr:`~django.test.TestCase.databases` with the\n   850\t   :ref:`serialized_rollback <test-case-serialized-rollback>` option enabled.\n   851\t\n   852\t.. setting:: TEST_TEMPLATE\n   853\t\n   854\t``TEMPLATE``\n   855\t^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   856\t\n   857\tThis is a PostgreSQL-specific setting.\n   858\t\n   859\tThe name of a `template`_ (e.g. ``'template0'``) from which to create the test\n   860\tdatabase.\n   861\t\n   862\t.. _template: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-createdatabase.html\n   863\t\n   864\t.. setting:: TEST_CREATE\n   865\t\n   866\t``CREATE_DB``\n   867\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   868\t\n   869\tDefault: ``True``\n   870\t\n   871\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   872\t\n   873\tIf it is set to ``False``, the test tablespaces won't be automatically created\n   874\tat the beginning of the tests or dropped at the end.\n   875\t\n   876\t.. setting:: TEST_USER_CREATE\n   877\t\n   878\t``CREATE_USER``\n   879\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   880\t\n   881\tDefault: ``True``\n   882\t\n   883\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   884\t\n   885\tIf it is set to ``False``, the test user won't be automatically created at the\n   886\tbeginning of the tests and dropped at the end.\n   887\t\n   888\t.. setting:: TEST_USER\n   889\t\n   890\t``USER``\n   891\t^^^^^^^^\n   892\t\n   893\tDefault: ``None``\n   894\t\n   895\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   896\t\n   897\tThe username to use when connecting to the Oracle database that will be used\n   898\twhen running tests. If not provided, Django will use ``'test_' + USER``.\n   899\t\n   900\t.. setting:: TEST_PASSWD\n   901\t\n   902\t``PASSWORD``\n   903\t^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   904\t\n   905\tDefault: ``None``\n   906\t\n   907\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   908\t\n   909\tThe password to use when connecting to the Oracle database that will be used\n   910\twhen running tests. If not provided, Django will generate a random password.\n   911\t\n   912\t.. setting:: TEST_ORACLE_MANAGED_FILES\n   913\t\n   914\t``ORACLE_MANAGED_FILES``\n   915\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   916\t\n   917\tDefault: ``False``\n   918\t\n   919\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   920\t\n   921\tIf set to ``True``, Oracle Managed Files (OMF) tablespaces will be used.\n   922\t:setting:`DATAFILE` and :setting:`DATAFILE_TMP` will be ignored.\n   923\t\n   924\t.. setting:: TEST_TBLSPACE\n   925\t\n   926\t``TBLSPACE``\n   927\t^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   928\t\n   929\tDefault: ``None``\n   930\t\n   931\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   932\t\n   933\tThe name of the tablespace that will be used when running tests. If not\n   934\tprovided, Django will use ``'test_' + USER``.\n   935\t\n   936\t.. setting:: TEST_TBLSPACE_TMP\n   937\t\n   938\t``TBLSPACE_TMP``\n   939\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   940\t\n   941\tDefault: ``None``\n   942\t\n   943\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   944\t\n   945\tThe name of the temporary tablespace that will be used when running tests. If\n   946\tnot provided, Django will use ``'test_' + USER + '_temp'``.\n   947\t\n   948\t.. setting:: DATAFILE\n   949\t\n   950\t``DATAFILE``\n   951\t^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   952\t\n   953\tDefault: ``None``\n   954\t\n   955\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   956\t\n   957\tThe name of the datafile to use for the TBLSPACE. If not provided, Django will\n   958\tuse ``TBLSPACE + '.dbf'``.\n   959\t\n   960\t.. setting:: DATAFILE_TMP\n   961\t\n   962\t``DATAFILE_TMP``\n   963\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   964\t\n   965\tDefault: ``None``\n   966\t\n   967\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   968\t\n   969\tThe name of the datafile to use for the TBLSPACE_TMP. If not provided, Django\n   970\twill use ``TBLSPACE_TMP + '.dbf'``.\n   971\t\n   972\t.. setting:: DATAFILE_MAXSIZE\n   973\t\n   974\t``DATAFILE_MAXSIZE``\n   975\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   976\t\n   977\tDefault: ``'500M'``\n   978\t\n   979\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   980\t\n   981\tThe maximum size that the DATAFILE is allowed to grow to.\n   982\t\n   983\t.. setting:: DATAFILE_TMP_MAXSIZE\n   984\t\n   985\t``DATAFILE_TMP_MAXSIZE``\n   986\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   987\t\n   988\tDefault: ``'500M'``\n   989\t\n   990\tThis is an Oracle-specific setting.\n   991\t\n   992\tThe maximum size that the DATAFILE_TMP is allowed to grow to.\n   993\t\n   994\t.. setting:: DATAFILE_SIZE\n   995\t\n   996\t``DATAFILE_SIZE``\n   997\t^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\n   998\t\n   999\tDefault: ``'50M'``\n  1000\t\n","message":"1000 lines read from file starting from line 1. Total lines in file: 3705.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolCallPart","payload":{"arguments_part":" read"}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolCallPart","payload":{"arguments_part":" the"}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolCallPart","payload":{"arguments_part":" current"}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolCallPart","payload":{"arguments_part":" test"}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolCallPart","payload":{"arguments_part":" files"}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolCallPart","payload":{"arguments_part":","}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:3","agent_id":"ab68fbeca","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolCall","payload":{"type":"function","id":"functions.ReadFile:4","function":{"name":"ReadFile","arguments":""},"extras":null}}}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:3","agent_id":"ab68fbeca","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.ReadFile:3","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"     1\t==========\n     2\tTime zones\n     3\t==========\n     4\t\n     5\t.. _time-zones-overview:\n     6\t\n     7\tOverview\n     8\t========\n     9\t\n    10\tWhen support for time zones is enabled, Django stores datetime information in\n    11\tUTC in the database, uses time-zone-aware datetime objects internally, and\n    12\ttranslates them to the end user's time zone in templates and forms.\n    13\t\n    14\tThis is handy if your users live in more than one time zone and you want to\n    15\tdisplay datetime information according to each user's wall clock.\n    16\t\n    17\tEven if your website is available in only one time zone, it's still good\n    18\tpractice to store data in UTC in your database. The main reason is daylight\n    19\tsaving time (DST). Many countries have a system of DST, where clocks are moved\n    20\tforward in spring and backward in autumn. If you're working in local time,\n    21\tyou're likely to encounter errors twice a year, when the transitions happen.\n    22\t(The pytz_ documentation discusses `these issues`_ in greater detail.) This\n    23\tprobably doesn't matter for your blog, but it's a problem if you over-bill or\n    24\tunder-bill your customers by one hour, twice a year, every year. The solution\n    25\tto this problem is to use UTC in the code and use local time only when\n    26\tinteracting with end users.\n    27\t\n    28\tTime zone support is disabled by default. To enable it, set :setting:`USE_TZ =\n    29\tTrue <USE_TZ>` in your settings file.\n    30\t\n    31\t.. note::\n    32\t\n    33\t    In Django 5.0, time zone support will be enabled by default.\n    34\t\n    35\tBy default, time zone support uses pytz_, which is installed when you install\n    36\tDjango; Django also supports the use of other time zone implementations like\n    37\t:mod:`zoneinfo` by passing :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` objects directly to\n    38\tfunctions in :mod:`django.utils.timezone`.\n    39\t\n    40\t.. versionchanged:: 3.2\n    41\t\n    42\t    Support for non-``pytz`` timezone implementations was added.\n    43\t\n    44\t.. note::\n    45\t\n    46\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n    47\t    startproject <startproject>` includes :setting:`USE_TZ = True <USE_TZ>`\n    48\t    for convenience.\n    49\t\n    50\tIf you're wrestling with a particular problem, start with the :ref:`time zone\n    51\tFAQ <time-zones-faq>`.\n    52\t\n    53\tConcepts\n    54\t========\n    55\t\n    56\t.. _naive_vs_aware_datetimes:\n    57\t\n    58\tNaive and aware datetime objects\n    59\t--------------------------------\n    60\t\n    61\tPython's :class:`datetime.datetime` objects have a ``tzinfo`` attribute that\n    62\tcan be used to store time zone information, represented as an instance of a\n    63\tsubclass of :class:`datetime.tzinfo`. When this attribute is set and describes\n    64\tan offset, a datetime object is **aware**. Otherwise, it's **naive**.\n    65\t\n    66\tYou can use :func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_aware` and\n    67\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_naive` to determine whether datetimes are\n    68\taware or naive.\n    69\t\n    70\tWhen time zone support is disabled, Django uses naive datetime objects in local\n    71\ttime. This is sufficient for many use cases. In this mode, to obtain the\n    72\tcurrent time, you would write::\n    73\t\n    74\t    import datetime\n    75\t\n    76\t    now = datetime.datetime.now()\n    77\t\n    78\tWhen time zone support is enabled (:setting:`USE_TZ=True <USE_TZ>`), Django uses\n    79\ttime-zone-aware datetime objects. If your code creates datetime objects, they\n    80\tshould be aware too. In this mode, the example above becomes::\n    81\t\n    82\t    from django.utils import timezone\n    83\t\n    84\t    now = timezone.now()\n    85\t\n    86\t.. warning::\n    87\t\n    88\t    Dealing with aware datetime objects isn't always intuitive. For instance,\n    89\t    the ``tzinfo`` argument of the standard datetime constructor doesn't work\n    90\t    reliably for time zones with DST. Using UTC is generally safe; if you're\n    91\t    using other time zones, you should review the `pytz`_ documentation\n    92\t    carefully.\n    93\t\n    94\t.. note::\n    95\t\n    96\t    Python's :class:`datetime.time` objects also feature a ``tzinfo``\n    97\t    attribute, and PostgreSQL has a matching ``time with time zone`` type.\n    98\t    However, as PostgreSQL's docs put it, this type \"exhibits properties which\n    99\t    lead to questionable usefulness\".\n   100\t\n   101\t    Django only supports naive time objects and will raise an exception if you\n   102\t    attempt to save an aware time object, as a timezone for a time with no\n   103\t    associated date does not make sense.\n   104\t\n   105\t.. _naive-datetime-objects:\n   106\t\n   107\tInterpretation of naive datetime objects\n   108\t----------------------------------------\n   109\t\n   110\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, Django still accepts naive datetime\n   111\tobjects, in order to preserve backwards-compatibility. When the database layer\n   112\treceives one, it attempts to make it aware by interpreting it in the\n   113\t:ref:`default time zone <default-current-time-zone>` and raises a warning.\n   114\t\n   115\tUnfortunately, during DST transitions, some datetimes don't exist or are\n   116\tambiguous. In such situations, pytz_ raises an exception. That's why you should\n   117\talways create aware datetime objects when time zone support is enabled.\n   118\t\n   119\tIn practice, this is rarely an issue. Django gives you aware datetime objects\n   120\tin the models and forms, and most often, new datetime objects are created from\n   121\texisting ones through :class:`~datetime.timedelta` arithmetic. The only\n   122\tdatetime that's often created in application code is the current time, and\n   123\t:func:`timezone.now() <django.utils.timezone.now>` automatically does the\n   124\tright thing.\n   125\t\n   126\t.. _default-current-time-zone:\n   127\t\n   128\tDefault time zone and current time zone\n   129\t---------------------------------------\n   130\t\n   131\tThe **default time zone** is the time zone defined by the :setting:`TIME_ZONE`\n   132\tsetting.\n   133\t\n   134\tThe **current time zone** is the time zone that's used for rendering.\n   135\t\n   136\tYou should set the current time zone to the end user's actual time zone with\n   137\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.activate`. Otherwise, the default time zone is\n   138\tused.\n   139\t\n   140\t.. note::\n   141\t\n   142\t    As explained in the documentation of :setting:`TIME_ZONE`, Django sets\n   143\t    environment variables so that its process runs in the default time zone.\n   144\t    This happens regardless of the value of :setting:`USE_TZ` and of the\n   145\t    current time zone.\n   146\t\n   147\t    When :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, this is useful to preserve\n   148\t    backwards-compatibility with applications that still rely on local time.\n   149\t    However, :ref:`as explained above <naive-datetime-objects>`, this isn't\n   150\t    entirely reliable, and you should always work with aware datetimes in UTC\n   151\t    in your own code. For instance, use :meth:`~datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp`\n   152\t    and set the ``tz`` parameter to :data:`~django.utils.timezone.utc`.\n   153\t\n   154\tSelecting the current time zone\n   155\t-------------------------------\n   156\t\n   157\tThe current time zone is the equivalent of the current :term:`locale <locale\n   158\tname>` for translations. However, there's no equivalent of the\n   159\t``Accept-Language`` HTTP header that Django could use to determine the user's\n   160\ttime zone automatically. Instead, Django provides :ref:`time zone selection\n   161\tfunctions <time-zone-selection-functions>`. Use them to build the time zone\n   162\tselection logic that makes sense for you.\n   163\t\n   164\tMost websites that care about time zones ask users in which time zone they live\n   165\tand store this information in the user's profile. For anonymous users, they use\n   166\tthe time zone of their primary audience or UTC. pytz_ provides helpers_, like a\n   167\tlist of time zones per country, that you can use to pre-select the most likely\n   168\tchoices.\n   169\t\n   170\tHere's an example that stores the current timezone in the session. (It skips\n   171\terror handling entirely for the sake of simplicity.)\n   172\t\n   173\tAdd the following middleware to :setting:`MIDDLEWARE`::\n   174\t\n   175\t    import pytz\n   176\t\n   177\t    from django.utils import timezone\n   178\t\n   179\t    class TimezoneMiddleware:\n   180\t        def __init__(self, get_response):\n   181\t            self.get_response = get_response\n   182\t\n   183\t        def __call__(self, request):\n   184\t            tzname = request.session.get('django_timezone')\n   185\t            if tzname:\n   186\t                timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(tzname))\n   187\t            else:\n   188\t                timezone.deactivate()\n   189\t            return self.get_response(request)\n   190\t\n   191\tCreate a view that can set the current timezone::\n   192\t\n   193\t    from django.shortcuts import redirect, render\n   194\t\n   195\t    def set_timezone(request):\n   196\t        if request.method == 'POST':\n   197\t            request.session['django_timezone'] = request.POST['timezone']\n   198\t            return redirect('/')\n   199\t        else:\n   200\t            return render(request, 'template.html', {'timezones': pytz.common_timezones})\n   201\t\n   202\tInclude a form in ``template.html`` that will ``POST`` to this view:\n   203\t\n   204\t.. code-block:: html+django\n   205\t\n   206\t    {% load tz %}\n   207\t    {% get_current_timezone as TIME_ZONE %}\n   208\t    <form action=\"{% url 'set_timezone' %}\" method=\"POST\">\n   209\t        {% csrf_token %}\n   210\t        <label for=\"timezone\">Time zone:</label>\n   211\t        <select name=\"timezone\">\n   212\t            {% for tz in timezones %}\n   213\t            <option value=\"{{ tz }}\"{% if tz == TIME_ZONE %} selected{% endif %}>{{ tz }}</option>\n   214\t            {% endfor %}\n   215\t        </select>\n   216\t        <input type=\"submit\" value=\"Set\">\n   217\t    </form>\n   218\t\n   219\t.. _time-zones-in-forms:\n   220\t\n   221\tTime zone aware input in forms\n   222\t==============================\n   223\t\n   224\tWhen you enable time zone support, Django interprets datetimes entered in\n   225\tforms in the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` and returns\n   226\taware datetime objects in ``cleaned_data``.\n   227\t\n   228\tIf the current time zone raises an exception for datetimes that don't exist or\n   229\tare ambiguous because they fall in a DST transition (the timezones provided by\n   230\tpytz_ do this), such datetimes will be reported as invalid values.\n   231\t\n   232\t.. _time-zones-in-templates:\n   233\t\n   234\tTime zone aware output in templates\n   235\t===================================\n   236\t\n   237\tWhen you enable time zone support, Django converts aware datetime objects to\n   238\tthe :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` when they're rendered\n   239\tin templates. This behaves very much like :doc:`format localization\n   240\t</topics/i18n/formatting>`.\n   241\t\n   242\t.. warning::\n   243\t\n   244\t    Django doesn't convert naive datetime objects, because they could be\n   245\t    ambiguous, and because your code should never produce naive datetimes when\n   246\t    time zone support is enabled. However, you can force conversion with the\n   247\t    template filters described below.\n   248\t\n   249\tConversion to local time isn't always appropriate -- you may be generating\n   250\toutput for computers rather than for humans. The following filters and tags,\n   251\tprovided by the ``tz`` template tag library, allow you to control the time zone\n   252\tconversions.\n   253\t\n   254\t.. highlight:: html+django\n   255\t\n   256\tTemplate tags\n   257\t-------------\n   258\t\n   259\t.. templatetag:: localtime\n   260\t\n   261\t``localtime``\n   262\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   263\t\n   264\tEnables or disables conversion of aware datetime objects to the current time\n   265\tzone in the contained block.\n   266\t\n   267\tThis tag has exactly the same effects as the :setting:`USE_TZ` setting as far\n   268\tas the template engine is concerned. It allows a more fine grained control of\n   269\tconversion.\n   270\t\n   271\tTo activate or deactivate conversion for a template block, use::\n   272\t\n   273\t    {% load tz %}\n   274\t\n   275\t    {% localtime on %}\n   276\t        {{ value }}\n   277\t    {% endlocaltime %}\n   278\t\n   279\t    {% localtime off %}\n   280\t        {{ value }}\n   281\t    {% endlocaltime %}\n   282\t\n   283\t.. note::\n   284\t\n   285\t    The value of :setting:`USE_TZ` isn't respected inside of a\n   286\t    ``{% localtime %}`` block.\n   287\t\n   288\t.. templatetag:: timezone\n   289\t\n   290\t``timezone``\n   291\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   292\t\n   293\tSets or unsets the current time zone in the contained block. When the current\n   294\ttime zone is unset, the default time zone applies.\n   295\t\n   296\t::\n   297\t\n   298\t    {% load tz %}\n   299\t\n   300\t    {% timezone \"Europe/Paris\" %}\n   301\t        Paris time: {{ value }}\n   302\t    {% endtimezone %}\n   303\t\n   304\t    {% timezone None %}\n   305\t        Server time: {{ value }}\n   306\t    {% endtimezone %}\n   307\t\n   308\t.. templatetag:: get_current_timezone\n   309\t\n   310\t``get_current_timezone``\n   311\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   312\t\n   313\tYou can get the name of the current time zone using the\n   314\t``get_current_timezone`` tag::\n   315\t\n   316\t    {% get_current_timezone as TIME_ZONE %}\n   317\t\n   318\tAlternatively, you can activate the\n   319\t:func:`~django.template.context_processors.tz` context processor and\n   320\tuse the ``TIME_ZONE`` context variable.\n   321\t\n   322\tTemplate filters\n   323\t----------------\n   324\t\n   325\tThese filters accept both aware and naive datetimes. For conversion purposes,\n   326\tthey assume that naive datetimes are in the default time zone. They always\n   327\treturn aware datetimes.\n   328\t\n   329\t.. templatefilter:: localtime\n   330\t\n   331\t``localtime``\n   332\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   333\t\n   334\tForces conversion of a single value to the current time zone.\n   335\t\n   336\tFor example::\n   337\t\n   338\t    {% load tz %}\n   339\t\n   340\t    {{ value|localtime }}\n   341\t\n   342\t.. templatefilter:: utc\n   343\t\n   344\t``utc``\n   345\t~~~~~~~\n   346\t\n   347\tForces conversion of a single value to UTC.\n   348\t\n   349\tFor example::\n   350\t\n   351\t    {% load tz %}\n   352\t\n   353\t    {{ value|utc }}\n   354\t\n   355\t.. templatefilter:: timezone\n   356\t\n   357\t``timezone``\n   358\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   359\t\n   360\tForces conversion of a single value to an arbitrary timezone.\n   361\t\n   362\tThe argument must be an instance of a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` subclass or a\n   363\ttime zone name.\n   364\t\n   365\tFor example::\n   366\t\n   367\t    {% load tz %}\n   368\t\n   369\t    {{ value|timezone:\"Europe/Paris\" }}\n   370\t\n   371\t.. highlight:: python\n   372\t\n   373\t.. _time-zones-migration-guide:\n   374\t\n   375\tMigration guide\n   376\t===============\n   377\t\n   378\tHere's how to migrate a project that was started before Django supported time\n   379\tzones.\n   380\t\n   381\tDatabase\n   382\t--------\n   383\t\n   384\tPostgreSQL\n   385\t~~~~~~~~~~\n   386\t\n   387\tThe PostgreSQL backend stores datetimes as ``timestamp with time zone``. In\n   388\tpractice, this means it converts datetimes from the connection's time zone to\n   389\tUTC on storage, and from UTC to the connection's time zone on retrieval.\n   390\t\n   391\tAs a consequence, if you're using PostgreSQL, you can switch between ``USE_TZ\n   392\t= False`` and ``USE_TZ = True`` freely. The database connection's time zone\n   393\twill be set to :setting:`TIME_ZONE` or ``UTC`` respectively, so that Django\n   394\tobtains correct datetimes in all cases. You don't need to perform any data\n   395\tconversions.\n   396\t\n   397\tOther databases\n   398\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   399\t\n   400\tOther backends store datetimes without time zone information. If you switch\n   401\tfrom ``USE_TZ = False`` to ``USE_TZ = True``, you must convert your data from\n   402\tlocal time to UTC -- which isn't deterministic if your local time has DST.\n   403\t\n   404\tCode\n   405\t----\n   406\t\n   407\tThe first step is to add :setting:`USE_TZ = True <USE_TZ>` to your settings\n   408\tfile. At this point, things should mostly work. If you create naive datetime\n   409\tobjects in your code, Django makes them aware when necessary.\n   410\t\n   411\tHowever, these conversions may fail around DST transitions, which means you\n   412\taren't getting the full benefits of time zone support yet. Also, you're likely\n   413\tto run into a few problems because it's impossible to compare a naive datetime\n   414\twith an aware datetime. Since Django now gives you aware datetimes, you'll get\n   415\texceptions wherever you compare a datetime that comes from a model or a form\n   416\twith a naive datetime that you've created in your code.\n   417\t\n   418\tSo the second step is to refactor your code wherever you instantiate datetime\n   419\tobjects to make them aware. This can be done incrementally.\n   420\t:mod:`django.utils.timezone` defines some handy helpers for compatibility\n   421\tcode: :func:`~django.utils.timezone.now`,\n   422\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_aware`,\n   423\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_naive`,\n   424\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.make_aware`, and\n   425\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.make_naive`.\n   426\t\n   427\tFinally, in order to help you locate code that needs upgrading, Django raises\n   428\ta warning when you attempt to save a naive datetime to the database::\n   429\t\n   430\t    RuntimeWarning: DateTimeField ModelName.field_name received a naive\n   431\t    datetime (2012-01-01 00:00:00) while time zone support is active.\n   432\t\n   433\tDuring development, you can turn such warnings into exceptions and get a\n   434\ttraceback by adding the following to your settings file::\n   435\t\n   436\t    import warnings\n   437\t    warnings.filterwarnings(\n   438\t        'error', r\"DateTimeField .* received a naive datetime\",\n   439\t        RuntimeWarning, r'django\\.db\\.models\\.fields',\n   440\t    )\n   441\t\n   442\tFixtures\n   443\t--------\n   444\t\n   445\tWhen serializing an aware datetime, the UTC offset is included, like this::\n   446\t\n   447\t    \"2011-09-01T13:20:30+03:00\"\n   448\t\n   449\tWhile for a naive datetime, it isn't::\n   450\t\n   451\t    \"2011-09-01T13:20:30\"\n   452\t\n   453\tFor models with :class:`~django.db.models.DateTimeField`\\ s, this difference\n   454\tmakes it impossible to write a fixture that works both with and without time\n   455\tzone support.\n   456\t\n   457\tFixtures generated with ``USE_TZ = False``, or before Django 1.4, use the\n   458\t\"naive\" format. If your project contains such fixtures, after you enable time\n   459\tzone support, you'll see :exc:`RuntimeWarning`\\ s when you load them. To get\n   460\trid of the warnings, you must convert your fixtures to the \"aware\" format.\n   461\t\n   462\tYou can regenerate fixtures with :djadmin:`loaddata` then :djadmin:`dumpdata`.\n   463\tOr, if they're small enough, you can edit them to add the UTC offset that\n   464\tmatches your :setting:`TIME_ZONE` to each serialized datetime.\n   465\t\n   466\t.. _time-zones-faq:\n   467\t\n   468\tFAQ\n   469\t===\n   470\t\n   471\tSetup\n   472\t-----\n   473\t\n   474\t#. **I don't need multiple time zones. Should I enable time zone support?**\n   475\t\n   476\t   Yes. When time zone support is enabled, Django uses a more accurate model\n   477\t   of local time. This shields you from subtle and unreproducible bugs around\n   478\t   daylight saving time (DST) transitions.\n   479\t\n   480\t   When you enable time zone support, you'll encounter some errors because\n   481\t   you're using naive datetimes where Django expects aware datetimes. Such\n   482\t   errors show up when running tests. You'll quickly learn how to avoid invalid\n   483\t   operations.\n   484\t\n   485\t   On the other hand, bugs caused by the lack of time zone support are much\n   486\t   harder to prevent, diagnose and fix. Anything that involves scheduled tasks\n   487\t   or datetime arithmetic is a candidate for subtle bugs that will bite you\n   488\t   only once or twice a year.\n   489\t\n   490\t   For these reasons, time zone support is enabled by default in new projects,\n   491\t   and you should keep it unless you have a very good reason not to.\n   492\t\n   493\t#. **I've enabled time zone support. Am I safe?**\n   494\t\n   495\t   Maybe. You're better protected from DST-related bugs, but you can still\n   496\t   shoot yourself in the foot by carelessly turning naive datetimes into aware\n   497\t   datetimes, and vice-versa.\n   498\t\n   499\t   If your application connects to other systems -- for instance, if it queries\n   500\t   a web service -- make sure datetimes are properly specified. To transmit\n   501\t   datetimes safely, their representation should include the UTC offset, or\n   502\t   their values should be in UTC (or both!).\n   503\t\n   504\t   Finally, our calendar system contains interesting edge cases. For example,\n   505\t   you can't always subtract one year directly from a given date::\n   506\t\n   507\t       >>> import datetime\n   508\t       >>> def one_year_before(value):  # Wrong example.\n   509\t       ...     return value.replace(year=value.year - 1)\n   510\t       >>> one_year_before(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 1, 10, 0))\n   511\t       datetime.datetime(2011, 3, 1, 10, 0)\n   512\t       >>> one_year_before(datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 29, 10, 0))\n   513\t       Traceback (most recent call last):\n   514\t       ...\n   515\t       ValueError: day is out of range for month\n   516\t\n   517\t   To implement such a function correctly, you must decide whether 2012-02-29\n   518\t   minus one year is 2011-02-28 or 2011-03-01, which depends on your business\n   519\t   requirements.\n   520\t\n   521\t#. **How do I interact with a database that stores datetimes in local time?**\n   522\t\n   523\t   Set the :setting:`TIME_ZONE <DATABASE-TIME_ZONE>` option to the appropriate\n   524\t   time zone for this database in the :setting:`DATABASES` setting.\n   525\t\n   526\t   This is useful for connecting to a database that doesn't support time zones\n   527\t   and that isn't managed by Django when :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``.\n   528\t\n   529\tTroubleshooting\n   530\t---------------\n   531\t\n   532\t#. **My application crashes with** ``TypeError: can't compare offset-naive``\n   533\t   ``and offset-aware datetimes`` **-- what's wrong?**\n   534\t\n   535\t   Let's reproduce this error by comparing a naive and an aware datetime::\n   536\t\n   537\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   538\t       >>> aware = timezone.now()\n   539\t       >>> naive = timezone.make_naive(aware)\n   540\t       >>> naive == aware\n   541\t       Traceback (most recent call last):\n   542\t       ...\n   543\t       TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes\n   544\t\n   545\t   If you encounter this error, most likely your code is comparing these two\n   546\t   things:\n   547\t\n   548\t   - a datetime provided by Django -- for instance, a value read from a form or\n   549\t     a model field. Since you enabled time zone support, it's aware.\n   550\t   - a datetime generated by your code, which is naive (or you wouldn't be\n   551\t     reading this).\n   552\t\n   553\t   Generally, the correct solution is to change your code to use an aware\n   554\t   datetime instead.\n   555\t\n   556\t   If you're writing a pluggable application that's expected to work\n   557\t   independently of the value of :setting:`USE_TZ`, you may find\n   558\t   :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` useful. This function returns the current\n   559\t   date and time as a naive datetime when ``USE_TZ = False`` and as an aware\n   560\t   datetime when ``USE_TZ = True``. You can add or subtract\n   561\t   :class:`datetime.timedelta` as needed.\n   562\t\n   563\t#. **I see lots of** ``RuntimeWarning: DateTimeField received a naive\n   564\t   datetime`` ``(YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS)`` ``while time zone support is active``\n   565\t   **-- is that bad?**\n   566\t\n   567\t   When time zone support is enabled, the database layer expects to receive\n   568\t   only aware datetimes from your code. This warning occurs when it receives a\n   569\t   naive datetime. This indicates that you haven't finished porting your code\n   570\t   for time zone support. Please refer to the :ref:`migration guide\n   571\t   <time-zones-migration-guide>` for tips on this process.\n   572\t\n   573\t   In the meantime, for backwards compatibility, the datetime is considered to\n   574\t   be in the default time zone, which is generally what you expect.\n   575\t\n   576\t#. ``now.date()`` **is yesterday! (or tomorrow)**\n   577\t\n   578\t   If you've always used naive datetimes, you probably believe that you can\n   579\t   convert a datetime to a date by calling its :meth:`~datetime.datetime.date`\n   580\t   method. You also consider that a :class:`~datetime.date` is a lot like a\n   581\t   :class:`~datetime.datetime`, except that it's less accurate.\n   582\t\n   583\t   None of this is true in a time zone aware environment::\n   584\t\n   585\t       >>> import datetime\n   586\t       >>> import pytz\n   587\t       >>> paris_tz = pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Paris\")\n   588\t       >>> new_york_tz = pytz.timezone(\"America/New_York\")\n   589\t       >>> paris = paris_tz.localize(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30))\n   590\t       # This is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   591\t       >>> new_york = new_york_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(new_york_tz))\n   592\t       >>> paris == new_york, paris.date() == new_york.date()\n   593\t       (True, False)\n   594\t       >>> paris - new_york, paris.date() - new_york.date()\n   595\t       (datetime.timedelta(0), datetime.timedelta(1))\n   596\t       >>> paris\n   597\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   598\t       >>> new_york\n   599\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 2, 19, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/New_York' EST-1 day, 19:00:00 STD>)\n   600\t\n   601\t   As this example shows, the same datetime has a different date, depending on\n   602\t   the time zone in which it is represented. But the real problem is more\n   603\t   fundamental.\n   604\t\n   605\t   A datetime represents a **point in time**. It's absolute: it doesn't depend\n   606\t   on anything. On the contrary, a date is a **calendaring concept**. It's a\n   607\t   period of time whose bounds depend on the time zone in which the date is\n   608\t   considered. As you can see, these two concepts are fundamentally different,\n   609\t   and converting a datetime to a date isn't a deterministic operation.\n   610\t\n   611\t   What does this mean in practice?\n   612\t\n   613\t   Generally, you should avoid converting a :class:`~datetime.datetime` to\n   614\t   :class:`~datetime.date`. For instance, you can use the :tfilter:`date`\n   615\t   template filter to only show the date part of a datetime. This filter will\n   616\t   convert the datetime into the current time zone before formatting it,\n   617\t   ensuring the results appear correctly.\n   618\t\n   619\t   If you really need to do the conversion yourself, you must ensure the\n   620\t   datetime is converted to the appropriate time zone first. Usually, this\n   621\t   will be the current timezone::\n   622\t\n   623\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   624\t       >>> timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(\"Asia/Singapore\"))\n   625\t       # For this example, we set the time zone to Singapore, but here's how\n   626\t       # you would obtain the current time zone in the general case.\n   627\t       >>> current_tz = timezone.get_current_timezone()\n   628\t       # Again, this is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   629\t       >>> local = current_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(current_tz))\n   630\t       >>> local\n   631\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 8, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Asia/Singapore' SGT+8:00:00 STD>)\n   632\t       >>> local.date()\n   633\t       datetime.date(2012, 3, 3)\n   634\t\n   635\t#. **I get an error** \"``Are time zone definitions for your database\n   636\t   installed?``\"\n   637\t\n   638\t   If you are using MySQL, see the :ref:`mysql-time-zone-definitions` section\n   639\t   of the MySQL notes for instructions on loading time zone definitions.\n   640\t\n   641\tUsage\n   642\t-----\n   643\t\n   644\t#. **I have a string** ``\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\"`` **and I know it's in the**\n   645\t   ``\"Europe/Helsinki\"`` **time zone. How do I turn that into an aware\n   646\t   datetime?**\n   647\t\n   648\t   This is exactly what pytz_ is for.\n   649\t\n   650\t       >>> from django.utils.dateparse import parse_datetime\n   651\t       >>> naive = parse_datetime(\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\")\n   652\t       >>> import pytz\n   653\t       >>> pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Helsinki\").localize(naive, is_dst=None)\n   654\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 21, 10, 28, 45, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Helsinki' EET+2:00:00 STD>)\n   655\t\n   656\t   Note that ``localize`` is a pytz extension to the :class:`~datetime.tzinfo`\n   657\t   API. Also, you may want to catch ``pytz.InvalidTimeError``. The\n   658\t   documentation of pytz contains `more examples`_. You should review it\n   659\t   before attempting to manipulate aware datetimes.\n   660\t\n   661\t#. **How can I obtain the local time in the current time zone?**\n   662\t\n   663\t   Well, the first question is, do you really need to?\n   664\t\n   665\t   You should only use local time when you're interacting with humans, and the\n   666\t   template layer provides :ref:`filters and tags <time-zones-in-templates>`\n   667\t   to convert datetimes to the time zone of your choice.\n   668\t\n   669\t   Furthermore, Python knows how to compare aware datetimes, taking into\n   670\t   account UTC offsets when necessary. It's much easier (and possibly faster)\n   671\t   to write all your model and view code in UTC. So, in most circumstances,\n   672\t   the datetime in UTC returned by :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` will be\n   673\t   sufficient.\n   674\t\n   675\t   For the sake of completeness, though, if you really want the local time\n   676\t   in the current time zone, here's how you can obtain it::\n   677\t\n   678\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   679\t       >>> timezone.localtime(timezone.now())\n   680\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 20, 10, 53, 873365, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   681\t\n   682\t   In this example, the current time zone is ``\"Europe/Paris\"``.\n   683\t\n   684\t#. **How can I see all available time zones?**\n   685\t\n   686\t   pytz_ provides helpers_, including a list of current time zones and a list\n   687\t   of all available time zones -- some of which are only of historical\n   688\t   interest. :mod:`zoneinfo` also provides similar functionality via\n   689\t   :func:`zoneinfo.available_timezones`.\n   690\t\n   691\t.. _pytz: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/\n   692\t.. _more examples: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#example-usage\n   693\t.. _these issues: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#problems-with-localtime\n   694\t.. _helpers: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#helpers\n   695\t.. _tz database: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database\n","message":"695 lines read from file starting from line 1.
