Most of the\n     9\tmodules in ``django.utils`` are designed for internal use and only the\n    10\tfollowing parts can be considered stable and thus backwards compatible as per\n    11\tthe :ref:`internal release deprecation policy <internal-release-deprecation-policy>`.\n    12\t\n    13\t``django.utils.cache``\n    14\t======================\n    15\t\n    16\t.. module:: django.utils.cache\n    17\t   :synopsis: Helper functions for controlling caching.\n    18\t\n    19\tThis module contains helper functions for controlling HTTP caching. It does so\n    20\tby managing the ``Vary`` header of responses. It includes functions to patch\n    21\tthe header of response objects directly and decorators that change functions to\n    22\tdo that header-patching themselves.\n    23\t\n    24\tFor information on the ``Vary`` header, see :rfc:`7231#section-7.1.4`.\n    25\t\n    26\tEssentially, the ``Vary`` HTTP header defines which headers a cache should take\n    27\tinto account when building its cache key. Requests with the same path but\n    28\tdifferent header content for headers named in ``Vary`` need to get different\n    29\tcache keys to prevent delivery of wrong content.\n    30\t\n    31\tFor example, :doc:`internationalization </topics/i18n/index>` middleware would\n    32\tneed to distinguish caches by the ``Accept-language`` header.\n    33\t\n    34\t.. function:: patch_cache_control(response, **kwargs)\n    35\t\n    36\t    This function patches the ``Cache-Control`` header by adding all keyword\n    37\t    arguments to it. The transformation is as follows:\n    38\t\n    39\t    * All keyword parameter names are turned to lowercase, and underscores\n    40\t      are converted to hyphens.\n    41\t    * If the value of a parameter is ``True`` (exactly ``True``, not just a\n    42\t      true value), only the parameter name is added to the header.\n    43\t    * All other parameters are added with their value, after applying\n    44\t      ``str()`` to it.\n    45\t\n    46\t.. function:: get_max_age(response)\n    47\t\n    48\t    Returns the max-age from the response Cache-Control header as an integer\n    49\t    (or ``None`` if it wasn't found or wasn't an integer).\n    50\t\n    51\t.. function:: patch_response_headers(response, cache_timeout=None)\n    52\t\n    53\t    Adds some useful headers to the given ``HttpResponse`` object:\n    54\t\n    55\t    * ``Expires``\n    56\t    * ``Cache-Control``\n    57\t\n    58\t    Each header is only added if it isn't already set.\n    59\t\n    60\t    ``cache_timeout`` is in seconds. The :setting:`CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_SECONDS`\n    61\t    setting is used by default.\n    62\t\n    63\t.. function:: add_never_cache_headers(response)\n    64\t\n    65\t    Adds a ``Cache-Control: max-age=0, no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate,\n    66\t    private`` header to a response to indicate that a page should never be\n    67\t    cached.\n    68\t\n    69\t.. function:: patch_vary_headers(response, newheaders)\n    70\t\n    71\t    Adds (or updates) the ``Vary`` header in the given ``HttpResponse`` object.\n    72\t    ``newheaders`` is a list of header names that should be in ``Vary``. If\n    73\t    headers contains an asterisk, then ``Vary`` header will consist of a single\n    74\t    asterisk ``'*'``, according to :rfc:`7231#section-7.1.4`. Otherwise,\n    75\t    existing headers in ``Vary`` aren't removed.\n    76\t\n    77\t.. function:: get_cache_key(request, key_prefix=None, method='GET', cache=None)\n    78\t\n    79\t    Returns a cache key based on the request path. It can be used in the\n    80\t    request phase because it pulls the list of headers to take into account\n    81\t    from the global path registry and uses those to build a cache key to\n    82\t    check against.\n    83\t\n    84\t    If there is no headerlist stored, the page needs to be rebuilt, so this\n    85\t    function returns ``None``.\n    86\t\n    87\t.. function:: learn_cache_key(request, response, cache_timeout=None, key_prefix=None, cache=None)\n    88\t\n    89\t    Learns what headers to take into account for some request path from the\n    90\t    response object. It stores those headers in a global path registry so that\n    91\t    later access to that path will know what headers to take into account\n    92\t    without building the response object itself. The headers are named in\n    93\t    the ``Vary`` header of the response, but we want to prevent response\n    94\t    generation.\n    95\t\n    96\t    The list of headers to use for cache key generation is stored in the same\n    97\t    cache as the pages themselves. If the cache ages some data out of the\n    98\t    cache, this means that we have to build the response once to get at the\n    99\t    Vary header and so at the list of headers to use for the cache key.\n   100\t\n   101\t``django.utils.dateparse``\n   102\t==========================\n   103\t\n   104\t.. module:: django.utils.dateparse\n   105\t   :synopsis: Functions to parse strings to datetime objects.\n   106\t\n   107\tThe functions defined in this module share the following properties:\n   108\t\n   109\t- They accept strings in ISO 8601 date/time formats (or some close\n   110\t  alternatives) and return objects from the corresponding classes in Python's\n   111\t  :mod:`datetime` module.\n   112\t- They raise :exc:`ValueError` if their input is well formatted but isn't a\n   113\t  valid date or time.\n   114\t- They return ``None`` if it isn't well formatted at all.\n   115\t- They accept up to picosecond resolution in input, but they truncate it to\n   116\t  microseconds, since that's what Python supports.\n   117\t\n   118\t.. function:: parse_date(value)\n   119\t\n   120\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.date`.\n   121\t\n   122\t.. function:: parse_time(value)\n   123\t\n   124\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.time`.\n   125\t\n   126\t    UTC offsets aren't supported; if ``value`` describes one, the result is\n   127\t    ``None``.\n   128\t\n   129\t.. function:: parse_datetime(value)\n   130\t\n   131\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.datetime`.\n   132\t\n   133\t    UTC offsets are supported; if ``value`` describes one, the result's\n   134\t    ``tzinfo`` attribute is a :class:`datetime.timezone` instance.\n   135\t\n   136\t.. function:: parse_duration(value)\n   137\t\n   138\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.timedelta`.\n   139\t\n   140\t    Expects data in the format ``\"DD HH:MM:SS.uuuuuu\"``,\n   141\t    ``\"DD HH:MM:SS,uuuuuu\"``,  or as specified by ISO 8601 (e.g.\n   142\t    ``P4DT1H15M20S`` which is equivalent to ``4 1:15:20``) or PostgreSQL's\n   143\t    day-time interval format (e.g. ``3 days 04:05:06``).\n   144\t\n   145\t``django.utils.decorators``\n   146\t===========================\n   147\t\n   148\t.. module:: django.utils.decorators\n   149\t    :synopsis: Functions that help with creating decorators for views.\n   150\t\n   151\t.. function:: method_decorator(decorator, name='')\n   152\t\n   153\t    Converts a function decorator into a method decorator. It can be used to\n   154\t    decorate methods or classes; in the latter case, ``name`` is the name\n   155\t    of the method to be decorated and is required.\n   156\t\n   157\t    ``decorator`` may also be a list or tuple of functions. They are wrapped\n   158\t    in reverse order so that the call order is the order in which the functions\n   159\t    appear in the list/tuple.\n   160\t\n   161\t    See :ref:`decorating class based views <decorating-class-based-views>` for\n   162\t    example usage.\n   163\t\n   164\t.. function:: decorator_from_middleware(middleware_class)\n   165\t\n   166\t    Given a middleware class, returns a view decorator. This lets you use\n   167\t    middleware functionality on a per-view basis. The middleware is created\n   168\t    with no params passed.\n   169\t\n   170\t    It assumes middleware that's compatible with the old style of Django 1.9\n   171\t    and earlier (having methods like ``process_request()``,\n   172\t    ``process_exception()``, and ``process_response()``).\n   173\t\n   174\t.. function:: decorator_from_middleware_with_args(middleware_class)\n   175\t\n   176\t    Like ``decorator_from_middleware``, but returns a function\n   177\t    that accepts the arguments to be passed to the middleware_class.\n   178\t    For example, the :func:`~django.views.decorators.cache.cache_page`\n   179\t    decorator is created from the ``CacheMiddleware`` like this::\n   180\t\n   181\t         cache_page = decorator_from_middleware_with_args(CacheMiddleware)\n   182\t\n   183\t         @cache_page(3600)\n   184\t         def my_view(request):\n   185\t             pass\n   186\t\n   187\t.. function:: sync_only_middleware(middleware)\n   188\t\n   189\t    Marks a middleware as :ref:`synchronous-only <async-middleware>`. (The\n   190\t    default in Django, but this allows you to future-proof if the default ever\n   191\t    changes in a future release.)\n   192\t\n   193\t.. function:: async_only_middleware(middleware)\n   194\t\n   195\t    Marks a middleware as :ref:`asynchronous-only <async-middleware>`. Django\n   196\t    will wrap it in an asynchronous event loop when it is called from the WSGI\n   197\t    request path.\n   198\t\n   199\t.. function:: sync_and_async_middleware(middleware)\n   200\t\n   201\t    Marks a middleware as :ref:`sync and async compatible <async-middleware>`,\n   202\t    this allows to avoid converting requests. You must implement detection of\n   203\t    the current request type to use this decorator. See :ref:`asynchronous\n   204\t    middleware documentation <async-middleware>` for details.\n   205\t\n   206\t``django.utils.encoding``\n   207\t=========================\n   208\t\n   209\t.. module:: django.utils.encoding\n   210\t   :synopsis: A series of helper functions to manage character encoding.\n   211\t\n   212\t.. function:: smart_str(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   213\t\n   214\t    Returns a ``str`` object representing arbitrary object ``s``. Treats\n   215\t    bytestrings using the ``encoding`` codec.\n   216\t\n   217\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   218\t    objects.\n   219\t\n   220\t.. function:: is_protected_type(obj)\n   221\t\n   222\t    Determine if the object instance is of a protected type.\n   223\t\n   224\t    Objects of protected types are preserved as-is when passed to\n   225\t    ``force_str(strings_only=True)``.\n   226\t\n   227\t.. function:: force_str(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   228\t\n   229\t    Similar to ``smart_str()``, except that lazy instances are resolved to\n   230\t    strings, rather than kept as lazy objects.\n   231\t\n   232\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   233\t    objects.\n   234\t\n   235\t.. function:: smart_bytes(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   236\t\n   237\t    Returns a bytestring version of arbitrary object ``s``, encoded as\n   238\t    specified in ``encoding``.\n   239\t\n   240\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   241\t    objects.\n   242\t\n   243\t.. function:: force_bytes(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   244\t\n   245\t    Similar to ``smart_bytes``, except that lazy instances are resolved to\n   246\t    bytestrings, rather than kept as lazy objects.\n   247\t\n   248\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   249\t    objects.\n   250\t\n   251\t.. function:: iri_to_uri(iri)\n   252\t\n   253\t    Convert an Internationalized Resource Identifier (IRI) portion to a URI\n   254\t    portion that is suitable for inclusion in a URL.\n   255\t\n   256\t    This is the algorithm from section 3.1 of :rfc:`3987#section-3.1`, slightly\n   257\t    simplified since the input is assumed to be a string rather than an\n   258\t    arbitrary byte stream.\n   259\t\n   260\t    Takes an IRI (string or UTF-8 bytes) and returns a string containing the\n   261\t    encoded result.\n   262\t\n   263\t.. function:: uri_to_iri(uri)\n   264\t\n   265\t    Converts a Uniform Resource Identifier into an Internationalized Resource\n   266\t    Identifier.\n   267\t\n   268\t    This is an algorithm from section 3.2 of :rfc:`3987#section-3.2`.\n   269\t\n   270\t    Takes a URI in ASCII bytes and returns a string containing the encoded\n   271\t    result.\n   272\t\n   273\t.. function:: filepath_to_uri(path)\n   274\t\n   275\t    Convert a file system path to a URI portion that is suitable for inclusion\n   276\t    in a URL. The path is assumed to be either UTF-8 bytes, string, or a\n   277\t    :class:`~pathlib.Path`.\n   278\t\n   279\t    This method will encode certain characters that would normally be\n   280\t    recognized as special characters for URIs.  Note that this method does not\n   281\t    encode the ' character, as it is a valid character within URIs. See\n   282\t    ``encodeURIComponent()`` JavaScript function for more details.\n   283\t\n   284\t    Returns an ASCII string containing the encoded result.\n   285\t\n   286\t.. function:: escape_uri_path(path)\n   287\t\n   288\t    Escapes the unsafe characters from the path portion of a Uniform Resource\n   289\t    Identifier (URI).\n   290\t\n   291\t``django.utils.feedgenerator``\n   292\t==============================\n   293\t\n   294\t.. module:: django.utils.feedgenerator\n   295\t   :synopsis: Syndication feed generation library -- used for generating RSS, etc.\n   296\t\n   297\tSample usage::\n   298\t\n   299\t    >>> from django.utils import feedgenerator\n   300\t    >>> feed = feedgenerator.Rss201rev2Feed(\n   301\t    ...     title=\"Poynter E-Media Tidbits\",\n   302\t    ...     link=\"http://www.poynter.org/column.asp?id=31\",\n   303\t    ...     description=\"A group blog by the sharpest minds in online media/journalism/publishing.\",\n   304\t    ...     language=\"en\",\n   305\t    ... )\n   306\t    >>> feed.add_item(\n   307\t    ...     title=\"Hello\",\n   308\t    ...     link=\"http://www.holovaty.com/test/\",\n   309\t    ...     description=\"Testing.\",\n   310\t    ... )\n   311\t    >>> with open('test.rss', 'w') as fp:\n   312\t    ...     feed.write(fp, 'utf-8')\n   313\t\n   314\tFor simplifying the selection of a generator use ``feedgenerator.DefaultFeed``\n   315\twhich is currently ``Rss201rev2Feed``\n   316\t\n   317\tFor definitions of the different versions of RSS, see:\n   318\thttps://web.archive.org/web/20110718035220/http://diveintomark.org/archives/2004/02/04/incompatible-rss\n   319\t\n   320\t.. function:: get_tag_uri(url, date)\n   321\t\n   322\t    Creates a TagURI.\n   323\t\n   324\t    See https://web.archive.org/web/20110514113830/http://diveintomark.org/archives/2004/05/28/howto-atom-id\n   325\t\n   326\t``SyndicationFeed``\n   327\t-------------------\n   328\t\n   329\t.. class:: SyndicationFeed\n   330\t\n   331\t    Base class for all syndication feeds. Subclasses should provide write().\n   332\t\n   333\t    .. method:: __init__(title, link, description, language=None, author_email=None, author_name=None, author_link=None, subtitle=None, categories=None, feed_url=None, feed_copyright=None, feed_guid=None, ttl=None, **kwargs)\n   334\t\n   335\t        Initialize the feed with the given dictionary of metadata, which applies\n   336\t        to the entire feed.\n   337\t\n   338\t        Any extra keyword arguments you pass to ``__init__`` will be stored in\n   339\t        ``self.feed``.\n   340\t\n   341\t        All parameters should be strings, except ``categories``, which should\n   342\t        be a sequence of strings.\n   343\t\n   344\t    .. method:: add_item(title, link, description, author_email=None, author_name=None, author_link=None, pubdate=None, comments=None, unique_id=None, categories=(), item_copyright=None, ttl=None, updateddate=None, enclosures=None, **kwargs)\n   345\t\n   346\t        Adds an item to the feed. All args are expected to be strings except\n   347\t        ``pubdate`` and ``updateddate``, which are ``datetime.datetime``\n   348\t        objects, and ``enclosures``, which is a list of ``Enclosure`` instances.\n   349\t\n   350\t    .. method:: num_items()\n   351\t\n   352\t    .. method:: root_attributes()\n   353\t\n   354\t        Return extra attributes to place on the root (i.e. feed/channel)\n   355\t        element. Called from ``write()``.\n   356\t\n   357\t    .. method:: add_root_elements(handler)\n   358\t\n   359\t        Add elements in the root (i.e. feed/channel) element.\n   360\t        Called from ``write()``.\n   361\t\n   362\t    .. method:: item_attributes(item)\n   363\t\n   364\t        Return extra attributes to place on each item (i.e. item/entry)\n   365\t        element.\n   366\t\n   367\t    .. method:: add_item_elements(handler, item)\n   368\t\n   369\t        Add elements on each item (i.e. item/entry) element.\n   370\t\n   371\t    .. method:: write(outfile, encoding)\n   372\t\n   373\t        Outputs the feed in the given encoding to ``outfile``, which is a\n   374\t        file-like object. Subclasses should override this.\n   375\t\n   376\t    .. method:: writeString(encoding)\n   377\t\n   378\t        Returns the feed in the given encoding as a string.\n   379\t\n   380\t    .. method:: latest_post_date()\n   381\t\n   382\t        Returns the latest ``pubdate`` or ``updateddate`` for all items in the\n   383\t        feed. If no items have either of these attributes this returns the\n   384\t        current UTC date/time.\n   385\t\n   386\t``Enclosure``\n   387\t-------------\n   388\t\n   389\t.. class:: Enclosure\n   390\t\n   391\t    Represents an RSS enclosure\n   392\t\n   393\t``RssFeed``\n   394\t-----------\n   395\t\n   396\t.. class:: RssFeed(SyndicationFeed)\n   397\t\n   398\t``Rss201rev2Feed``\n   399\t------------------\n   400\t\n   401\t.. class:: Rss201rev2Feed(RssFeed)\n   402\t\n   403\t    Spec: https://cyber.harvard.edu/rss/rss.html\n   404\t\n   405\t``RssUserland091Feed``\n   406\t----------------------\n   407\t\n   408\t.. class:: RssUserland091Feed(RssFeed)\n   409\t\n   410\t    Spec: http://backend.userland.com/rss091\n   411\t\n   412\t``Atom1Feed``\n   413\t-------------\n   414\t\n   415\t.. class:: Atom1Feed(SyndicationFeed)\n   416\t\n   417\t    Spec: :rfc:`4287`\n   418\t\n   419\t``django.utils.functional``\n   420\t===========================\n   421\t\n   422\t.. module:: django.utils.functional\n   423\t    :synopsis: Functional programming tools.\n   424\t\n   425\t.. class:: cached_property(func, name=None)\n   426\t\n   427\t    The ``@cached_property`` decorator caches the result of a method with a\n   428\t    single ``self`` argument as a property. The cached result will persist\n   429\t    as long as the instance does, so if the instance is passed around and the\n   430\t    function subsequently invoked, the cached result will be returned.\n   431\t\n   432\t    Consider a typical case, where a view might need to call a model's method\n   433\t    to perform some computation, before placing the model instance into the\n   434\t    context, where the template might invoke the method once more::\n   435\t\n   436\t        # the model\n   437\t        class Person(models.Model):\n   438\t\n   439\t            def friends(self):\n   440\t                # expensive computation\n   441\t                ...\n   442\t                return friends\n   443\t\n   444\t        # in the view:\n   445\t        if person.friends():\n   446\t            ...\n   447\t\n   448\t    And in the template you would have:\n   449\t\n   450\t    .. code-block:: html+django\n   451\t\n   452\t        {% for friend in person.friends %}\n   453\t\n   454\t    Here, ``friends()`` will be called twice. Since the instance ``person`` in\n   455\t    the view and the template are the same, decorating the ``friends()`` method\n   456\t    with ``@cached_property`` can avoid that::\n   457\t\n   458\t        from django.utils.functional import cached_property\n   459\t\n   460\t        class Person(models.Model):\n   461\t\n   462\t            @cached_property\n   463\t            def friends(self):\n   464\t                ...\n   465\t\n   466\t    Note that as the method is now a property, in Python code it will need to\n   467\t    be accessed appropriately::\n   468\t\n   469\t        # in the view:\n   470\t        if person.friends:\n   471\t            ...\n   472\t\n   473\t    The cached value can be treated like an ordinary attribute of the instance::\n   474\t\n   475\t        # clear it, requiring re-computation next time it's called\n   476\t        del person.friends # or delattr(person, \"friends\")\n   477\t\n   478\t        # set a value manually, that will persist on the instance until cleared\n   479\t        person.friends = [\"Huckleberry Finn\", \"Tom Sawyer\"]\n   480\t\n   481\t    Because of the way the :py:ref:`descriptor protocol\n   482\t    <descriptor-invocation>` works, using ``del`` (or ``delattr``) on a\n   483\t    ``cached_property`` that hasn't been accessed raises ``AttributeError``.\n   484\t\n   485\t    As well as offering potential performance advantages, ``@cached_property``\n   486\t    can ensure that an attribute's value does not change unexpectedly over the\n   487\t    life of an instance. This could occur with a method whose computation is\n   488\t    based on ``datetime.now()``, or if a change were saved to the database by\n   489\t    some other process in the brief interval between subsequent invocations of\n   490\t    a method on the same instance.\n   491\t\n   492\t    You can make cached properties of methods. For example, if you had an\n   493\t    expensive ``get_friends()`` method and wanted to allow calling it without\n   494\t    retrieving the cached value, you could write::\n   495\t\n   496\t        friends = cached_property(get_friends)\n   497\t\n   498\t    While ``person.get_friends()`` will recompute the friends on each call, the\n   499\t    value of the cached property will persist until you delete it as described\n   500\t    above::\n   501\t\n   502\t        x = person.friends         # calls first time\n   503\t        y = person.get_friends()   # calls again\n   504\t        z = person.friends         # does not call\n   505\t        x is z                     # is True\n   506\t\n   507\t.. class:: classproperty(method=None)\n   508\t\n   509\t    Similar to :py:func:`@classmethod <classmethod>`, the ``@classproperty``\n   510\t    decorator converts the result of a method with a single ``cls`` argument\n   511\t    into a property that can be accessed directly from the class.\n   512\t\n   513\t.. function:: keep_lazy(func, *resultclasses)\n   514\t\n   515\t    Django offers many utility functions (particularly in ``django.utils``)\n   516\t    that take a string as their first argument and do something to that string.\n   517\t    These functions are used by template filters as well as directly in other\n   518\t    code.\n   519\t\n   520\t    If you write your own similar functions and deal with translations, you'll\n   521\t    face the problem of what to do when the first argument is a lazy\n   522\t    translation object. You don't want to convert it to a string immediately,\n   523\t    because you might be using this function outside of a view (and hence the\n   524\t    current thread's locale setting will not be correct).\n   525\t\n   526\t    For cases like this, use the ``django.utils.functional.keep_lazy()``\n   527\t    decorator. It modifies the function so that *if* it's called with a lazy\n   528\t    translation as one of its arguments, the function evaluation is delayed\n   529\t    until it needs to be converted to a string.\n   530\t\n   531\t    For example::\n   532\t\n   533\t        from django.utils.functional import keep_lazy, keep_lazy_text\n   534\t\n   535\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   536\t            # Do some conversion on string 's'\n   537\t            ...\n   538\t        fancy_utility_function = keep_lazy(str)(fancy_utility_function)\n   539\t\n   540\t        # Or more succinctly:\n   541\t        @keep_lazy(str)\n   542\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   543\t            ...\n   544\t\n   545\t    The ``keep_lazy()`` decorator takes a number of extra arguments (``*args``)\n   546\t    specifying the type(s) that the original function can return. A common\n   547\t    use case is to have functions that return text. For these, you can pass the\n   548\t    ``str`` type to ``keep_lazy`` (or use the :func:`keep_lazy_text` decorator\n   549\t    described in the next section).\n   550\t\n   551\t    Using this decorator means you can write your function and assume that the\n   552\t    input is a proper string, then add support for lazy translation objects at\n   553\t    the end.\n   554\t\n   555\t.. function:: keep_lazy_text(func)\n   556\t\n   557\t    A shortcut for ``keep_lazy(str)(func)``.\n   558\t\n   559\t    If you have a function that returns text and you want to be able to take\n   560\t    lazy arguments while delaying their evaluation, you can use this\n   561\t    decorator::\n   562\t\n   563\t        from django.utils.functional import keep_lazy, keep_lazy_text\n   564\t\n   565\t        # Our previous example was:\n   566\t        @keep_lazy(str)\n   567\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   568\t            ...\n   569\t\n   570\t        # Which can be rewritten as:\n   571\t        @keep_lazy_text\n   572\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   573\t            ...\n   574\t\n   575\t``django.utils.html``\n   576\t=====================\n   577\t\n   578\t.. module:: django.utils.html\n   579\t   :synopsis: HTML helper functions\n   580\t\n   581\tUsually you should build up HTML using Django's templates to make use of its\n   582\tautoescape mechanism, using the utilities in :mod:`django.utils.safestring`\n   583\twhere appropriate. This module provides some additional low level utilities for\n   584\tescaping HTML.\n   585\t\n   586\t.. function:: escape(text)\n   587\t\n   588\t    Returns the given text with ampersands, quotes and angle brackets encoded\n   589\t    for use in HTML. The input is first coerced to a string and the output has\n   590\t    :func:`~django.utils.safestring.mark_safe` applied.\n   591\t\n   592\t.. function:: conditional_escape(text)\n   593\t\n   594\t    Similar to ``escape()``, except that it doesn't operate on pre-escaped\n   595\t    strings, so it will not double escape.\n   596\t\n   597\t.. function:: format_html(format_string, *args, **kwargs)\n   598\t\n   599\t    This is similar to :meth:`str.format`, except that it is appropriate for\n   600\t    building up HTML fragments. All args and kwargs are passed through\n   601\t    :func:`conditional_escape` before being passed to ``str.format()``.\n   602\t\n   603\t    For the case of building up small HTML fragments, this function is to be\n   604\t    preferred over string interpolation using ``%`` or ``str.format()``\n   605\t    directly, because it applies escaping to all arguments - just like the\n   606\t    template system applies escaping by default.\n   607\t\n   608\t    So, instead of writing::\n   609\t\n   610\t        mark_safe(\"%s <b>%s</b> %s\" % (\n   611\t            some_html,\n   612\t            escape(some_text),\n   613\t            escape(some_other_text),\n   614\t        ))\n   615\t\n   616\t    You should instead use::\n   617\t\n   618\t        format_html(\"{} <b>{}</b> {}\",\n   619\t            mark_safe(some_html),\n   620\t            some_text,\n   621\t            some_other_text,\n   622\t        )\n   623\t\n   624\t    This has the advantage that you don't need to apply :func:`escape` to each\n   625\t    argument and risk a bug and an XSS vulnerability if you forget one.\n   626\t\n   627\t    Note that although this function uses ``str.format()`` to do the\n   628\t    interpolation, some of the formatting options provided by ``str.format()``\n   629\t    (e.g. number formatting) will not work, since all arguments are passed\n   630\t    through :func:`conditional_escape` which (ultimately) calls\n   631\t    :func:`~django.utils.encoding.force_str` on the values.\n   632\t\n   633\t.. function:: format_html_join(sep, format_string, args_generator)\n   634\t\n   635\t    A wrapper of :func:`format_html`, for the common case of a group of\n   636\t    arguments that need to be formatted using the same format string, and then\n   637\t    joined using ``sep``. ``sep`` is also passed through\n   638\t    :func:`conditional_escape`.\n   639\t\n   640\t    ``args_generator`` should be an iterator that returns the sequence of\n   641\t    ``args`` that will be passed to :func:`format_html`. For example::\n   642\t\n   643\t        format_html_join(\n   644\t            '\\n', \"<li>{} {}</li>\",\n   645\t            ((u.first_name, u.last_name) for u in users)\n   646\t        )\n   647\t\n   648\t.. function:: strip_tags(value)\n   649\t\n   650\t    Tries to remove anything that looks like an HTML tag from the string, that\n   651\t    is anything contained within ``<>``.\n   652\t\n   653\t    Absolutely NO guarantee is provided about the resulting string being\n   654\t    HTML safe. So NEVER mark safe the result of a ``strip_tag`` call without\n   655\t    escaping it first, for example with :func:`~django.utils.html.escape`.\n   656\t\n   657\t    For example::\n   658\t\n   659\t        strip_tags(value)\n   660\t\n   661\t    If ``value`` is ``\"<b>Joel</b> <button>is</button> a <span>slug</span>\"``\n   662\t    the return value will be ``\"Joel is a slug\"``.\n   663\t\n   664\t    If you are looking for a more robust solution, take a look at the `bleach\n   665\t    <https://pypi.org/project/bleach/>`_ Python library.\n   666\t\n   667\t.. function:: html_safe()\n   668\t\n   669\t    The ``__html__()`` method on a class helps non-Django templates detect\n   670\t    classes whose output doesn't require HTML escaping.\n   671\t\n   672\t    This decorator defines the ``__html__()`` method on the decorated class\n   673\t    by wrapping ``__str__()`` in :meth:`~django.utils.safestring.mark_safe`.\n   674\t    Ensure the ``__str__()`` method does indeed return text that doesn't\n   675\t    require HTML escaping.\n   676\t\n   677\t``django.utils.http``\n   678\t=====================\n   679\t\n   680\t.. module:: django.utils.http\n   681\t   :synopsis: HTTP helper functions. (URL encoding, cookie handling, ...)\n   682\t\n   683\t.. function:: urlencode(query, doseq=False)\n   684\t\n   685\t    A version of Python's :func:`urllib.parse.urlencode` function that can\n   686\t    operate on ``MultiValueDict`` and non-string values.\n   687\t\n   688\t.. function:: http_date(epoch_seconds=None)\n   689\t\n   690\t    Formats the time to match the :rfc:`1123#section-5.2.14` date format as\n   691\t    specified by HTTP :rfc:`7231#section-7.1.1.1`.\n   692\t\n   693\t    Accepts a floating point number expressed in seconds since the epoch in\n   694\t    UTC--such as that outputted by ``time.time()``. If set to ``None``,\n   695\t    defaults to the current time.\n   696\t\n   697\t    Outputs a string in the format ``Wdy, DD Mon YYYY HH:MM:SS GMT``.\n   698\t\n   699\t.. function:: base36_to_int(s)\n   700\t\n   701\t    Converts a base 36 string to an integer.\n   702\t\n   703\t.. function:: int_to_base36(i)\n   704\t\n   705\t    Converts a positive integer to a base 36 string.\n   706\t\n   707\t.. function:: urlsafe_base64_encode(s)\n   708\t\n   709\t    Encodes a bytestring to a base64 string for use in URLs, stripping any\n   710\t    trailing equal signs.\n   711\t\n   712\t.. function::  urlsafe_base64_decode(s)\n   713\t\n   714\t    Decodes a base64 encoded string, adding back any trailing equal signs that\n   715\t    might have been stripped.\n   716\t\n   717\t``django.utils.module_loading``\n   718\t===============================\n   719\t\n   720\t.. module:: django.utils.module_loading\n   721\t   :synopsis: Functions for working with Python modules.\n   722\t\n   723\tFunctions for working with Python modules.\n   724\t\n   725\t.. function:: import_string(dotted_path)\n   726\t\n   727\t    Imports a dotted module path and returns the attribute/class designated by\n   728\t    the last name in the path. Raises ``ImportError`` if the import failed. For\n   729\t    example::\n   730\t\n   731\t        from django.utils.module_loading import import_string\n   732\t        ValidationError = import_string('django.core.exceptions.ValidationError')\n   733\t\n   734\t    is equivalent to::\n   735\t\n   736\t        from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError\n   737\t\n   738\t``django.utils.safestring``\n   739\t===========================\n   740\t\n   741\t.. module:: django.utils.safestring\n   742\t   :synopsis: Functions and classes for working with strings that can be displayed safely without further escaping in HTML.\n   743\t\n   744\tFunctions and classes for working with \"safe strings\": strings that can be\n   745\tdisplayed safely without further escaping in HTML. Marking something as a \"safe\n   746\tstring\" means that the producer of the string has already turned characters\n   747\tthat should not be interpreted by the HTML engine (e.g. '<') into the\n   748\tappropriate entities.\n   749\t\n   750\t.. class:: SafeString\n   751\t\n   752\t    A ``str`` subclass that has been specifically marked as \"safe\" (requires no\n   753\t    further escaping) for HTML output purposes.\n   754\t\n   755\t.. function:: mark_safe(s)\n   756\t\n   757\t    Explicitly mark a string as safe for (HTML) output purposes. The returned\n   758\t    object can be used everywhere a string is appropriate.\n   759\t\n   760\t    Can be called multiple times on a single string.\n   761\t\n   762\t    Can also be used as a decorator.\n   763\t\n   764\t    For building up fragments of HTML, you should normally be using\n   765\t    :func:`django.utils.html.format_html` instead.\n   766\t\n   767\t    String marked safe will become unsafe again if modified. For example::\n   768\t\n   769\t        >>> mystr = '<b>Hello World</b>   '\n   770\t        >>> mystr = mark_safe(mystr)\n   771\t        >>> type(mystr)\n   772\t        <class 'django.utils.safestring.SafeString'>\n   773\t\n   774\t        >>> mystr = mystr.strip()  # removing whitespace\n   775\t        >>> type(mystr)\n   776\t        <type 'str'>\n   777\t\n   778\t``django.utils.text``\n   779\t=====================\n   780\t\n   781\t.. module:: django.utils.text\n   782\t    :synopsis: Text manipulation.\n   783\t\n   784\t.. function:: format_lazy(format_string, *args, **kwargs)\n   785\t\n   786\t    A version of :meth:`str.format` for when ``format_string``, ``args``,\n   787\t    and/or ``kwargs`` contain lazy objects. The first argument is the string to\n   788\t    be formatted. For example::\n   789\t\n   790\t        from django.utils.text import format_lazy\n   791\t        from django.utils.translation import pgettext_lazy\n   792\t\n   793\t        urlpatterns = [\n   794\t            path(format_lazy('{person}/<int:pk>/', person=pgettext_lazy('URL', 'person')),\n   795\t                 PersonDetailView.as_view()),\n   796\t        ]\n   797\t\n   798\t    This example allows translators to translate part of the URL. If \"person\"\n   799\t    is translated to \"persona\", the regular expression will match\n   800\t    ``persona/(?P<pk>\\d+)/$``, e.g. ``persona/5/``.\n   801\t\n   802\t.. function:: slugify(value, allow_unicode=False)\n   803\t\n   804\t    Converts a string to a URL slug by:\n   805\t\n   806\t    #. Converting to ASCII if ``allow_unicode`` is ``False`` (the default).\n   807\t    #. Converting to lowercase.\n   808\t    #. Removing characters that aren't alphanumerics, underscores, hyphens, or\n   809\t       whitespace.\n   810\t    #. Replacing any whitespace or repeated dashes with single dashes.\n   811\t    #. Removing leading and trailing whitespace, dashes, and underscores.\n   812\t\n   813\t    For example::\n   814\t\n   815\t        >>> slugify(' Joel is a slug ')\n   816\t        'joel-is-a-slug'\n   817\t\n   818\t    If you want to allow Unicode characters, pass ``allow_unicode=True``. For\n   819\t    example::\n   820\t\n   821\t        >>> slugify('你好 World', allow_unicode=True)\n   822\t        '你好-world'\n   823\t\n   824\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   825\t\n   826\t        In older versions, leading and trailing dashes and underscores are not\n   827\t        removed.\n   828\t\n   829\t.. _time-zone-selection-functions:\n   830\t\n   831\t``django.utils.timezone``\n   832\t=========================\n   833\t\n   834\t.. module:: django.utils.timezone\n   835\t    :synopsis: Timezone support.\n   836\t\n   837\t.. data:: utc\n   838\t\n   839\t    :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents UTC.\n   840\t\n   841\t.. function:: get_fixed_timezone(offset)\n   842\t\n   843\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents a time zone\n   844\t    with a fixed offset from UTC.\n   845\t\n   846\t    ``offset`` is a :class:`datetime.timedelta` or an integer number of\n   847\t    minutes. Use positive values for time zones east of UTC and negative\n   848\t    values for west of UTC.\n   849\t\n   850\t.. function:: get_default_timezone()\n   851\t\n   852\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents the\n   853\t    :ref:`default time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   854\t\n   855\t.. function:: get_default_timezone_name()\n   856\t\n   857\t    Returns the name of the :ref:`default time zone\n   858\t    <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   859\t\n   860\t.. function:: get_current_timezone()\n   861\t\n   862\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents the\n   863\t    :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   864\t\n   865\t.. function:: get_current_timezone_name()\n   866\t\n   867\t    Returns the name of the :ref:`current time zone\n   868\t    <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   869\t\n   870\t.. function:: activate(timezone)\n   871\t\n   872\t    Sets the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`. The\n   873\t    ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo`\n   874\t    subclass or a time zone name.\n   875\t\n   876\t.. function:: deactivate()\n   877\t\n   878\t    Unsets the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   879\t\n   880\t.. function:: override(timezone)\n   881\t\n   882\t    This is a Python context manager that sets the :ref:`current time zone\n   883\t    <default-current-time-zone>` on entry with :func:`activate()`, and restores\n   884\t    the previously active time zone on exit. If the ``timezone`` argument is\n   885\t    ``None``, the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` is unset\n   886\t    on entry with :func:`deactivate()` instead.\n   887\t\n   888\t    ``override`` is also usable as a function decorator.\n   889\t\n   890\t.. function:: localtime(value=None, timezone=None)\n   891\t\n   892\t    Converts an aware :class:`~datetime.datetime` to a different time zone,\n   893\t    by default the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   894\t\n   895\t    When ``value`` is omitted, it defaults to :func:`now`.\n   896\t\n   897\t    This function doesn't work on naive datetimes; use :func:`make_aware`\n   898\t    instead.\n   899\t\n   900\t.. function:: localdate(value=None, timezone=None)\n   901\t\n   902\t    Uses :func:`localtime` to convert an aware :class:`~datetime.datetime` to a\n   903\t    :meth:`~datetime.datetime.date` in a different time zone, by default the\n   904\t    :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   905\t\n   906\t    When ``value`` is omitted, it defaults to :func:`now`.\n   907\t\n   908\t    This function doesn't work on naive datetimes.\n   909\t\n   910\t.. function:: now()\n   911\t\n   912\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.datetime` that represents the\n   913\t    current point in time. Exactly what's returned depends on the value of\n   914\t    :setting:`USE_TZ`:\n   915\t\n   916\t    * If :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``False``, this will be a\n   917\t      :ref:`naive <naive_vs_aware_datetimes>` datetime (i.e. a datetime\n   918\t      without an associated timezone) that represents the current time\n   919\t      in the system's local timezone.\n   920\t\n   921\t    * If :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, this will be an\n   922\t      :ref:`aware <naive_vs_aware_datetimes>` datetime representing the\n   923\t      current time in UTC. Note that :func:`now` will always return\n   924\t      times in UTC regardless of the value of :setting:`TIME_ZONE`;\n   925\t      you can use :func:`localtime` to get the time in the current time zone.\n   926\t\n   927\t.. function:: is_aware(value)\n   928\t\n   929\t    Returns ``True`` if ``value`` is aware, ``False`` if it is naive. This\n   930\t    function assumes that ``value`` is a :class:`~datetime.datetime`.\n   931\t\n   932\t.. function:: is_naive(value)\n   933\t\n   934\t    Returns ``True`` if ``value`` is naive, ``False`` if it is aware. This\n   935\t    function assumes that ``value`` is a :class:`~datetime.datetime`.\n   936\t\n   937\t.. function:: make_aware(value, timezone=None, is_dst=None)\n   938\t\n   939\t    Returns an aware :class:`~datetime.datetime` that represents the same\n   940\t    point in time as ``value`` in ``timezone``, ``value`` being a naive\n   941\t    :class:`~datetime.datetime`. If ``timezone`` is set to ``None``, it\n   942\t    defaults to the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   943\t\n   944\t    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.AmbiguousTimeError`` exception is raised if\n   945\t    you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition where the same time\n   946\t    occurs twice (when reverting from DST). Setting ``is_dst`` to ``True`` or\n   947\t    ``False`` will avoid the exception by choosing if the time is\n   948\t    pre-transition or post-transition respectively.\n   949\t\n   950\t    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.NonExistentTimeError`` exception is raised\n   951\t    if you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition such that the\n   952\t    time never occurred. For example, if the 2:00 hour is skipped during a DST\n   953\t    transition, trying to make 2:30 aware in that time zone will raise an\n   954\t    exception. To avoid that you can use ``is_dst`` to specify how\n   955\t    ``make_aware()`` should interpret such a nonexistent time. If\n   956\t    ``is_dst=True`` then the above time would be interpreted as 2:30 DST time\n   957\t    (equivalent to 1:30 local time). Conversely, if ``is_dst=False`` the time\n   958\t    would be interpreted as 2:30 standard time (equivalent to 3:30 local time).\n   959\t\n   960\t    The ``is_dst`` parameter has no effect when using non-``pytz`` timezone\n   961\t    implementations.\n   962\t\n   963\t.. function:: make_naive(value, timezone=None)\n   964\t\n   965\t    Returns a naive :class:`~datetime.datetime` that represents in\n   966\t    ``timezone``  the same point in time as ``value``, ``value`` being an\n   967\t    aware :class:`~datetime.datetime`. If ``timezone`` is set to ``None``, it\n   968\t    defaults to the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   969\t\n   970\t``django.utils.translation``\n   971\t============================\n   972\t\n   973\t.. module:: django.utils.translation\n   974\t   :synopsis: Internationalization support.\n   975\t\n   976\tFor a complete discussion on the usage of the following see the\n   977\t:doc:`translation documentation </topics/i18n/translation>`.\n   978\t\n   979\t.. function:: gettext(message)\n   980\t\n   981\t    Translates ``message`` and returns it as a string.\n   982\t\n   983\t.. function:: pgettext(context, message)\n   984\t\n   985\t    Translates ``message`` given the ``context`` and returns it as a string.\n   986\t\n   987\t    For more information, see :ref:`contextual-markers`.\n   988\t\n   989\t.. function:: gettext_lazy(message)\n   990\t.. function:: pgettext_lazy(context, message)\n   991\t\n   992\t    Same as the non-lazy versions above, but using lazy execution.\n   993\t\n   994\t    See :ref:`lazy translations documentation <lazy-translations>`.\n   995\t\n   996\t.. function:: gettext_noop(message)\n   997\t\n   998\t    Marks strings for translation but doesn't translate them now. get_fixed_timezone(offset):\n    34\t    \"\"\"Return a tzinfo instance with a fixed offset from UTC.\"\"\"\n    35\t    if isinstance(offset, timedelta):\n    36\t        offset = offset.total_seconds() // 60\n    37\t    sign = '-' if offset < 0 else '+'\n    38\t    hhmm = '%02d%02d' % divmod(abs(offset), 60)\n    39\t    name = sign + hhmm\n    40\t    return don't change os.environ['TZ'] and call time.tzset()\n    84\t# because it isn't thread safe.\n    85\t\n    86\t\n    87\tdef activate(timezone):\n    88\t    \"\"\"\n    89\t    Set the time zone for the current thread.\n    90\t\n    91\t    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a tzinfo subclass or a\n    92\t    time zone name.\n    93\t    \"\"\"\n    94\t    if isinstance(timezone, override(ContextDecorator):\n   113\t    \"\"\"\n   114\t    Temporarily set the time zone for the current thread.\n   115\t\n   116\t    This is a context manager that uses django.utils.timezone.activate()\n   117\t    to set the timezone on entry and restores the previously active timezone\n   118\t    on exit.\n   119\t\n   120\t    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a ``tzinfo`` subclass, a\n   121\t    time zone name, or ``None``. If it is ``None``, Django enables the default\n   122\t    time zone.\n   123\t    \"\"\"\n   124\t    def __init__(self, timezone):\n   125\t        self.timezone = timezone\n   126\t\n   127\t    def __enter__(self):\n   128\t        self.old_timezone = getattr(_active, 'value', None)\n   129\t        if self.timezone is None:\n   130\t            deactivate()\n   131\t        else:\n   132\t            activate(self.timezone)\n   133\t\n   134\t    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):\n   135\t        if self.old_timezone is None:\n   136\t            deactivate()\n   137\t        else:\n   138\t            _active.value = self.old_timezone\n   139\t\n   140\t\n   141\t# Templates\n   142\t\n   143\tdef template_localtime(value, use_tz=None):\n   144\t    \"\"\"\n   145\t    Check if value is a datetime and converts it to local time if necessary.\n   146\t\n   147\t    If use_tz is provided and is not None, that will force the value to\n   148\t    be converted (or not), overriding the value of settings.USE_TZ.\n   149\t\n   150\t    This function is designed for use by the template engine.\n   151\t    \"\"\"\n   152\t    should_convert = (\n   153\t        isinstance(value, datetime) and\n   154\t        (settings.USE_TZ if use_tz is None else use_tz) and\n   155\t        not is_naive(value) and\n   156\t        getattr(value, 'convert_to_local_time', True)\n   157\t    )\n   158\t    return localtime(value) if should_convert else value\n   159\t\n   160\t\n   161\t# Utilities\n   162\t\n   163\tdef localtime(value=None, timezone=None):\n   164\t    \"\"\"\n   165\t    Convert an aware datetime.datetime to local time.\n   166\t\n   167\t    Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to\n   168\t    now().\n   169\t\n   170\t    Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone\n   171\t    is specified.\n   172\t    \"\"\"\n   173\t    if value is None:\n   174\t        value = now()\n   175\t    if timezone is None:\n   176\t        timezone = get_current_timezone()\n   177\t    # Emulate the behavior of astimezone() on Python < 3.6.\n   178\t    if is_naive(value):\n   179\t        raise ValueError(\"localtime() cannot be applied to a naive datetime\")\n   180\t    return value.astimezone(timezone)\n   181\t\n   182\t\n   183\tdef localdate(value=None, timezone=None):\n   184\t    \"\"\"\n   185\t    Convert an aware datetime to local time and return the value's date.\n   186\t\n   187\t    Only aware datetimes are allowed. ForcedTimeZoneDatabaseTests(TransactionTestCase):\n   598\t    \"\"\"\n   599\t    Test the TIME_ZONE database configuration parameter.\n   600\t\n   601\t    Since this involves reading and writing @classmethod\n   608\t    def setUpClass(cls):\n   609\t        # @skipIfDBFeature and @skipUnlessDBFeature cannot be chained. The\n   610\t        # outermost takes precedence. Handle skipping manually instead.\n   611\t        if connection.features.supports_timezones:\n   612\t            raise SkipTest(\"Database has feature(s) supports_timezones\")\n   613\t        if not connection.features.test_db_allows_multiple_connections:\n   614\t            raise SkipTest(\"Database doesn't support feature(s): test_db_allows_multiple_connections\")\n   615\t\n   616\t        super().setUpClass()\n   617\t\n   618\t    def test_read_datetime(self):\n   619\t        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   620\t        Event.objects.create(dt=fake_dt)\n   621\t\n   622\t        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\n   623\t            event = Event.objects.get()\n   624\t            dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   625\t        self.assertEqual(event.dt, dt)\n   626\t\n   627\t    def test_write_datetime(self):\n   628\t        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   629\t        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\n   630\t            Event.objects.create(dt=dt)\n   631\t\n   632\t        event = Event.objects.get()\n   633\t        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   634\t        self.assertEqual(event.dt, fake_dt)\n   635\t\n   636\t\n   637\t@override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi')\n   638\tclass SerializationTests(SimpleTestCase):\n   639\t\n   640\t    # Backend-specific notes:\n   641\t    # - JSON supports only milliseconds, microseconds will be truncated.\n   642\t    # - PyYAML dumps the UTC offset correctly for timezone-aware datetimes.\n   643\t    #   When PyYAML < 5.3 loads this representation, it subtracts the offset\n   644\t    #   and returns a naive datetime object in UTC. PyYAML 5.3+ loads timezones\n   645\t    #   correctly.\n   646\t    # Tests are adapted to take these quirks into account.\n   647\t\n   648\t    def assert_python_contains_datetime(self, objects, dt):\n   649\t        self.assertEqual(objects[0]['fields']['dt'], dt)\n   650\t\n   651\t    def assert_json_contains_datetime(self, json, dt):\n   652\t        self.assertIn('\"fields\": {\"dt\": \"%s\"}' % dt, json)\n   653\t\n   654\t    def assert_xml_contains_datetime(self, xml, dt):\n   655\t        field = parseString(xml).getElementsByTagName('field')[0]\n   656\t        self.assertXMLEqual(field.childNodes[0].wholeText, dt)\n   657\t\n   658\t    def assert_yaml_contains_datetime(self, yaml, dt):\n   659\t        # Depending on the yaml dumper, '!timestamp' might be absent\n   660\t        self.assertRegex(yaml, r\"\\n  fields: {dt: !(!timestamp)? '%s'}\" % re.escape(dt))\n   661\t\n   662\t    def test_naive_datetime(self):\n   663\t        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30)\n   664\t\n   665\t        data = serializers.serialize('python', [Event(dt=dt)])\n   666\t        self.assert_python_contains_datetime(data, dt)\n   667\t        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('python', data)).object\n   668\t        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   669\t\n   670\t        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\n   671\t        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \"2011-09-01T13:20:30\")\n   672\t        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\n   673\t        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   674\t\n   675\t        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\n   676\t        self.assert_xml_contains_datetime(data, \"2011-09-01T13:20:30\")\n   677\t        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('xml', data)).object\n   678\t        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   679\t\n   680\t        if not isinstance(serializers.get_serializer('yaml'), serializers.BadSerializer):\n   681\t            data = serializers.serialize('yaml', [Event(dt=dt)], default_flow_style=None)\n   682\t            self.assert_yaml_contains_datetime(data, \"2011-09-01 13:20:30\")\n   683\t            obj = next(serializers.deserialize('yaml', data)).object\n   684\t            self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   685\t\n   686\t    def test_naive_datetime_with_microsecond(self):\n   687\t        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, 405060)\n   688\t\n         else:\n   813\t                self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   814\t\n   815\t\n   816\t# RemovedInDjango50Warning: When the deprecation ends, remove setUpClass() and\n   817\t# USE_L10N=False. The tests should remain because format-related settings will\n   818\t# take precedence over locale-dictated formats.\n   819\t@override_settings(DATETIME_FORMAT='c', TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi', USE_L10N=False, USE_TZ=True)\n   820\tclass TemplateTests(SimpleTestCase):\n   821\t    @classmethod\n   822\t    def setUpClass(cls):\n   823\t        with ignore_warnings(category=RemovedInDjango50Warning):\n   824\t            super().setUpClass()\n   825\t\n   826\t    @requires_tz_support\n   827\t    def test_localtime_templatetag_and_filters(self):\n   828\t        \"\"\"\n   829\t        Test the {% localtime %} templatetag and related filters.\n   830\t        \"\"\"\n   831\t        datetimes = {\n   832\t            'utc': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC),\n   833\t            'eat': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, tzinfo=EAT),\n   834\t            'ict': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=ICT),\n   835\t            'naive': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30),\n   836\t        }\n   837\t        templates = {\n   838\t            'notag': Template(\"{% load tz %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}\"),\n   839\t            'noarg': Template(\n   840\t                \"{% load tz %}{% localtime %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|\"\n   841\t                \"{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}{% endlocaltime %}\"\n   842\t            ),\n   843\t            'on': Template(\n   844\t                \"{% load tz %}{% localtime on %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|\"\n   845\t                \"{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}{% endlocaltime %}\"\n   846\t            ),\n   847\t            'off': Template(\n   848\t                \"{% load tz %}{% localtime off %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|\"\n   849\t                \"{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}{% endlocaltime %}\"\n   850\t            ),\n   851\t        }\n   852\t\n   853\t        # Transform a list of keys in 'datetimes' to the expected template\n   854\t        # output. Specifically, it will exclude any\n  1196\tsetting whose name includes any of the following:\n  1197\t\n  1198\t* ``'API'``\n  1199\t* ``'KEY'``\n  1200\t* ``'PASS'``\n  1201\t* ``'SECRET'``\n  1202\t* ``'SIGNATURE'``\n  1203\t* ``'TOKEN'``\n  1204\t\n  1205\tNote that these are *partial* matches. ``'PASS'`` will also match PASSWORD,\n  1206\tjust as ``'TOKEN'`` will also match TOKENIZED and so on.\n  1207\t\n  1208\tStill, note that there are always going to be sections of your debug output\n  1209\tthat are inappropriate for public consumption. File paths, configuration\n  1210\toptions and the like all give attackers extra information about your server.\n  1211\t\n  1212\tIt is also important to remember that when running with :setting:`DEBUG`\n  1213\tturned on, Django will remember every SQL query it executes. This is useful\n  1214\twhen you're debugging, but it'll rapidly consume memory on a production server.\n  1215\t\n  1216\tFinally, if :setting:`DEBUG` is ``False``, you also need to properly set\n  1217\tthe :setting:`ALLOWED_HOSTS` setting. Failing to do so will result in all\n  1218\trequests being returned as \"Bad Request (400)\".\n  1219\t\n  1220\t.. note::\n  1221\t\n  1222\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n  1223\t    startproject <startproject>` sets ``DEBUG = True`` for convenience.\n  1224\t\n  1225\t.. setting:: DEBUG_PROPAGATE_EXCEPTIONS\n  1226\t\n  1227\t``DEBUG_PROPAGATE_EXCEPTIONS``\n  1228\t------------------------------\n  1229\t\n  1230\tDefault: ``False``\n  1231\t\n  1232\tIf set to ``True``, Django's exception handling of view functions\n  1233\t(:data:`~django.conf.urls.handler500`, or the debug view if :setting:`DEBUG`\n  1234\tis ``True``) and logging of 500 responses (:ref:`django-request-logger`) is\n  1235\tskipped and exceptions propagate upward.\n  1236\t\n  1237\tThis can be useful for some test setups. It shouldn't be used on a live site\n  1238\tunless you want your web server (instead of Django) to generate \"Internal\n  1239\tServer Error\" responses.
