 1260\t----------------------\n  1261\t\n  1262\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n  1263\t\n  1264\tDefault: ``'``:class:`django.db.models.AutoField`\\ ``'``\n  1265\t\n  1266\tDefault primary key field type to use for models that don't have a field with\n  1267\t:attr:`primary_key=True <django.db.models.Field.primary_key>`.\n  1268\t\n  1269\t.. admonition:: Migrating auto-created through tables\n  1270\t\n  1271\t    The value of ``DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD`` will be respected when creating new\n  1272\t    auto-created through tables for many-to-many relationships.\n  1273\t\n  1274\t    Unfortunately, the primary keys of existing auto-created through tables\n  1275\t    cannot currently be updated by the migrations framework.\n  1276\t\n  1277\t    This means that if you switch the value of ``DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD`` and then\n  1278\t    generate migrations, the primary keys of the related models will be\n  1279\t    updated, as will the foreign keys from the through table, but the primary\n  1280\t    key of the auto-created through table will not be migrated.\n  1281\t\n  1282\t    In order to address this, you should add a\n  1283\t    :class:`~django.db.migrations.operations.RunSQL` operation to your\n  1284\t    migrations to perform the required ``ALTER TABLE`` step. You can check the\n  1285\t    existing table name through ``sqlmigrate``, ``dbshell``, or with the\n  1286\t    field’s ``remote_field.through._meta.db_table`` property.\n  1287\t\n  1288\t    Explicitly defined through models are already handled by the migrations\n  1289\t    system.\n  1290\t\n  1291\t    Allowing automatic migrations for the primary key of existing auto-created\n  1292\t    through tables :ticket:`may be implemented at a later date <32674>`.\n  1293\t\n  1294\t.. setting:: DEFAULT_CHARSET\n  1295\t\n  1296\t``DEFAULT_CHARSET``\n  1297\t-------------------\n  1298\t\n  1299\tDefault: ``'utf-8'``\n  1300\t\n  1301\tDefault charset to use for all ``HttpResponse`` objects, if a MIME type isn't\n  1302\tmanually specified. Used when constructing the ``Content-Type`` header.\n  1303\t\n  1304\t.. setting:: DEFAULT_EXCEPTION_REPORTER\n  1305\t\n  1306\t``DEFAULT_EXCEPTION_REPORTER``\n  1307\t------------------------------\n  1308\t\n  1309\tDefault: ``'``:class:`django.views.debug.ExceptionReporter`\\ ``'``\n  1310\t\n  1311\tDefault exception reporter class to be used if none has been assigned to the\n  1312\t:class:`~django.http.HttpRequest` instance yet. See\n  1313\t:ref:`custom-error-reports`.\n  1314\t\n  1315\t.. setting:: DEFAULT_EXCEPTION_REPORTER_FILTER\n  1316\t\n  1317\t``DEFAULT_EXCEPTION_REPORTER_FILTER``\n  1318\t-------------------------------------\n  1319\t\n  1320\tDefault: ``'``:class:`django.views.debug.SafeExceptionReporterFilter`\\ ``'``\n  1321\t\n  1322\tDefault exception reporter filter class to be used if none has been assigned to\n  1323\tthe :class:`~django.http.HttpRequest` instance yet.\n  1324\tSee :ref:`Filtering error reports<filtering-error-reports>`.\n  1325\t\n  1326\t.. setting:: DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE\n  1327\t\n  1328\t``DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE``\n  1329\t------------------------\n  1330\t\n  1331\tDefault: ``'``:class:`django.core.files.storage.FileSystemStorage`\\ ``'``\n  1332\t\n  1333\tDefault file storage class to be used for any file-related operations that don't\n  1334\tspecify a particular storage system. See :doc:`/topics/files`.\n  1335\t\n  1336\t.. setting:: DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL\n  1337\t\n  1338\t``DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL``\n  1339\t----------------------\n  1340\t\n  1341\tDefault: ``'webmaster@localhost'``\n  1342\t\n  1343\tDefault email address to use for various automated correspondence from the\n  1344\tsite manager(s). This doesn't include error messages sent to :setting:`ADMINS`\n  1345\tand :setting:`MANAGERS`; for that, see :setting:`SERVER_EMAIL`.\n  1346\t\n  1347\t.. setting:: DEFAULT_INDEX_TABLESPACE\n  1348\t\n  1349\t``DEFAULT_INDEX_TABLESPACE``\n  1350\t----------------------------\n  1351\t\n  1352\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n  1353\t\n  1354\tDefault tablespace to use for indexes on fields that don't specify\n  1355\tone, if the backend supports it (see :doc:`/topics/db/tablespaces`).\n  1356\t\n  1357\t.. setting:: DEFAULT_TABLESPACE\n  1358\t\n  1359\t``DEFAULT_TABLESPACE``\n  1360\t----------------------\n  1361\t\n  1362\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n  1363\t\n  1364\tDefault tablespace to use for models that don't specify one, if the\n  1365\tbackend supports it (see :doc:`/topics/db/tablespaces`).\n  1366\t\n  1367\t.. setting:: DISALLOWED_USER_AGENTS\n  1368\t\n  1369\t``DISALLOWED_USER_AGENTS``\n  1370\t--------------------------\n  1371\t\n  1372\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  1373\t\n  1374\tList of compiled regular expression objects representing User-Agent strings\n  1375\tthat are not allowed to visit any page, systemwide. Use this for bots/crawlers.\n  1376\tThis is only used if ``CommonMiddleware`` is installed (see\n  1377\t:doc:`/topics/http/middleware`).\n  1378\t\n  1379\t.. setting:: EMAIL_BACKEND\n  1380\t\n  1381\t``EMAIL_BACKEND``\n  1382\t-----------------\n  1383\t\n  1384\tDefault: ``'``:class:`django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend`\\ ``'``\n  1385\t\n  1386\tThe backend to use for sending emails. For the list of available backends see\n  1387\t:doc:`/topics/email`.\n  1388\t\n  1389\t.. setting:: EMAIL_FILE_PATH\n  1390\t\n  1391\t``EMAIL_FILE_PATH``\n  1392\t-------------------\n  1393\t\n  1394\tDefault: Not defined\n  1395\t\n  1396\tThe directory used by the :ref:`file email backend <topic-email-file-backend>`\n  1397\tto store output files.\n  1398\t\n  1399\t.. setting:: EMAIL_HOST\n  1400\t\n  1401\t``EMAIL_HOST``\n  1402\t--------------\n  1403\t\n  1404\tDefault: ``'localhost'``\n  1405\t\n  1406\tThe host to use for sending email.\n  1407\t\n  1408\tSee also :setting:`EMAIL_PORT`.\n  1409\t\n  1410\t.. setting:: EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD\n  1411\t\n  1412\t``EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD``\n  1413\t-----------------------\n  1414\t\n  1415\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n  1416\t\n  1417\tPassword to use for the SMTP server defined in :setting:`EMAIL_HOST`. This\n  1418\tsetting is used in conjunction with :setting:`EMAIL_HOST_USER` when\n  1419\tauthenticating to the SMTP server. If either of these settings is empty,\n  1420\tDjango won't attempt authentication.\n  1421\t\n  1422\tSee also :setting:`EMAIL_HOST_USER`.\n  1423\t\n  1424\t.. setting:: EMAIL_HOST_USER\n  1425\t\n  1426\t``EMAIL_HOST_USER``\n  1427\t-------------------\n  1428\t\n  1429\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n  1430\t\n  1431\tUsername to use for the SMTP server defined in :setting:`EMAIL_HOST`.\n  1432\tIf empty, Django won't attempt authentication.\n  1433\t\n  1434\tSee also :setting:`EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD`.\n  1435\t\n  1436\t.. setting:: EMAIL_PORT\n  1437\t\n  1438\t``EMAIL_PORT``\n  1439\t--------------\n  1440\t\n  1441\tDefault: ``25``\n  1442\t\n  1443\tPort to use for the SMTP server defined in :setting:`EMAIL_HOST`.\n  1444\t\n  1445\t.. setting:: EMAIL_SUBJECT_PREFIX\n  1446\t\n  1447\t``EMAIL_SUBJECT_PREFIX``\n  1448\t------------------------\n  1449\t\n  1450\tDefault: ``'[Django] '``\n  1451\t\n  1452\tSubject-line prefix for email messages sent with ``django.core.mail.mail_admins``\n  1453\tor ``django.core.mail.mail_managers``. You'll probably want to include the\n  1454\ttrailing space.\n  1455\t\n  1456\t.. setting:: EMAIL_USE_LOCALTIME\n  1457\t\n  1458\t``EMAIL_USE_LOCALTIME``\n  1459\t-----------------------\n  1460\t\n  1461\tDefault: ``False``\n  1462\t\n  1463\tWhether to send the SMTP ``Date`` header of email messages in the local time\n  1464\tzone (``True``) or in UTC (``False``).\n  1465\t\n  1466\t.. setting:: EMAIL_USE_TLS\n  1467\t\n  1468\t``EMAIL_USE_TLS``\n  1469\t-----------------\n  1470\t\n  1471\tDefault: ``False``\n  1472\t\n  1473\tWhether to use a TLS (secure) connection when talking to the SMTP server.\n  1474\tThis is used for explicit TLS connections, generally on port 587. If you are\n  1475\texperiencing hanging connections, see the implicit TLS setting\n  1476\t:setting:`EMAIL_USE_SSL`.\n  1477\t\n  1478\t.. setting:: EMAIL_USE_SSL\n  1479\t\n  1480\t``EMAIL_USE_SSL``\n  1481\t-----------------\n  1482\t\n  1483\tDefault: ``False``\n  1484\t\n  1485\tWhether to use an implicit TLS (secure) connection when talking to the SMTP\n  1486\tserver. In most email documentation this type of TLS connection is referred\n  1487\tto as SSL. It is generally used on port 465. If you are experiencing problems,\n  1488\tsee the explicit TLS setting :setting:`EMAIL_USE_TLS`.\n  1489\t\n  1490\tNote that :setting:`EMAIL_USE_TLS`/:setting:`EMAIL_USE_SSL` are mutually\n  1491\texclusive, so only set one of those settings to ``True``.\n  1492\t\n  1493\t.. setting:: EMAIL_SSL_CERTFILE\n  1494\t\n  1495\t``EMAIL_SSL_CERTFILE``\n  1496\t----------------------\n  1497\t\n  1498\tDefault: ``None``\n  1499\t\n  1500\tIf :setting:`EMAIL_USE_SSL` or :setting:`EMAIL_USE_TLS` is ``True``, you can\n  1501\toptionally specify the path to a PEM-formatted certificate chain file to use\n  1502\tfor the SSL connection.\n  1503\t\n  1504\t.. setting:: EMAIL_SSL_KEYFILE\n  1505\t\n  1506\t``EMAIL_SSL_KEYFILE``\n  1507\t---------------------\n  1508\t\n  1509\tDefault: ``None``\n  1510\t\n  1511\tIf :setting:`EMAIL_USE_SSL` or :setting:`EMAIL_USE_TLS` is ``True``, you can\n  1512\toptionally specify the path to a PEM-formatted private key file to use for the\n  1513\tSSL connection.\n  1514\t\n  1515\tNote that setting :setting:`EMAIL_SSL_CERTFILE` and :setting:`EMAIL_SSL_KEYFILE`\n  1516\tdoesn't result in any certificate checking. They're passed to the underlying SSL\n  1517\tconnection. Please refer to the documentation of Python's\n  1518\t:func:`python:ssl.wrap_socket` function for details on how the certificate chain\n  1519\tfile and private key file are handled.\n  1520\t\n  1521\t.. setting:: EMAIL_TIMEOUT\n  1522\t\n  1523\t``EMAIL_TIMEOUT``\n  1524\t-----------------\n  1525\t\n  1526\tDefault: ``None``\n  1527\t\n  1528\tSpecifies a timeout in seconds for blocking operations like the connection\n  1529\tattempt.\n  1530\t\n  1531\t.. setting:: FILE_UPLOAD_HANDLERS\n  1532\t\n  1533\t``FILE_UPLOAD_HANDLERS``\n  1534\t------------------------\n  1535\t\n  1536\tDefault::\n  1537\t\n  1538\t    [\n  1539\t        'django.core.files.uploadhandler.MemoryFileUploadHandler',\n  1540\t        'django.core.files.uploadhandler.TemporaryFileUploadHandler',\n  1541\t    ]\n  1542\t\n  1543\tA list of handlers to use for uploading. Changing this setting allows complete\n  1544\tcustomization -- even replacement -- of Django's upload process.\n  1545\t\n  1546\tSee :doc:`/topics/files` for details.\n  1547\t\n  1548\t.. setting:: FILE_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE\n  1549\t\n  1550\t``FILE_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE``\n  1551\t-------------------------------\n  1552\t\n  1553\tDefault: ``2621440`` (i.e. 2.5 MB).\n  1554\t\n  1555\tThe maximum size (in bytes) that an upload will be before it gets streamed to\n  1556\tthe file system. See :doc:`/topics/files` for details.\n  1557\t\n  1558\tSee also :setting:`DATA_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE`.\n  1559\t\n  1560\t.. setting:: FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS\n  1561\t\n  1562\t``FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS``\n  1563\t-------------------------------------\n  1564\t\n  1565\tDefault: ``None``\n  1566\t\n  1567\tThe numeric mode to apply to directories created in the process of uploading\n  1568\tfiles.\n  1569\t\n  1570\tThis setting also determines the default permissions for collected static\n  1571\tdirectories when using the :djadmin:`collectstatic` management command. See\n  1572\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` for details on overriding it.\n  1573\t\n  1574\tThis value mirrors the functionality and caveats of the\n  1575\t:setting:`FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS` setting.\n  1576\t\n  1577\t.. setting:: FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS\n  1578\t\n  1579\t``FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS``\n  1580\t---------------------------\n  1581\t\n  1582\tDefault: ``0o644``\n  1583\t\n  1584\tThe numeric mode (i.e. ``0o644``) to set newly uploaded files to. For\n  1585\tmore information about what these modes mean, see the documentation for\n  1586\t:func:`os.chmod`.\n  1587\t\n  1588\tIf ``None``, you'll get operating-system dependent behavior. On most platforms,\n  1589\ttemporary files will have a mode of ``0o600``, and files saved from memory will\n  1590\tbe saved using the system's standard umask.\n  1591\t\n  1592\tFor security reasons, these permissions aren't applied to the temporary files\n  1593\tthat are stored in :setting:`FILE_UPLOAD_TEMP_DIR`.\n  1594\t\n  1595\tThis setting also determines the default permissions for collected static files\n  1596\twhen using the :djadmin:`collectstatic` management command. See\n  1597\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` for details on overriding it.\n  1598\t\n  1599\t.. warning::\n  1600\t\n  1601\t    **Always prefix the mode with** ``0o`` **.**\n  1602\t\n  1603\t    If you're not familiar with file modes, please note that the ``0o`` prefix\n  1604\t    is very important: it indicates an octal number, which is the way that\n  1605\t    modes must be specified. If you try to use ``644``, you'll get totally\n  1606\t    incorrect behavior.\n  1607\t\n  1608\t.. setting:: FILE_UPLOAD_TEMP_DIR\n  1609\t\n  1610\t``FILE_UPLOAD_TEMP_DIR``\n  1611\t------------------------\n  1612\t\n  1613\tDefault: ``None``\n  1614\t\n  1615\tThe directory to store data to (typically files larger than\n  1616\t:setting:`FILE_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE`) temporarily while uploading files.\n  1617\tIf ``None``, Django will use the standard temporary directory for the operating\n  1618\tsystem. For example, this will default to ``/tmp`` on \\*nix-style operating\n  1619\tsystems.\n  1620\t\n  1621\tSee :doc:`/topics/files` for details.\n  1622\t\n  1623\t.. setting:: FIRST_DAY_OF_WEEK\n  1624\t\n  1625\t``FIRST_DAY_OF_WEEK``\n  1626\t---------------------\n  1627\t\n  1628\tDefault: ``0`` (Sunday)\n  1629\t\n  1630\tA number representing the first day of the week. This is especially useful\n  1631\twhen displaying a calendar. This value is only used when not using\n  1632\tformat internationalization, or when a format cannot be found for the\n  1633\tcurrent locale.\n  1634\t\n  1635\tThe value must be an integer from 0 to 6, where 0 means Sunday, 1 means\n  1636\tMonday and so on.\n  1637\t\n  1638\t.. setting:: FIXTURE_DIRS\n  1639\t\n  1640\t``FIXTURE_DIRS``\n  1641\t----------------\n  1642\t\n  1643\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  1644\t\n  1645\tList of directories searched for fixture files, in addition to the\n  1646\t``fixtures`` directory of each application, in search order.\n  1647\t\n  1648\tNote that these paths should use Unix-style forward slashes, even on Windows.\n  1649\t\n  1650\tSee :ref:`initial-data-via-fixtures` and :ref:`topics-testing-fixtures`.\n  1651\t\n  1652\t.. setting:: FORCE_SCRIPT_NAME\n  1653\t\n  1654\t``FORCE_SCRIPT_NAME``\n  1655\t---------------------\n  1656\t\n  1657\tDefault: ``None``\n  1658\t\n  1659\tIf not ``None``, this will be used as the value of the ``SCRIPT_NAME``\n  1660\tenvironment variable in any HTTP request. This setting can be used to override\n  1661\tthe server-provided value of ``SCRIPT_NAME``, which may be a rewritten version\n  1662\tof the preferred value or not supplied at all. It is also used by\n  1663\t:func:`django.setup()` to set the URL resolver script prefix outside of the\n  1664\trequest/response cycle (e.g. in management commands and standalone scripts) to\n  1665\tgenerate correct URLs when ``SCRIPT_NAME`` is not ``/``.\n  1666\t\n  1667\t.. setting:: FORM_RENDERER\n  1668\t\n  1669\t``FORM_RENDERER``\n  1670\t-----------------\n  1671\t\n  1672\tDefault: ``'``:class:`django.forms.renderers.DjangoTemplates`\\ ``'``\n  1673\t\n  1674\tThe class that renders form widgets. It must implement :ref:`the low-level\n  1675\trender API <low-level-widget-render-api>`. Included form renderers are:\n  1676\t\n  1677\t* ``'``:class:`django.forms.renderers.DjangoTemplates`\\ ``'``\n  1678\t* ``'``:class:`django.forms.renderers.Jinja2`\\ ``'``\n  1679\t\n  1680\t.. setting:: FORMAT_MODULE_PATH\n  1681\t\n  1682\t``FORMAT_MODULE_PATH``\n  1683\t----------------------\n  1684\t\n  1685\tDefault: ``None``\n  1686\t\n  1687\tA full Python path to a Python package that contains custom format definitions\n  1688\tfor project locales. If not ``None``, Django will check for a ``formats.py``\n  1689\tfile, under the directory named as the current locale, and will use the\n  1690\tformats defined in this file.\n  1691\t\n  1692\tFor example, if :setting:`FORMAT_MODULE_PATH` is set to ``mysite.formats``,\n  1693\tand current language is ``en`` (English), Django will expect a directory tree\n  1694\tlike::\n  1695\t\n  1696\t    mysite/\n  1697\t        formats/\n  1698\t            __init__.py\n  1699\t            en/\n  1700\t                __init__.py\n  1701\t                formats.py\n  1702\t\n  1703\tYou can also set this setting to a list of Python paths, for example::\n  1704\t\n  1705\t    FORMAT_MODULE_PATH = [\n  1706\t        'mysite.formats',\n  1707\t        'some_app.formats',\n  1708\t    ]\n  1709\t\n  1710\tWhen Django searches for a certain format, it will go through all given Python\n  1711\tpaths until it finds a module that actually defines the given format. This\n  1712\tmeans that formats defined in packages farther up in the list will take\n  1713\tprecedence over the same formats in packages farther down.\n  1714\t\n  1715\tAvailable formats are:\n  1716\t\n  1717\t* :setting:`DATE_FORMAT`\n  1718\t* :setting:`DATE_INPUT_FORMATS`\n  1719\t* :setting:`DATETIME_FORMAT`,\n  1720\t* :setting:`DATETIME_INPUT_FORMATS`\n  1721\t* :setting:`DECIMAL_SEPARATOR`\n  1722\t* :setting:`FIRST_DAY_OF_WEEK`\n  1723\t* :setting:`MONTH_DAY_FORMAT`\n  1724\t* :setting:`NUMBER_GROUPING`\n  1725\t* :setting:`SHORT_DATE_FORMAT`\n  1726\t* :setting:`SHORT_DATETIME_FORMAT`\n  1727\t* :setting:`THOUSAND_SEPARATOR`\n  1728\t* :setting:`TIME_FORMAT`\n  1729\t* :setting:`TIME_INPUT_FORMATS`\n  1730\t* :setting:`YEAR_MONTH_FORMAT`\n  1731\t\n  1732\t.. setting:: IGNORABLE_404_URLS\n  1733\t\n  1734\t``IGNORABLE_404_URLS``\n  1735\t----------------------\n  1736\t\n  1737\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  1738\t\n  1739\tList of compiled regular expression objects describing URLs that should be\n  1740\tignored when reporting HTTP 404 errors via email (see\n  1741\t:doc:`/howto/error-reporting`). Regular expressions are matched against\n  1742\t:meth:`request's full paths <django.http.HttpRequest.get_full_path>` (including\n  1743\tquery string, if any). Use this if your site does not provide a commonly\n  1744\trequested file such as ``favicon.ico`` or ``robots.txt``.\n  1745\t\n  1746\tThis is only used if\n  1747\t:class:`~django.middleware.common.BrokenLinkEmailsMiddleware` is enabled (see\n  1748\t:doc:`/topics/http/middleware`).\n  1749\t\n  1750\t.. setting:: INSTALLED_APPS\n  1751\t\n  1752\t``INSTALLED_APPS``\n  1753\t------------------\n  1754\t\n  1755\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  1756\t\n  1757\tA list of strings designating all applications that are enabled in this\n  1758\tDjango installation. Each string should be a dotted Python path to:\n  1759\t\n  1760\t* an application configuration class (preferred), or\n  1761\t* a package containing an application.\n  1762\t\n  1763\t:doc:`Learn more about application configurations </ref/applications>`.\n  1764\t\n  1765\t.. admonition:: Use the application registry for introspection\n  1766\t\n  1767\t    Your code should never access :setting:`INSTALLED_APPS` directly. Use\n  1768\t    :attr:`django.apps.apps` instead.\n  1769\t\n  1770\t.. admonition:: Application names and labels must be unique in\n  1771\t                :setting:`INSTALLED_APPS`\n  1772\t\n  1773\t    Application :attr:`names <django.apps.AppConfig.name>` — the dotted Python\n  1774\t    path to the application package — must be unique. There is no way to\n  1775\t    include the same application twice, short of duplicating its code under\n  1776\t    another name.\n  1777\t\n  1778\t    Application :attr:`labels <django.apps.AppConfig.label>` — by default the\n  1779\t    final part of the name — must be unique too. For example, you can't\n  1780\t    include both ``django.contrib.auth`` and ``myproject.auth``. However, you\n  1781\t    can relabel an application with a custom configuration that defines a\n  1782\t    different :attr:`~django.apps.AppConfig.label`.\n  1783\t\n  1784\t    These rules apply regardless of whether :setting:`INSTALLED_APPS`\n  1785\t    references application configuration classes or application packages.\n  1786\t\n  1787\tWhen several applications provide different versions of the same resource\n  1788\t(template, static file, management command, translation), the application\n  1789\tlisted first in :setting:`INSTALLED_APPS` has precedence.\n  1790\t\n  1791\t.. setting:: INTERNAL_IPS\n  1792\t\n  1793\t``INTERNAL_IPS``\n  1794\t----------------\n  1795\t\n  1796\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  1797\t\n  1798\tA list of IP addresses, as strings, that:\n  1799\t\n  1800\t* Allow the :func:`~django.template.context_processors.debug` context processor\n  1801\t  to add some variables to the template context.\n  1802\t* Can use the :ref:`admindocs bookmarklets <admindocs-bookmarklets>` even if\n  1803\t  not logged in as a staff user.\n  1804\t* Are marked as \"internal\" (as opposed to \"EXTERNAL\") in\n  1805\t  :class:`~django.utils.log.AdminEmailHandler` emails.\n  1806\t\n  1807\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_CODE\n  1808\t\n  1809\t``LANGUAGE_CODE``\n  1810\t-----------------\n  1811\t\n  1812\tDefault: ``'en-us'``\n  1813\t\n  1814\tA string representing the language code for this installation. This should be in\n  1815\tstandard :term:`language ID format <language code>`. For example, U.S. English\n  1816\tis ``\"en-us\"``. See also the `list of language identifiers`_ and\n  1817\t:doc:`/topics/i18n/index`.\n  1818\t\n  1819\t:setting:`USE_I18N` must be active for this setting to have any effect.\n  1820\t\n  1821\tIt serves two purposes:\n  1822\t\n  1823\t* If the locale middleware isn't in use, it decides which translation is served\n  1824\t  to all users.\n  1825\t* If the locale middleware is active, it provides a fallback language in case the\n  1826\t  user's preferred language can't be determined or is not supported by the\n  1827\t  website. It also provides the fallback translation when a translation for a\n  1828\t  given literal doesn't exist for the user's preferred language.\n  1829\t\n  1830\tSee :ref:`how-django-discovers-language-preference` for more details.\n  1831\t\n  1832\t.. _list of language identifiers: http://www.i18nguy.com/unicode/language-identifiers.html\n  1833\t\n  1834\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_COOKIE_AGE\n  1835\t\n  1836\t``LANGUAGE_COOKIE_AGE``\n  1837\t-----------------------\n  1838\t\n  1839\tDefault: ``None`` (expires at browser close)\n  1840\t\n  1841\tThe age of the language cookie, in seconds.\n  1842\t\n  1843\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_COOKIE_DOMAIN\n  1844\t\n  1845\t``LANGUAGE_COOKIE_DOMAIN``\n  1846\t--------------------------\n  1847\t\n  1848\tDefault: ``None``\n  1849\t\n  1850\tThe domain to use for the language cookie. Set this to a string such as\n  1851\t``\"example.com\"`` for cross-domain cookies, or use ``None`` for a standard\n  1852\tdomain cookie.\n  1853\t\n  1854\tBe cautious when updating this setting on a production site. If you update\n  1855\tthis setting to enable cross-domain cookies on a site that previously used\n  1856\tstandard domain cookies, existing user cookies that have the old domain\n  1857\twill not be updated. This will result in site users being unable to switch\n  1858\tthe language as long as these cookies persist. The only safe and reliable\n  1859\toption to perform the switch is to change the language cookie name\n  1860\tpermanently (via the :setting:`LANGUAGE_COOKIE_NAME` setting) and to add\n  1861\ta middleware that copies the value from the old cookie to a new one and then\n  1862\tdeletes the old one.\n  1863\t\n  1864\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_COOKIE_HTTPONLY\n  1865\t\n  1866\t``LANGUAGE_COOKIE_HTTPONLY``\n  1867\t----------------------------\n  1868\t\n  1869\tDefault: ``False``\n  1870\t\n  1871\tWhether to use ``HttpOnly`` flag on the language cookie. If this is set to\n  1872\t``True``, client-side JavaScript will not be able to access the language\n  1873\tcookie.\n  1874\t\n  1875\tSee :setting:`SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY` for details on ``HttpOnly``.\n  1876\t\n  1877\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_COOKIE_NAME\n  1878\t\n  1879\t``LANGUAGE_COOKIE_NAME``\n  1880\t------------------------\n  1881\t\n  1882\tDefault: ``'django_language'``\n  1883\t\n  1884\tThe name of the cookie to use for the language cookie. This can be whatever\n  1885\tyou want (as long as it's different from the other cookie names in your\n  1886\tapplication). See :doc:`/topics/i18n/index`.\n  1887\t\n  1888\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_COOKIE_PATH\n  1889\t\n  1890\t``LANGUAGE_COOKIE_PATH``\n  1891\t------------------------\n  1892\t\n  1893\tDefault: ``'/'``\n  1894\t\n  1895\tThe path set on the language cookie. This should either match the URL path of your\n  1896\tDjango installation or be a parent of that path.\n  1897\t\n  1898\tThis is useful if you have multiple Django instances running under the same\n  1899\thostname. They can use different cookie paths and each instance will only see\n  1900\tits own language cookie.\n  1901\t\n  1902\tBe cautious when updating this setting on a production site. If you update this\n  1903\tsetting to use a deeper path than it previously used, existing user cookies that\n  1904\thave the old path will not be updated. This will result in site users being\n  1905\tunable to switch the language as long as these cookies persist. The only safe\n  1906\tand reliable option to perform the switch is to change the language cookie name\n  1907\tpermanently (via the :setting:`LANGUAGE_COOKIE_NAME` setting), and to add\n  1908\ta middleware that copies the value from the old cookie to a new one and then\n  1909\tdeletes the one.\n  1910\t\n  1911\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_COOKIE_SAMESITE\n  1912\t\n  1913\t``LANGUAGE_COOKIE_SAMESITE``\n  1914\t----------------------------\n  1915\t\n  1916\tDefault: ``None``\n  1917\t\n  1918\tThe value of the `SameSite`_ flag on the language cookie. This flag prevents the\n  1919\tcookie from being sent in cross-site requests.\n  1920\t\n  1921\tSee :setting:`SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE` for details about ``SameSite``.\n  1922\t\n  1923\t.. setting:: LANGUAGE_COOKIE_SECURE\n  1924\t\n  1925\t``LANGUAGE_COOKIE_SECURE``\n  1926\t--------------------------\n  1927\t\n  1928\tDefault: ``False``\n  1929\t\n  1930\tWhether to use a secure cookie for the language cookie. If this is set to\n  1931\t``True``, the cookie will be marked as \"secure\", which means browsers may\n  1932\tensure that the cookie is only sent under an HTTPS connection.\n  1933\t\n  1934\t.. setting:: LANGUAGES\n  1935\t\n  1936\t``LANGUAGES``\n  1937\t-------------\n  1938\t\n  1939\tDefault: A list of all available languages. This list is continually growing\n  1940\tand including a copy here would inevitably become rapidly out of date. You can\n  1941\tsee the current list of translated languages by looking in\n  1942\t:source:`django/conf/global_settings.py`.\n  1943\t\n  1944\tThe list is a list of two-tuples in the format\n  1945\t(:term:`language code<language code>`, ``language name``) -- for example,\n  1946\t``('ja', 'Japanese')``.\n  1947\tThis specifies which languages are available for language selection. See\n  1948\t:doc:`/topics/i18n/index`.\n  1949\t\n  1950\tGenerally, the default value should suffice.     1\t==================\n     2\tDatabase Functions\n     3\t==================\n     4\t\n     5\t.. module:: django.db.models.functions\n     6\t    :synopsis: Database Functions\n     7\t\n     8\tThe classes documented below provide a way for users to use functions provided\n     9\tby the underlying database as annotations, aggregations, or filters in Django.\n    10\tFunctions are also :doc:`expressions <expressions>`, so they can be used and\n    11\tcombined with other expressions like :ref:`aggregate functions\n    12\t<aggregation-functions>`.\n    13\t\n    14\tWe'll be using the following model in examples of each function::\n    15\t\n    16\t    class Author(models.Model):\n    17\t        name = models.CharField(max_length=50)\n    18\t        age = models.PositiveIntegerField(null=True, blank=True)\n    19\t        alias = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True)\n    20\t        goes_by = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True)\n    21\t\n    22\tWe don't usually recommend allowing ``null=True`` for ``CharField`` since this\n    23\tallows the field to have two \"empty values\", but it's important for the\n    24\t``Coalesce`` example below.\n    25\t\n    26\t.. _comparison-functions:\n    27\t\n    28\tComparison and conversion functions\n    29\t===================================\n    30\t\n    31\t``Cast``\n    32\t--------\n    33\t\n    34\t.. class:: Cast(expression, output_field)\n    35\t\n    36\tForces the result type of ``expression`` to be the one from ``output_field``.\n    37\t\n    38\tUsage example::\n    39\t\n    40\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n    41\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Cast\n    42\t    >>> Author.objects.create(age=25, name='Margaret Smith')\n    43\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n    44\t    ...    age_as_float=Cast('age', output_field=FloatField()),\n    45\t    ... ).get()\n    46\t    >>> print(author.age_as_float)\n    47\t    25.0\n    48\t\n    49\t``Coalesce``\n    50\t------------\n    51\t\n    52\t.. class:: Coalesce(*expressions, **extra)\n    53\t\n    54\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n    55\tfirst non-null value (note that an empty string is not considered a null\n    56\tvalue). Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and numbers\n    57\twill result in a database error.\n    58\t\n    59\tUsage examples::\n    60\t\n    61\t    >>> # Get a screen name from least to most public\n    62\t    >>> from django.db.models import Sum\n    63\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Coalesce\n    64\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', goes_by='Maggie')\n    65\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n    66\t    ...    screen_name=Coalesce('alias', 'goes_by', 'name')).get()\n    67\t    >>> print(author.screen_name)\n    68\t    Maggie\n    69\t\n    70\t    >>> # Prevent an aggregate Sum() from returning None\n    71\t    >>> # The aggregate default argument uses Coalesce() under the hood.\n    72\t    >>> aggregated = Author.objects.aggregate(\n    73\t    ...    combined_age=Sum('age'),\n    74\t    ...    combined_age_default=Sum('age', default=0),\n    75\t    ...    combined_age_coalesce=Coalesce(Sum('age'), 0),\n    76\t    ... )\n    77\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age'])\n    78\t    None\n    79\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age_default'])\n    80\t    0\n    81\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age_coalesce'])\n    82\t    0\n    83\t\n    84\t.. warning::\n    85\t\n    86\t    A Python value passed to ``Coalesce`` on MySQL may be converted to an\n    87\t    incorrect type unless explicitly cast to the correct database type:\n    88\t\n    89\t    >>> from django.db.models import DateTimeField\n    90\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Cast, Coalesce\n    91\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n    92\t    >>> now = timezone.now()\n    93\t    >>> Coalesce('updated', Cast(now, DateTimeField()))\n    94\t\n    95\t``Collate``\n    96\t-----------\n    97\t\n    98\t.. class:: Collate(expression, collation)\n    99\t\n   100\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n   101\t\n   102\tTakes an expression and a collation name to query against.\n   103\t\n   104\tFor example, to filter case-insensitively in SQLite::\n   105\t\n   106\t    >>> Author.objects.filter(name=Collate(Value('john'), 'nocase'))\n   107\t    <QuerySet [<Author: John>, <Author: john>]>\n   108\t\n   109\tIt can also be used when ordering, for example with PostgreSQL::\n   110\t\n   111\t    >>> Author.objects.order_by(Collate('name', 'et-x-icu'))\n   112\t    <QuerySet [<Author: Ursula>, <Author: Veronika>, <Author: Ülle>]>\n   113\t\n   114\t``Greatest``\n   115\t------------\n   116\t\n   117\t.. class:: Greatest(*expressions, **extra)\n   118\t\n   119\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n   120\tgreatest value. Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and\n   121\tnumbers will result in a database error.\n   122\t\n   123\tUsage example::\n   124\t\n   125\t    class Blog(models.Model):\n   126\t        body = models.TextField()\n   127\t        modified = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)\n   128\t\n   129\t    class Comment(models.Model):\n   130\t        body = models.TextField()\n   131\t        modified = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)\n   132\t        blog = models.ForeignKey(Blog, on_delete=models.CASCADE)\n   133\t\n   134\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Greatest\n   135\t    >>> blog = Blog.objects.create(body='Greatest is the best.')\n   136\t    >>> comment = Comment.objects.create(body='No, Least is better.', blog=blog)\n   137\t    >>> comments = Comment.objects.annotate(last_updated=Greatest('modified', 'blog__modified'))\n   138\t    >>> annotated_comment = comments.get()\n   139\t\n   140\t``annotated_comment.last_updated`` will be the most recent of ``blog.modified``\n   141\tand ``comment.modified``.\n   142\t\n   143\t.. warning::\n   144\t\n   145\t    The behavior of ``Greatest`` when one or more expression may be ``null``\n   146\t    varies between databases:\n   147\t\n   148\t    - PostgreSQL: ``Greatest`` will return the largest non-null expression,\n   149\t      or ``null`` if all expressions are ``null``.\n   150\t    - SQLite, Oracle, and MySQL: If any expression is ``null``, ``Greatest``\n   151\t      will return ``null``.\n   152\t\n   153\t    The PostgreSQL behavior can be emulated using ``Coalesce`` if you know\n   154\t    a sensible minimum value to provide as a default.\n   155\t\n   156\t``JSONObject``\n   157\t--------------\n   158\t\n   159\t.. class:: JSONObject(**fields)\n   160\t\n   161\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n   162\t\n   163\tTakes a list of key-value pairs and returns a JSON object containing those\n   164\tpairs.\n   165\t\n   166\tUsage example::\n   167\t\n   168\t    >>> from django.db.models import F\n   169\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import JSONObject, Lower\n   170\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', alias='msmith', age=25)\n   171\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(json_object=JSONObject(\n   172\t    ...     name=Lower('name'),\n   173\t    ...     alias='alias',\n   174\t    ...     age=F('age') * 2,\n   175\t    ... )).get()\n   176\t    >>> author.json_object\n   177\t    {'name': 'margaret smith', 'alias': 'msmith', 'age': 50}\n   178\t\n   179\t``Least``\n   180\t---------\n   181\t\n   182\t.. class:: Least(*expressions, **extra)\n   183\t\n   184\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n   185\tleast value. Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and numbers\n   186\twill result in a database error.\n   187\t\n   188\t.. warning::\n   189\t\n   190\t    The behavior of ``Least`` when one or more expression may be ``null``\n   191\t    varies between databases:\n   192\t\n   193\t    - PostgreSQL: ``Least`` will return the smallest non-null expression,\n   194\t      or ``null`` if all expressions are ``null``.\n   195\t    - SQLite, Oracle, and MySQL: If any expression is ``null``, ``Least``\n   196\t      will return ``null``.\n   197\t\n   198\t    The PostgreSQL behavior can be emulated using ``Coalesce`` if you know\n   199\t    a sensible maximum value to provide as a default.\n   200\t\n   201\t``NullIf``\n   202\t----------\n   203\t\n   204\t.. class:: NullIf(expression1, expression2)\n   205\t\n   206\tAccepts two expressions and returns ``None`` if they are equal, otherwise\n   207\treturns ``expression1``.\n   208\t\n   209\t.. admonition:: Caveats on Oracle\n   210\t\n   211\t    Due to an :ref:`Oracle convention<oracle-null-empty-strings>`, this\n   212\t    function returns the empty string instead of ``None`` when the expressions\n   213\t    are of type :class:`~django.db.models.CharField`.\n   214\t\n   215\t    Passing ``Value(None)`` to ``expression1`` is prohibited on Oracle since\n   216\t    Oracle doesn't accept ``NULL`` as the first argument.\n   217\t\n   218\t.. _date-functions:\n   219\t\n   220\tDate functions\n   221\t==============\n   222\t\n   223\tWe'll be using the following model in examples of each function::\n   224\t\n   225\t    class Experiment(models.Model):\n   226\t        start_datetime = models.DateTimeField()\n   227\t        start_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)\n   228\t        start_time = models.TimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   229\t        end_datetime = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   230\t        end_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)\n   231\t        end_time = models.TimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   232\t\n   233\t``Extract``\n   234\t-----------\n   235\t\n   236\t.. class:: Extract(expression, lookup_name=None, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   237\t\n   238\tExtracts a component of a date as a number.\n   239\t\n   240\tTakes an ``expression`` representing a ``DateField``, ``DateTimeField``,\n   241\t``TimeField``, or ``DurationField`` and a ``lookup_name``, and returns the part\n   242\tof the date referenced by ``lookup_name`` as an ``IntegerField``.\n   243\tDjango usually uses the databases' extract function, so you may use any\n   244\t``lookup_name`` that your database supports. A ``tzinfo`` subclass, usually\n   245\tprovided by ``pytz``, can be passed to extract a value in a specific timezone.\n   246\t\n   247\tGiven the datetime ``2015-06-15 23:30:01.000321+00:00``, the built-in\n   248\t``lookup_name``\\s return:\n   249\t\n   250\t* \"year\": 2015\n   251\t* \"iso_year\": 2015\n   252\t* \"quarter\": 2\n   253\t* \"month\": 6\n   254\t* \"day\": 15\n   255\t* \"week\": 25\n   256\t* \"week_day\": 2\n   257\t* \"iso_week_day\": 1\n   258\t* \"hour\": 23\n   259\t* \"minute\": 30\n   260\t* \"second\": 1\n   261\t\n   262\tIf a different timezone like ``Australia/Melbourne`` is active in Django, then\n   263\tthe datetime is converted to the timezone before the value is extracted. The\n   264\ttimezone offset for Melbourne in the example date above is +10:00. The values\n   265\treturned when this timezone is active will be the same as above except for:\n   266\t\n   267\t* \"day\": 16\n   268\t* \"week_day\": 3\n   269\t* \"iso_week_day\": 2\n   270\t* \"hour\": 9\n   271\t\n   272\t.. admonition:: ``week_day`` values\n   273\t\n   274\t    The ``week_day`` ``lookup_type`` is calculated differently from most\n   275\t    databases and from Python's standard functions. This function will return\n   276\t    ``1`` for Sunday, ``2`` for Monday, through ``7`` for Saturday.\n   277\t\n   278\t    The equivalent calculation in Python is::\n   279\t\n   280\t        >>> from datetime import datetime\n   281\t        >>> dt = datetime(2015, 6, 15)\n   282\t        >>> (dt.isoweekday() % 7) + 1\n   283\t        2\n   284\t\n   285\t.. admonition:: ``week`` values\n   286\t\n   287\t    The ``week`` ``lookup_type`` is calculated based on `ISO-8601\n   288\t    <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO-8601>`_, i.e.,\n   289\t    a week starts on a Monday. The first week of a year is the one that\n   290\t    contains the year's first Thursday, i.e. the first week has the majority\n   291\t    (four or more) of its days in the year. The value returned is in the range\n   292\t    1 to 52 or 53.\n   293\t\n   294\tEach ``lookup_name`` above has a corresponding ``Extract`` subclass (listed\n   295\tbelow) that should typically be used instead of the more verbose equivalent,\n   296\te.g. use ``ExtractYear(...)`` rather than ``Extract(..., lookup_name='year')``.\n   297\t\n   298\tUsage example::\n   299\t\n   300\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   301\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Extract\n   302\t    >>> start = datetime(2015, 6, 15)\n   303\t    >>> end = datetime(2015, 7, 2)\n   304\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   305\t    ...    start_datetime=start, start_date=start.date(),\n   306\t    ...    end_datetime=end, end_date=end.date())\n   307\t    >>> # Add the experiment start year as a field in the QuerySet.\n   308\t    >>> experiment = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   309\t    ...    start_year=Extract('start_datetime', 'year')).get()\n   310\t    >>> experiment.start_year\n   311\t    2015\n   312\t    >>> # How many experiments completed in the same year in which they started?\n   313\t    >>> Experiment.objects.filter(\n   314\t    ...    start_datetime__year=Extract('end_datetime', 'year')).count()\n   315\t    1\n   316\t\n   317\t``DateField`` extracts\n   318\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   319\t\n   320\t.. class:: ExtractYear(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   321\t\n   322\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'year'\n   323\t\n   324\t.. class:: ExtractIsoYear(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   325\t\n   326\t    Returns the ISO-8601 week-numbering year.\n   327\t\n   328\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'iso_year'\n   329\t\n   330\t.. class:: ExtractMonth(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   331\t\n   332\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'month'\n   333\t\n   334\t.. class:: ExtractDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   335\t\n   336\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'day'\n   337\t\n   338\t.. class:: ExtractWeekDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   339\t\n   340\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'week_day'\n   341\t\n   342\t.. class:: ExtractIsoWeekDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   343\t\n   344\t    Returns the ISO-8601 week day with day 1 being Monday and day 7 being\n   345\t    Sunday.\n   346\t\n   347\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'iso_week_day'\n   348\t\n   349\t.. class:: ExtractWeek(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   350\t\n   351\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'week'\n   352\t\n   353\t.. class:: ExtractQuarter(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   354\t\n   355\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'quarter'\n   356\t\n   357\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Extract('date_field', lookup_name)``. Each\n   358\tclass is also a ``Transform`` registered on ``DateField`` and ``DateTimeField``\n   359\tas ``__(lookup_name)``, e.g. ``__year``.\n   360\t\n   361\tSince ``DateField``\\s don't have a time component, only ``Extract`` subclasses\n   362\tthat deal with date-parts can be used with ``DateField``::\n   363\t\n   364\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   365\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   366\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   367\t    ...     ExtractDay, ExtractMonth, ExtractQuarter, ExtractWeek,\n   368\t    ...     ExtractIsoWeekDay, ExtractWeekDay, ExtractIsoYear, ExtractYear,\n   369\t    ... )\n   370\t    >>> start_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 23, 30, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   371\t    >>> end_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 16, 13, 11, 27, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   372\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   373\t    ...    start_datetime=start_2015, start_date=start_2015.date(),\n   374\t    ...    end_datetime=end_2015, end_date=end_2015.date())\n   375\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   376\t    ...     year=ExtractYear('start_date'),\n   377\t    ...     isoyear=ExtractIsoYear('start_date'),\n   378\t    ...     quarter=ExtractQuarter('start_date'),\n   379\t    ...     month=ExtractMonth('start_date'),\n   380\t    ...     week=ExtractWeek('start_date'),\n   381\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_date'),\n   382\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_date'),\n   383\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_date'),\n   384\t    ... ).values(\n   385\t    ...     'year', 'isoyear', 'quarter', 'month', 'week', 'day', 'weekday',\n   386\t    ...     'isoweekday',\n   387\t    ... ).get(end_date__year=ExtractYear('start_date'))\n   388\t    {'year': 2015, 'isoyear': 2015, 'quarter': 2, 'month': 6, 'week': 25,\n   389\t     'day': 15, 'weekday': 2, 'isoweekday': 1}\n   390\t\n   391\t``DateTimeField`` extracts\n   392\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   393\t\n   394\tIn addition to the following, all extracts for ``DateField`` listed above may\n   395\talso be used on ``DateTimeField``\\s .\n   396\t\n   397\t.. class:: ExtractHour(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   398\t\n   399\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'hour'\n   400\t\n   401\t.. class:: ExtractMinute(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   402\t\n   403\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'minute'\n   404\t\n   405\t.. class:: ExtractSecond(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   406\t\n   407\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'second'\n   408\t\n   409\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Extract('datetime_field', lookup_name)``.\n   410\tEach class is also a ``Transform`` registered on ``DateTimeField`` as\n   411\t``__(lookup_name)``, e.g. ``__minute``.\n   412\t\n   413\t``DateTimeField`` examples::\n   414\t\n   415\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   416\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   417\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   418\t    ...     ExtractDay, ExtractHour, ExtractMinute, ExtractMonth,\n   419\t    ...     ExtractQuarter, ExtractSecond, ExtractWeek, ExtractIsoWeekDay,\n   420\t    ...     ExtractWeekDay, ExtractIsoYear, ExtractYear,\n   421\t    ... )\n   422\t    >>> start_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 23, 30, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   423\t    >>> end_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 16, 13, 11, 27, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   424\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   425\t    ...    start_datetime=start_2015, start_date=start_2015.date(),\n   426\t    ...    end_datetime=end_2015, end_date=end_2015.date())\n   427\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   428\t    ...     year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   429\t    ...     isoyear=ExtractIsoYear('start_datetime'),\n   430\t    ...     quarter=ExtractQuarter('start_datetime'),\n   431\t    ...     month=ExtractMonth('start_datetime'),\n   432\t    ...     week=ExtractWeek('start_datetime'),\n   433\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_datetime'),\n   434\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   435\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   436\t    ...     hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime'),\n   437\t    ...     minute=ExtractMinute('start_datetime'),\n   438\t    ...     second=ExtractSecond('start_datetime'),\n   439\t    ... ).values(\n   440\t    ...     'year', 'isoyear', 'month', 'week', 'day',\n   441\t    ...     'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour', 'minute', 'second',\n   442\t    ... ).get(end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'))\n   443\t    {'year': 2015, 'isoyear': 2015, 'quarter': 2, 'month': 6, 'week': 25,\n   444\t     'day': 15, 'weekday': 2, 'isoweekday': 1, 'hour': 23, 'minute': 30,\n   445\t     'second': 1}\n   446\t\n   447\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True`` then datetimes are stored in the database\n   448\tin UTC. If a different timezone is active in Django, the datetime is converted\n   449\tto that timezone before the value is extracted. The example below converts to\n   450\tthe Melbourne timezone (UTC +10:00), which changes the day, weekday, and hour\n   451\tvalues that are returned::\n   452\t\n   453\t    >>> import pytz\n   454\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')  # UTC+10:00\n   455\t    >>> with timezone.override(melb):\n   456\t    ...    Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   457\t    ...        day=ExtractDay('start_datetime'),\n   458\t    ...        weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   459\t    ...        isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   460\t    ...        hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime'),\n   461\t    ...    ).values('day', 'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour').get(\n   462\t    ...        end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   463\t    ...    )\n   464\t    {'day': 16, 'weekday': 3, 'isoweekday': 2, 'hour': 9}\n   465\t\n   466\tExplicitly passing the timezone to the ``Extract`` function behaves in the same\n   467\tway, and takes priority over an active timezone::\n   468\t\n   469\t    >>> import pytz\n   470\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   471\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   472\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   473\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   474\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   475\t    ...     hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   476\t    ... ).values('day', 'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour').get(\n   477\t    ...     end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   478\t    ... )\n   479\t    {'day': 16, 'weekday': 3, 'isoweekday': 2, 'hour': 9}\n   480\t\n   481\t``Now``\n   482\t-------\n   483\t\n   484\t.. class:: Now()\n   485\t\n   486\tReturns the database server's current date and time when the query is executed,\n   487\ttypically using the SQL ``CURRENT_TIMESTAMP``.\n   488\t\n   489\tUsage example::\n   490\t\n   491\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Now\n   492\t    >>> Article.objects.filter(published__lte=Now())\n   493\t    <QuerySet [<Article: How to Django>]>\n   494\t\n   495\t.. admonition:: PostgreSQL considerations\n   496\t\n   497\t    On PostgreSQL, the SQL ``CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`` returns the time that the\n   498\t    current transaction started. Therefore for cross-database compatibility,\n   499\t    ``Now()`` uses ``STATEMENT_TIMESTAMP`` instead. If you need the transaction\n   500\t    timestamp, use :class:`django.contrib.postgres.functions.TransactionNow`.\n   501\t\n   502\t``Trunc``\n   503\t---------\n   504\t\n   505\t.. class:: Trunc(expression, kind, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   506\t\n   507\tTruncates a date up to a significant component.\n   508\t\n   509\tWhen you only care if something happened in a particular year, hour, or day,\n   510\tbut not the exact second, then ``Trunc`` (and its subclasses) can be useful to\n   511\tfilter or aggregate your data. For example, you can use ``Trunc`` to calculate\n   512\tthe number of sales per day.\n   513\t\n   514\t``Trunc`` takes a single ``expression``, representing a ``DateField``,\n   515\t``TimeField``, or ``DateTimeField``, a ``kind`` representing a date or time\n   516\tpart, and an ``output_field`` that's either ``DateTimeField()``,\n   517\t``TimeField()``, or ``DateField()``. It returns a datetime, date, or time\n   518\tdepending on ``output_field``, with fields up to ``kind`` set to their minimum\n   519\tvalue. If ``output_field`` is omitted, it will default to the ``output_field``\n   520\tof ``expression``. A ``tzinfo`` subclass, usually provided by ``pytz``, can be\n   521\tpassed to truncate a value in a specific timezone.\n   522\t\n   523\tThe ``is_dst`` parameter indicates whether or not ``pytz`` should interpret\n   524\tnonexistent and ambiguous datetimes in daylight saving time. By default (when\n   525\t``is_dst=None``), ``pytz`` raises an exception for such datetimes.\n   526\t\n   527\tGiven the datetime ``2015-06-15 14:30:50.000321+00:00``, the built-in ``kind``\\s\n   528\treturn:\n   529\t\n   530\t* \"year\": 2015-01-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   531\t* \"quarter\": 2015-04-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   532\t* \"month\": 2015-06-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   533\t* \"week\": 2015-06-15 00:00:00+00:00\n   534\t* \"day\": 2015-06-15 00:00:00+00:00\n   535\t* \"hour\": 2015-06-15 14:00:00+00:00\n   536\t* \"minute\": 2015-06-15 14:30:00+00:00\n   537\t* \"second\": 2015-06-15 14:30:50+00:00\n   538\t\n   539\tIf a different timezone like ``Australia/Melbourne`` is active in Django, then\n   540\tthe datetime is converted to the new timezone before the value is truncated.\n   541\tThe timezone offset for Melbourne in the example date above is +10:00. The\n   542\tvalues returned when this timezone is active will be:\n   543\t\n   544\t* \"year\": 2015-01-01 00:00:00+11:00\n   545\t* \"quarter\": 2015-04-01 00:00:00+10:00\n   546\t* \"month\": 2015-06-01 00:00:00+10:00\n   547\t* \"week\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   548\t* \"day\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   549\t* \"hour\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   550\t* \"minute\": 2015-06-16 00:30:00+10:00\n   551\t* \"second\": 2015-06-16 00:30:50+10:00\n   552\t\n   553\tThe year has an offset of +11:00 because the result transitioned into daylight\n   554\tsaving time.\n   555\t\n   556\tEach ``kind`` above has a corresponding ``Trunc`` subclass (listed below) that\n   557\tshould typically be used instead of the more verbose equivalent,\n   558\te.g. use ``TruncYear(...)`` rather than ``Trunc(..., kind='year')``.\n   559\t\n   560\tThe subclasses are all defined as transforms, but they aren't registered with\n   561\tany fields, because the lookup names are already reserved by the ``Extract``\n   562\tsubclasses.\n   563\t\n   564\tUsage example::\n   565\t\n   566\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   567\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count, DateTimeField\n   568\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Trunc\n   569\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321))\n   570\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 40, 2, 123))\n   571\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 12, 25, 10, 5, 27, 999))\n   572\t    >>> experiments_per_day = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   573\t    ...    start_day=Trunc('start_datetime', 'day', output_field=DateTimeField())\n   574\t    ... ).values('start_day').annotate(experiments=Count('id'))\n   575\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_day:\n   576\t    ...     print(exp['start_day'], exp['experiments'])\n   577\t    ...\n   578\t    2015-06-15 00:00:00 2\n   579\t    2015-12-25 00:00:00 1\n   580\t    >>> experiments = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   581\t    ...    start_day=Trunc('start_datetime', 'day', output_field=DateTimeField())\n   582\t    ... ).filter(start_day=datetime(2015, 6, 15))\n   583\t    >>> for exp in experiments:\n   584\t    ...     print(exp.start_datetime)\n   585\t    ...\n   586\t    2015-06-15 14:30:50.000321\n   587\t    2015-06-15 14:40:02.000123\n   588\t\n   589\t``DateField`` truncation\n   590\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   591\t\n   592\t.. class:: TruncYear(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   593\t\n   594\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'year'\n   595\t\n   596\t.. class:: TruncMonth(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   597\t\n   598\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'month'\n   599\t\n   600\t.. class:: TruncWeek(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   601\t\n   602\t    Truncates to midnight on the Monday of the week.\n   603\t\n   604\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'week'\n   605\t\n   606\t.. class:: TruncQuarter(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   607\t\n   608\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'quarter'\n   609\t\n   610\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('date_field', kind)``. They truncate\n   611\tall parts of the date up to ``kind`` which allows grouping or filtering dates\n   612\twith less precision. ``expression`` can have an ``output_field`` of either\n   613\t``DateField`` or ``DateTimeField``.\n   614\t\n   615\tSince ``DateField``\\s don't have a time component, only ``Trunc`` subclasses\n   616\tthat deal with date-parts can be used with ``DateField``::\n   617\t\n   618\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   619\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count\n   620\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import TruncMonth, TruncYear\n   621\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   622\t    >>> start1 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   623\t    >>> start2 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 40, 2, 123, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   624\t    >>> start3 = datetime(2015, 12, 31, 17, 5, 27, 999, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   625\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start1, start_date=start1.date())\n   626\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start2, start_date=start2.date())\n   627\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start3, start_date=start3.date())\n   628\t    >>> experiments_per_year = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   629\t    ...    year=TruncYear('start_date')).values('year').annotate(\n   630\t    ...    experiments=Count('id'))\n   631\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_year:\n   632\t    ...     print(exp['year'], exp['experiments'])\n   633\t    ...\n   634\t    2014-01-01 1\n   635\t    2015-01-01 2\n   636\t\n   637\t    >>> import pytz\n   638\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   639\t    >>> experiments_per_month = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   640\t    ...    month=TruncMonth('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb)).values('month').annotate(\n   641\t    ...    experiments=Count('id'))\n   642\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_month:\n   643\t    ...     print(exp['month'], exp['experiments'])\n   644\t    ...\n   645\t    2015-06-01 00:00:00+10:00 1\n   646\t    2016-01-01 00:00:00+11:00 1\n   647\t    2014-06-01 00:00:00+10:00 1\n   648\t\n   649\t``DateTimeField`` truncation\n   650\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   651\t\n   652\t.. class:: TruncDate(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   653\t\n   654\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'date'\n   655\t    .. attribute:: output_field = DateField()\n   656\t\n   657\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   658\t\n   659\t        The ``tzinfo`` parameter was added.\n   660\t\n   661\t``TruncDate`` casts ``expression`` to a date rather than using the built-in SQL\n   662\ttruncate function. It's also registered as a transform on  ``DateTimeField`` as\n   663\t``__date``.\n   664\t\n   665\t.. class:: TruncTime(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   666\t\n   667\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'time'\n   668\t    .. attribute:: output_field = TimeField()\n   669\t\n   670\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   671\t\n   672\t        The ``tzinfo`` parameter was added.\n   673\t\n   674\t``TruncTime`` casts ``expression`` to a time rather than using the built-in SQL\n   675\ttruncate function. It's also registered as a transform on ``DateTimeField`` as\n   676\t``__time``.\n   677\t\n   678\t.. class:: TruncDay(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   679\t\n   680\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'day'\n   681\t\n   682\t.. class:: TruncHour(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   683\t\n   684\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'hour'\n   685\t\n   686\t.. class:: TruncMinute(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   687\t\n   688\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'minute'\n   689\t\n   690\t.. class:: TruncSecond(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   691\t\n   692\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'second'\n   693\t\n   694\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('datetime_field', kind)``. They\n   695\ttruncate all parts of the date up to ``kind`` and allow grouping or filtering\n   696\tdatetimes with less precision. ``expression`` must have an ``output_field`` of\n   697\t``DateTimeField``.\n   698\t\n   699\tUsage example::\n   700\t\n   701\t    >>> from datetime import date, datetime\n   702\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count\n   703\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   704\t    ...     TruncDate, TruncDay, TruncHour, TruncMinute, TruncSecond,\n   705\t    ... )\n   706\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   707\t    >>> import pytz\n   708\t    >>> start1 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   709\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start1, start_date=start1.date())\n   710\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   711\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   712\t    ...     date=TruncDate('start_datetime'),\n   713\t    ...     day=TruncDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   714\t    ...     hour=TruncHour('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   715\t    ...     minute=TruncMinute('start_datetime'),\n   716\t    ...     second=TruncSecond('start_datetime'),\n   717\t    ... ).values('date', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', 'second').get()\n   718\t    {'date': datetime.date(2014, 6, 15),\n   719\t     'day': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 16, 0, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Australia/Melbourne' AEST+10:00:00 STD>),\n   720\t     'hour': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 16, 0, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Australia/Melbourne' AEST+10:00:00 STD>),\n   721\t     'minute': 'minute': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, tzinfo=<UTC>),\n   722\t     'second': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, tzinfo=<UTC>)\n   723\t    }\n   724\t\n   725\t``TimeField`` truncation\n   726\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   727\t\n   728\t.. class:: TruncHour(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   729\t    :noindex:\n   730\t\n   731\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'hour'\n   732\t\n   733\t.. class:: TruncMinute(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   734\t    :noindex:\n   735\t\n   736\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'minute'\n   737\t\n   738\t.. class:: TruncSecond(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   739\t    :noindex:\n   740\t\n   741\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'second'\n   742\t\n   743\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('time_field', kind)``. They truncate\n   744\tall parts of the time up to ``kind`` which allows grouping or filtering times\n   745\twith less precision. ``expression`` can have an ``output_field`` of either\n   746\t``TimeField`` or ``DateTimeField``.\n   747\t\n   748\tSince ``TimeField``\\s don't have a date component, only ``Trunc`` subclasses\n   749\tthat deal with time-parts can be used with ``TimeField``::\n   750\t\n   751\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   752\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count, TimeField\n   753\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import TruncHour\n   754\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   755\t    >>> start1 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   756\t    >>> start2 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, start_time=start1.time())\n   759\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start2, start_time=start2.time())\n   760\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start3, \"test\\narg2\")\n   142\t        buff, imports = OperationWriter(operation, indentation=0).serialize()\n   143\t        self.assertEqual(imports, {'import custom_migration_operations.operations'})\n   144\t        self.assertEqual(\n   145\t            buff,\n   146\t            \"custom_migration_operations.operations.ArgsOperation(\\n\"\n   147\t            \"    arg1='test\\\\n    arg1',\\n\"\n   148\t            \"    arg2='test\\\\narg2',\\n\"\n   149\t            \"),\"\n   150\t        )\n   151\t\n   152\t    def test_expand_args_signature(self):\n   153\t        operation =      )\n   175\t        buff, imports = OperationWriter(operation, indentation=0).serialize()\n   176\t        self.assertEqual(imports, {'import custom_migration_operations.operations'})\n   177\t        self.assertEqual(\n   178\t            buff,\n   179\t            'custom_migration_operations.operations.ExpandArgsOperation(\\n'\n   180\t            '    arg=[\\n'\n   181\t            '        custom_migration_operations.operations.KwargsOperation(\\n'\n   182\t            '            kwarg1=1,\\n'\n   183\t            '            kwarg2=2,\\n'\n   184\t            '        ),\\n'\n   185\t            '    ],\\n'\n   186\t            '),'\n   187\t        )\n   188\t\n   189\t\n   190\tclass WriterTests(SimpleTestCase):\n   191\t    \"\"\"\n   192\t    Tests the migration writer (makes migration files from Migration instances)\n   193\t    \"\"\"\n   194\t    class NestedEnum(enum.IntEnum):\n   195\t        A = 1\n   196\t        B = 2\n   197\t\n   198\t    class NestedChoices(models.TextChoices):\n   199\t        X = 'X', 'X value'\n   200\t        Y = 'Y', 'Y value'\n   201\t\n   202\t    def safe_exec(self, string, value=None):\n   203\t        d = {}\n   204\t        try:\n   205\t            exec(string, globals(), d)\n   206\t        except Exception as e:\n   207\t            if value:\n   208\t                self.fail(\"Could not exec %r (from value %r): %s\" % (string.strip(), value, e))\n   209\t            else:\n   210\t                self.fail(\"Could not exec %r: %s\" % (string.strip(), e))\n   211\t        return d\n   212\t\n   213\t    def serialize_round_trip(self, value):\n   214\t        string, imports = MigrationWriter.serialize(value)\n   215\t        return self.safe_exec(\"%s\\ntest_value_result = %s\" % (\"\\n\".join(imports), string), value)['test_value_result']\n   216\t\n   217\t    def assertSerializedEqual(self, value):\n   218\t        self.assertEqual(self.serialize_round_trip(value), value)\n   219\t\n   220\t    def assertSerializedResultEqual(self, value, target):\n   221\t        self.assertEqual(MigrationWriter.serialize(value), target)\n   222\t\n   223\t    def assertSerializedFieldEqual(self, value):\n   224\t        new_value = self.serialize_round_trip(value)\n   225\t        self.assertEqual(value.__class__, new_value.__class__)\n   226\t        self.assertEqual(value.max_length, new_value.max_length)\n   227\t        self.assertEqual(value.null, new_value.null)\n   228\t        self.assertEqual(value.unique, new_value.unique)\n   229\t\n   230\t    def test_serialize_numbers(self):\n   231\t        self.assertSerializedEqual(1)\n   232\t        self.assertSerializedEqual(1.2)\n   233\t        self.assertTrue(math.isinf(self.serialize_round_trip(float(\"inf\"))))\n   234\t        self.assertTrue(math.isinf(self.serialize_round_trip(float(\"-inf\"))))\n   235\t        self.assertTrue(math.isnan(self.serialize_round_trip(float(\"nan\"))))\n   236\t\n   237\t        self.assertSerializedEqual(decimal.Decimal('1.3'))\n   238\t
