The default value of this setting is an empty dictionary, but\n  2123\tthe default package name for migration modules is ``migrations``.\n  2124\t\n  2125\tExample::\n  2126\t\n  2127\t    {'blog': 'blog.db_migrations'}\n  2128\t\n  2129\tIn this case, migrations pertaining to the ``blog`` app will be contained in\n  2130\tthe ``blog.db_migrations`` package.\n  2131\t\n  2132\tIf you provide the ``app_label`` argument, :djadmin:`makemigrations` will\n  2133\tautomatically create the package if it doesn't already exist.\n  2134\t\n  2135\tWhen you supply ``None`` as a value for an app, Django will consider the app as\n  2136\tan app without migrations regardless of an existing ``migrations`` submodule.\n  2137\tThis can be used, for example, in a test settings file to skip migrations while\n  2138\ttesting (tables will still be created for the apps' models). To disable\n  2139\tmigrations for all apps during tests, you can set the\n  2140\t:setting:`MIGRATE <TEST_MIGRATE>` to ``False`` instead. If\n  2141\t``MIGRATION_MODULES`` is used in your general project settings, remember to use\n  2142\tthe :option:`migrate --run-syncdb` option if you want to create tables for the\n  2143\tapp.\n  2144\t\n  2145\t.. setting:: MONTH_DAY_FORMAT\n  2146\t\n  2147\t``MONTH_DAY_FORMAT``\n  2148\t--------------------\n  2149\t\n  2150\tDefault: ``'F j'``\n  2151\t\n  2152\tThe default formatting to use for date fields on Django admin change-list\n  2153\tpages -- and, possibly, by other parts of the system -- in cases when only the\n  2154\tmonth and day are displayed.\n  2155\t\n  2156\tFor example, when a Django admin change-list page is being filtered by a date\n  2157\tdrilldown, the header for a given day displays the day and month. Different\n  2158\tlocales have different formats. For example, U.S. English would say\n  2159\t\"January 1,\" whereas Spanish might say \"1 Enero.\"\n  2160\t\n  2161\tNote that if :setting:`USE_L10N` is set to ``True``, then the corresponding\n  2162\tlocale-dictated format has higher precedence and will be applied.\n  2163\t\n  2164\tSee :tfilter:`allowed date format strings <date>`. See also\n  2165\t:setting:`DATE_FORMAT`, :setting:`DATETIME_FORMAT`,\n  2166\t:setting:`TIME_FORMAT` and :setting:`YEAR_MONTH_FORMAT`.\n  2167\t\n  2168\t.. setting:: NUMBER_GROUPING\n  2169\t\n  2170\t``NUMBER_GROUPING``\n  2171\t-------------------\n  2172\t\n  2173\tDefault: ``0``\n  2174\t\n  2175\tNumber of digits grouped together on the integer part of a number.\n  2176\t\n  2177\tCommon use is to display a thousand separator. If this setting is ``0``, then\n  2178\tno grouping will be applied to the number. If this setting is greater than\n  2179\t``0``, then :setting:`THOUSAND_SEPARATOR` will be used as the separator between\n  2180\tthose groups.\n  2181\t\n  2182\tSome locales use non-uniform digit grouping, e.g. ``10,00,00,000`` in\n  2183\t``en_IN``. For this case, you can provide a sequence with the number of digit\n  2184\tgroup sizes to be applied. The first number defines the size of the group\n  2185\tpreceding the decimal delimiter, and each number that follows defines the size\n  2186\tof preceding groups. If the sequence is terminated with ``-1``, no further\n  2187\tgrouping is performed. If the sequence terminates with a ``0``, the last group\n  2188\tsize is used for the remainder of the number.\n  2189\t\n  2190\tExample tuple for ``en_IN``::\n  2191\t\n  2192\t    NUMBER_GROUPING = (3, 2, 0)\n  2193\t\n  2194\tNote that if :setting:`USE_L10N` is set to ``True``, then the locale-dictated\n  2195\tformat has higher precedence and will be applied instead.\n  2196\t\n  2197\tSee also :setting:`DECIMAL_SEPARATOR`, :setting:`THOUSAND_SEPARATOR` and\n  2198\t:setting:`USE_THOUSAND_SEPARATOR`.\n  2199\t\n  2200\t.. setting:: PREPEND_WWW\n  2201\t\n  2202\t``PREPEND_WWW``\n  2203\t---------------\n  2204\t\n  2205\tDefault: ``False``\n  2206\t\n  2207\tWhether to prepend the \"www.\" subdomain to URLs that don't have it. This is only\n  2208\tused if :class:`~django.middleware.common.CommonMiddleware` is installed\n  2209\t(see :doc:`/topics/http/middleware`). See also :setting:`APPEND_SLASH`.\n  2210\t\n  2211\t.. setting:: ROOT_URLCONF\n  2212\t\n  2213\t``ROOT_URLCONF``\n  2214\t----------------\n  2215\t\n  2216\tDefault: Not defined\n  2217\t\n  2218\tA string representing the full Python import path to your root URLconf, for\n  2219\texample ``\"mydjangoapps.urls\"``. Can be overridden on a per-request basis by\n  2220\tsetting the attribute ``urlconf`` on the incoming ``HttpRequest``\n  2221\tobject. See :ref:`how-django-processes-a-request` for details.\n  2222\t\n  2223\t.. setting:: SECRET_KEY\n  2224\t\n  2225\t``SECRET_KEY``\n  2226\t--------------\n  2227\t\n  2228\tDefault: ``''`` (Empty string)\n  2229\t\n  2230\tA secret key for a particular Django installation. This is used to provide\n  2231\t:doc:`cryptographic signing </topics/signing>`, and should be set to a unique,\n  2232\tunpredictable value.\n  2233\t\n  2234\t:djadmin:`django-admin startproject <startproject>` automatically adds a\n  2235\trandomly-generated ``SECRET_KEY`` to each new project.\n  2236\t\n  2237\tUses of the key shouldn't assume that it's text or bytes. Every use should go\n  2238\tthrough :func:`~django.utils.encoding.force_str` or\n  2239\t:func:`~django.utils.encoding.force_bytes` to convert it to the desired type.\n  2240\t\n  2241\tDjango will refuse to start if :setting:`SECRET_KEY` is not set.\n  2242\t\n  2243\t.. warning::\n  2244\t\n  2245\t    **Keep this value secret.**\n  2246\t\n  2247\t    Running Django with a known :setting:`SECRET_KEY` defeats many of Django's\n  2248\t    security protections, and can lead to privilege escalation and remote code\n  2249\t    execution vulnerabilities.\n  2250\t\n  2251\tThe secret key is used for:\n  2252\t\n  2253\t* All :doc:`sessions </topics/http/sessions>` if you are using\n  2254\t  any other session backend than ``django.contrib.sessions.backends.cache``,\n  2255\t  or are using the default\n  2256\t  :meth:`~django.contrib.auth.models.AbstractBaseUser.get_session_auth_hash()`.\n  2257\t* All :doc:`messages </ref/contrib/messages>` if you are using\n  2258\t  :class:`~django.contrib.messages.storage.cookie.CookieStorage` or\n  2259\t  :class:`~django.contrib.messages.storage.fallback.FallbackStorage`.\n  2260\t* All :class:`~django.contrib.auth.views.PasswordResetView` tokens.\n  2261\t* Any usage of :doc:`cryptographic signing </topics/signing>`, unless a\n  2262\t  different key is provided.\n  2263\t\n  2264\tIf you rotate your secret key, all of the above will be invalidated.\n  2265\tSecret keys are not used for passwords of users and key rotation will not\n  2266\taffect them.\n  2267\t\n  2268\t.. note::\n  2269\t\n  2270\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n  2271\t    startproject <startproject>` creates a unique ``SECRET_KEY`` for\n  2272\t    convenience.\n  2273\t\n  2274\t.. setting:: SECURE_CONTENT_TYPE_NOSNIFF\n  2275\t\n  2276\t``SECURE_CONTENT_TYPE_NOSNIFF``\n  2277\t-------------------------------\n  2278\t\n  2279\tDefault: ``True``\n  2280\t\n  2281\tIf ``True``, the :class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware`\n  2282\tsets the :ref:`x-content-type-options` header on all responses that do not\n  2283\talready have it.\n  2284\t\n  2285\t.. setting:: SECURE_CROSS_ORIGIN_OPENER_POLICY\n  2286\t\n  2287\t``SECURE_CROSS_ORIGIN_OPENER_POLICY``\n  2288\t-------------------------------------\n  2289\t\n  2290\t.. versionadded:: 4.0\n  2291\t\n  2292\tDefault: ``'same-origin'``\n  2293\t\n  2294\tUnless set to ``None``, the\n  2295\t:class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware` sets the\n  2296\t:ref:`cross-origin-opener-policy` header on all responses that do not already\n  2297\thave it to the value provided.\n  2298\t\n  2299\t.. setting:: SECURE_HSTS_INCLUDE_SUBDOMAINS\n  2300\t\n  2301\t``SECURE_HSTS_INCLUDE_SUBDOMAINS``\n  2302\t----------------------------------\n  2303\t\n  2304\tDefault: ``False``\n  2305\t\n  2306\tIf ``True``, the :class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware` adds\n  2307\tthe ``includeSubDomains`` directive to the :ref:`http-strict-transport-security`\n  2308\theader. It has no effect unless :setting:`SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS` is set to a\n  2309\tnon-zero value.\n  2310\t\n  2311\t.. warning::\n  2312\t    Setting this incorrectly can irreversibly (for the value of\n  2313\t    :setting:`SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS`) break your site. Read the\n  2314\t    :ref:`http-strict-transport-security` documentation first.\n  2315\t\n  2316\t.. setting:: SECURE_HSTS_PRELOAD\n  2317\t\n  2318\t``SECURE_HSTS_PRELOAD``\n  2319\t-----------------------\n  2320\t\n  2321\tDefault: ``False``\n  2322\t\n  2323\tIf ``True``, the :class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware` adds\n  2324\tthe ``preload`` directive to the :ref:`http-strict-transport-security`\n  2325\theader. It has no effect unless :setting:`SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS` is set to a\n  2326\tnon-zero value.\n  2327\t\n  2328\t.. setting:: SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS\n  2329\t\n  2330\t``SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS``\n  2331\t-----------------------\n  2332\t\n  2333\tDefault: ``0``\n  2334\t\n  2335\tIf set to a non-zero integer value, the\n  2336\t:class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware` sets the\n  2337\t:ref:`http-strict-transport-security` header on all responses that do not\n  2338\talready have it.\n  2339\t\n  2340\t.. warning::\n  2341\t    Setting this incorrectly can irreversibly (for some time) break your site.\n  2342\t    Read the :ref:`http-strict-transport-security` documentation first.\n  2343\t\n  2344\t.. setting:: SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER\n  2345\t\n  2346\t``SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER``\n  2347\t---------------------------\n  2348\t\n  2349\tDefault: ``None``\n  2350\t\n  2351\tA tuple representing a HTTP header/value combination that signifies a request\n  2352\tis secure. This controls the behavior of the request object's ``is_secure()``\n  2353\tmethod.\n  2354\t\n  2355\tBy default, ``is_secure()`` determines if a request is secure by confirming\n  2356\tthat a requested URL uses ``https://``. This method is important for Django's\n  2357\tCSRF protection, and it may be used by your own code or third-party apps.\n  2358\t\n  2359\tIf your Django app is behind a proxy, though, the proxy may be \"swallowing\"\n  2360\twhether the original request uses HTTPS or not. If there is a non-HTTPS\n  2361\tconnection between the proxy and Django then ``is_secure()`` would always\n  2362\treturn ``False`` -- even for requests that were made via HTTPS by the end user.\n  2363\tIn contrast, if there is an HTTPS connection between the proxy and Django then\n  2364\t``is_secure()`` would always return ``True`` -- even for requests that were\n  2365\tmade originally via HTTP.\n  2366\t\n  2367\tIn this situation, configure your proxy to set a custom HTTP header that tells\n  2368\tDjango whether the request came in via HTTPS, and set\n  2369\t``SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER`` so that Django knows what header to look for.\n  2370\t\n  2371\tSet a tuple with two elements -- the name of the header to look for and the\n  2372\trequired value. For example::\n  2373\t\n  2374\t    SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER = ('HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO', 'https')\n  2375\t\n  2376\tThis tells Django to trust the ``X-Forwarded-Proto`` header that comes from our\n  2377\tproxy, and any time its value is ``'https'``, then the request is guaranteed to\n  2378\tbe secure (i.e., it originally came in via HTTPS).\n  2379\t\n  2380\tYou should *only* set this setting if you control your proxy or have some other\n  2381\tguarantee that it sets/strips this header appropriately.\n  2382\t\n  2383\tNote that the header needs to be in the format as used by ``request.META`` --\n  2384\tall caps and likely starting with ``HTTP_``. (Remember, Django automatically\n  2385\tadds ``'HTTP_'`` to the start of x-header names before making the header\n  2386\tavailable in ``request.META``.)\n  2387\t\n  2388\t.. warning::\n  2389\t\n  2390\t    **Modifying this setting can compromise your site's security. Ensure you\n  2391\t    fully understand your setup before changing it.**\n  2392\t\n  2393\t    Make sure ALL of the following are true before setting this (assuming the\n  2394\t    values from the example above):\n  2395\t\n  2396\t    * Your Django app is behind a proxy.\n  2397\t    * Your proxy strips the ``X-Forwarded-Proto`` header from all incoming\n  2398\t      requests. In other words, if end users include that header in their\n  2399\t      requests, the proxy will discard it.\n  2400\t    * Your proxy sets the ``X-Forwarded-Proto`` header and sends it to Django,\n  2401\t      but only for requests that originally come in via HTTPS.\n  2402\t\n  2403\t    If any of those are not true, you should keep this setting set to ``None``\n  2404\t    and find another way of determining HTTPS, perhaps via custom middleware.\n  2405\t\n  2406\t.. setting:: SECURE_REDIRECT_EXEMPT\n  2407\t\n  2408\t``SECURE_REDIRECT_EXEMPT``\n  2409\t--------------------------\n  2410\t\n  2411\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  2412\t\n  2413\tIf a URL path matches a regular expression in this list, the request will not be\n  2414\tredirected to HTTPS. The\n  2415\t:class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware` strips leading slashes\n  2416\tfrom URL paths, so patterns shouldn't include them, e.g.\n  2417\t``SECURE_REDIRECT_EXEMPT = [r'^no-ssl/$', …]``. If\n  2418\t:setting:`SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT` is ``False``, this setting has no effect.\n  2419\t\n  2420\t.. setting:: SECURE_REFERRER_POLICY\n  2421\t\n  2422\t``SECURE_REFERRER_POLICY``\n  2423\t--------------------------\n  2424\t\n  2425\tDefault: ``'same-origin'``\n  2426\t\n  2427\tIf configured, the :class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware` sets\n  2428\tthe :ref:`referrer-policy` header on all responses that do not already have it\n  2429\tto the value provided.\n  2430\t\n  2431\t.. setting:: SECURE_SSL_HOST\n  2432\t\n  2433\t``SECURE_SSL_HOST``\n  2434\t-------------------\n  2435\t\n  2436\tDefault: ``None``\n  2437\t\n  2438\tIf a string (e.g. ``secure.example.com``), all SSL redirects will be directed\n  2439\tto this host rather than the originally-requested host\n  2440\t(e.g. ``www.example.com``). If :setting:`SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT` is ``False``, this\n  2441\tsetting has no effect.\n  2442\t\n  2443\t.. setting:: SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT\n  2444\t\n  2445\t``SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT``\n  2446\t-----------------------\n  2447\t\n  2448\tDefault: ``False``\n  2449\t\n  2450\tIf ``True``, the :class:`~django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware`\n  2451\t:ref:`redirects <ssl-redirect>` all non-HTTPS requests to HTTPS (except for\n  2452\tthose URLs matching a regular expression listed in\n  2453\t:setting:`SECURE_REDIRECT_EXEMPT`).\n  2454\t\n  2455\t.. note::\n  2456\t\n  2457\t   If turning this to ``True`` causes infinite redirects, it probably means\n  2458\t   your site is running behind a proxy and can't tell which requests are secure\n  2459\t   and which are not. Your proxy likely sets a header to indicate secure\n  2460\t   requests; you can correct the problem by finding out what that header is and\n  2461\t   configuring the :setting:`SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER` setting accordingly.\n  2462\t\n  2463\t.. setting:: SERIALIZATION_MODULES\n  2464\t\n  2465\t``SERIALIZATION_MODULES``\n  2466\t-------------------------\n  2467\t\n  2468\tDefault: Not defined\n  2469\t\n  2470\tA dictionary of modules containing serializer definitions (provided as\n  2471\tstrings), keyed by a string identifier for that serialization type. For\n  2472\texample, to define a YAML serializer, use::\n  2473\t\n  2474\t    SERIALIZATION_MODULES = {'yaml': 'path.to.yaml_serializer'}\n  2475\t\n  2476\t.. setting:: SERVER_EMAIL\n  2477\t\n  2478\t``SERVER_EMAIL``\n  2479\t----------------\n  2480\t\n  2481\tDefault: ``'root@localhost'``\n  2482\t\n  2483\tThe email address that error messages come from, such as those sent to\n  2484\t:setting:`ADMINS` and :setting:`MANAGERS`.\n  2485\t\n  2486\t.. admonition:: Why are my emails sent from a different address?\n  2487\t\n  2488\t    This address is used only for error messages. It is *not* the address that\n  2489\t    regular email messages sent with :meth:`~django.core.mail.send_mail()`\n  2490\t    come from; for that, see :setting:`DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL`.\n  2491\t\n  2492\t.. setting:: SHORT_DATE_FORMAT\n  2493\t\n  2494\t``SHORT_DATE_FORMAT``\n  2495\t---------------------\n  2496\t\n  2497\tDefault: ``'m/d/Y'`` (e.g. ``12/31/2003``)\n  2498\t\n  2499\tAn available formatting that can be used for displaying date fields on\n  2500\ttemplates. Note that if :setting:`USE_L10N` is set to ``True``, then the\n  2501\tcorresponding locale-dictated format has higher precedence and will be applied.\n  2502\tSee :tfilter:`allowed date format strings <date>`.\n  2503\t\n  2504\tSee also :setting:`DATE_FORMAT` and :setting:`SHORT_DATETIME_FORMAT`.\n  2505\t\n  2506\t.. setting:: SHORT_DATETIME_FORMAT\n  2507\t\n  2508\t``SHORT_DATETIME_FORMAT``\n  2509\t-------------------------\n  2510\t\n  2511\tDefault: ``'m/d/Y P'`` (e.g. ``12/31/2003 4 p.m.``)\n  2512\t\n  2513\tAn available formatting that can be used for displaying datetime fields on\n  2514\ttemplates. Note that if :setting:`USE_L10N` is set to ``True``, then the\n  2515\tcorresponding locale-dictated format has higher precedence and will be applied.\n  2516\tSee :tfilter:`allowed date format strings <date>`.\n  2517\t\n  2518\tSee also :setting:`DATE_FORMAT` and :setting:`SHORT_DATE_FORMAT`.\n  2519\t\n  2520\t.. setting:: SIGNING_BACKEND\n  2521\t\n  2522\t``SIGNING_BACKEND``\n  2523\t-------------------\n  2524\t\n  2525\tDefault: ``'django.core.signing.TimestampSigner'``\n  2526\t\n  2527\tThe backend used for signing cookies and other data.\n  2528\t\n  2529\tSee also the :doc:`/topics/signing` documentation.\n  2530\t\n  2531\t.. setting:: SILENCED_SYSTEM_CHECKS\n  2532\t\n  2533\t``SILENCED_SYSTEM_CHECKS``\n  2534\t--------------------------\n  2535\t\n  2536\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  2537\t\n  2538\tA list of identifiers of messages generated by the system check framework\n  2539\t(i.e. ``[\"models.W001\"]``) that you wish to permanently acknowledge and ignore.\n  2540\tSilenced checks will not be output to the console.\n  2541\t\n  2542\tSee also the :doc:`/ref/checks` documentation.\n  2543\t\n  2544\t.. setting:: TEMPLATES\n  2545\t\n  2546\t``TEMPLATES``\n  2547\t-------------\n  2548\t\n  2549\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  2550\t\n  2551\tA list containing the settings for all template engines to be used with\n  2552\tDjango. Each item of the list is a dictionary containing the options for an\n  2553\tindividual engine.\n  2554\t\n  2555\tHere's a setup that tells the Django template engine to load templates from the\n  2556\t``templates`` subdirectory inside each installed application::\n  2557\t\n  2558\t    TEMPLATES = [\n  2559\t        {\n  2560\t            'BACKEND': 'django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates',\n  2561\t            'APP_DIRS': True,\n  2562\t        },\n  2563\t    ]\n  2564\t\n  2565\tThe following options are available for all backends.\n  2566\t\n  2567\t.. setting:: TEMPLATES-BACKEND\n  2568\t\n  2569\t``BACKEND``\n  2570\t~~~~~~~~~~~\n  2571\t\n  2572\tDefault: Not defined\n  2573\t\n  2574\tThe template backend to use. The built-in template backends are:\n  2575\t\n  2576\t* ``'django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates'``\n  2577\t* ``'django.template.backends.jinja2.Jinja2'``\n  2578\t\n  2579\tYou can use a template backend that doesn't ship with Django by setting\n  2580\t``BACKEND`` to a fully-qualified path (i.e. ``'mypackage.whatever.Backend'``).\n  2581\t\n  2582\t.. setting:: TEMPLATES-NAME\n  2583\t\n  2584\t``NAME``\n  2585\t~~~~~~~~\n  2586\t\n  2587\tDefault: see below\n  2588\t\n  2589\tThe alias for this particular template engine. It's an identifier that allows\n  2590\tselecting an engine for rendering. Aliases must be unique across all\n  2591\tconfigured template engines.\n  2592\t\n  2593\tIt defaults to the name of the module defining the engine class, i.e. the\n  2594\tnext to last piece of :setting:`BACKEND <TEMPLATES-BACKEND>`, when it isn't\n  2595\tprovided. For example if the backend is ``'mypackage.whatever.Backend'`` then\n  2596\tits default name is ``'whatever'``.\n  2597\t\n  2598\t.. setting:: TEMPLATES-DIRS\n  2599\t\n  2600\t``DIRS``\n  2601\t~~~~~~~~\n  2602\t\n  2603\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  2604\t\n  2605\tDirectories where the engine should look for template source files, in search\n  2606\torder.\n  2607\t\n  2608\t.. setting:: TEMPLATES-APP_DIRS\n  2609\t\n  2610\t``APP_DIRS``\n  2611\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n  2612\t\n  2613\tDefault: ``False``\n  2614\t\n  2615\tWhether the engine should look for template source files inside installed\n  2616\tapplications.\n  2617\t\n  2618\t.. note::\n  2619\t\n  2620\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n  2621\t    startproject <startproject>` sets ``'APP_DIRS': True``.\n  2622\t\n  2623\t.. setting:: TEMPLATES-OPTIONS\n  2624\t\n  2625\t``OPTIONS``\n  2626\t~~~~~~~~~~~\n  2627\t\n  2628\tDefault: ``{}`` (Empty dict)\n  2629\t\n  2630\tExtra parameters to pass to the template backend. Available parameters vary\n  2631\tdepending on the template backend. See\n  2632\t:class:`~django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates` and\n  2633\t:class:`~django.template.backends.jinja2.Jinja2` for the options of the\n  2634\tbuilt-in backends.\n  2635\t\n  2636\t.. setting:: TEST_RUNNER\n  2637\t\n  2638\t``TEST_RUNNER``\n  2639\t---------------\n  2640\t\n  2641\tDefault: ``'django.test.runner.DiscoverRunner'``\n  2642\t\n  2643\tThe name of the class to use for starting the test suite. See the `list of\n  2727\ttime zones`_.\n  2728\t\n  2729\t.. note::\n  2730\t    Since Django was first released with the :setting:`TIME_ZONE` set to\n  2731\t    ``'America/Chicago'``, the global setting (used if nothing is defined in\n  2732\t    your project's ``settings.py``) remains ``'America/Chicago'`` for backwards\n  2733\t    compatibility. New project templates default to ``'UTC'``.\n  2734\t\n  2735\tNote that this isn't necessarily the time zone of the server. For example, one\n  2736\tserver may serve multiple Django-powered sites, each with a separate time zone\n  2737\tsetting.\n  2738\t\n  2739\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``False``, this is the time zone in which Django\n  2740\twill store all datetimes. When :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, this is the\n  2741\tdefault time zone that Django will use to display datetimes in templates and\n  2742\tto interpret datetimes entered in forms.\n  2743\t\n  2744\tOn Unix environments (where :func:`time.tzset` is implemented), Django sets the\n  2745\t``os.environ['TZ']`` variable to the time zone you specify in the\n  2746\t:setting:`TIME_ZONE` setting. Thus, all your views and models will\n  2747\tautomatically operate in this time zone. However, Django won't set the ``TZ``\n  2748\tenvironment variable if you're using the manual configuration option as\n  2749\tdescribed in :ref:`manually configuring settings\n  2750\t<settings-without-django-settings-module>`. If Django doesn't set the ``TZ``\n  2751\tenvironment variable, it's up to you to ensure your processes are running in\n  2752\tthe correct environment.\n  2753\t\n  2754\t.. note::\n  2755\t    Django cannot reliably use alternate time zones in a Windows environment.\n  2756\t    If you're running Django on Windows, :setting:`TIME_ZONE` must be set to\n  2757\t    match the system time zone.\n  2758\t\n  2759\t.. _list of time zones: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones\n  2760\t\n  2761\t.. setting:: USE_I18N\n  2762\t\n  2763\t``USE_I18N``\n  2764\t------------\n  2765\t\n  2766\tDefault: ``True``\n  2767\t\n  2768\tA boolean that specifies whether Django's translation system should be enabled.\n  2769\tThis provides a way to turn it off, for performance. For example::\n  1136\t\n  1137\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1138\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Radians\n  1139\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Radians)\n  1140\t    >>> # Get vectors whose radians are less than 1\n  1141\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__radians__lt=1, y__radians__lt=1)\n  1142\t\n  1143\t``Random``\n  1144\t----------\n  1145\t\n  1146\t.. class:: Random(**extra)\n  1147\t\n  1148\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n  1149\t\n  1150\tReturns a random value in the range ``0.0 ≤ x < 1.0``.\n  1151\t\n  1152\t``Round``\n  1153\t---------\n  1154\t\n  1155\t.. class:: Round(expression, precision=0, **extra)\n  1156\t\n  1157\tRounds a numeric field or expression to ``precision`` (must be an integer)\n  1158\tdecimal places. By default, it rounds to the nearest integer. Whether half\n  1159\tvalues are rounded up or down depends on the database.\n  1160\t\n  1161\tUsage example::\n  1162\t\n  1163\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Round\n  1164\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=5.4, y=-2.37)\n  1165\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_r=Round('x'), y_r=Round('y', precision=1)).get()\n  1166\t    >>> vector.x_r, vector.y_r\n  1167\t    (5.0, -2.4)\n  1168\t\n  1169\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1170\t\n  1171\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1172\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Round\n  1173\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Round)\n  1174\t    >>> # Get vectors whose round() is less than 20\n  1175\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__round__lt=20, y__round__lt=20)\n  1176\t\n  1177\t.. versionchanged:: 4.0\n  1178\t\n  1179\t    The ``precision`` argument was added.\n  1180\t\n  1181\t``Sign``\n  1182\t--------\n  1183\t\n  1184\t.. class:: Sign(expression, **extra)\n  1185\t\n  1186\tReturns the sign (-1, 0, 1) of a numeric field or expression.\n  1187\t\n  1188\tUsage example::\n  1189\t\n  1190\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sign\n  1191\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=5.4, y=-2.3)\n  1192\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_sign=Sign('x'), y_sign=Sign('y')).get()\n  1193\t    >>> vector.x_sign, vector.y_sign\n  1194\t    (1, -1)\n  1195\t\n  1196\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1197\t\n  1198\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1199\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sign\n  1200\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Sign)\n  1201\t    >>> # Get vectors whose signs of components are less than 0.\n  1202\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__sign__lt=0, y__sign__lt=0)\n  1203\t\n  1204\t``Sin``\n  1205\t-------\n  1206\t\n  1207\t.. class:: Sin(expression, **extra)\n  1208\t\n  1209\tReturns the sine of a numeric field or expression.\n  1210\t\n  1211\tUsage example::\n  1212\t\n  1213\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sin\n  1214\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=5.4, y=-2.3)\n  1215\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_sin=Sin('x'), y_sin=Sin('y')).get()\n  1216\t    >>> vector.x_sin, vector.y_sin\n  1217\t    (-0.7727644875559871, -0.7457052121767203)\n  1218\t\n  1219\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1220\t\n  1221\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1222\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sin\n  1223\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Sin)\n  1224\t    >>> # Get vectors whose sin() is less than 0\n  1225\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__sin__lt=0, y__sin__lt=0)\n  1226\t\n  1227\t``Sqrt``\n  1228\t--------\n  1229\t\n  1230\t.. class:: Sqrt(expression, **extra)\n  1231\t\n  1232\tReturns the square root of a nonnegative numeric field or expression.\n  1233\t\n  1234\tUsage example::\n  1235\t\n  1236\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sqrt\n  1237\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=4.0, y=12.0)\n  1238\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_sqrt=Sqrt('x'), y_sqrt=Sqrt('y')).get()\n  1239\t    >>> vector.x_sqrt, vector.y_sqrt\n  1240\t    (2.0, 3.46410)\n  1241\t\n  1242\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1243\t\n  1244\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1245\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sqrt\n  1246\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Sqrt)\n  1247\t    >>> # Get vectors whose sqrt() is less than 5\n  1248\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__sqrt__lt=5, y__sqrt__lt=5)\n  1249\t\n  1250\t``Tan``\n  1251\t-------\n  1252\t\n  1253\t.. class:: Tan(expression, **extra)\n  1254\t\n  1255\tReturns the tangent of a numeric field or expression.\n  1256\t\n  1257\tUsage example::\n  1258\t\n  1259\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Tan\n  1260\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=0, y=12)\n  1261\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_tan=Tan('x'), y_tan=Tan('y')).get()\n  1262\t    >>> vector.x_tan, vector.y_tan\n  1263\t    (0.0, -0.6358599286615808)\n  1264\t\n  1265\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1266\t\n  1267\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1268\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Tan\n  1269\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Tan)\n  1270\t    >>> # Get vectors whose tangent is less than 0\n  1271\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__tan__lt=0, y__tan__lt=0)\n  1272\t\n  1273\t.. _text-functions:\n  1274\t\n  1275\tText functions\n  1276\t==============\n  1277\t\n  1278\t``Chr``\n  1279\t-------\n  1280\t\n  1281\t.. class:: Chr(expression, **extra)\n  1282\t\n  1283\tAccepts a numeric field or expression and returns the text representation of\n  1284\tthe expression as a single character. It works the same as Python's :func:`chr`\n  1285\tfunction.\n  1286\t\n  1287\tLike :class:`Length`, it can be registered as a transform on ``IntegerField``.\n  1288\tThe default lookup name is ``chr``.\n  1289\t\n  1290\tUsage example::\n  1291\t\n  1292\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Chr\n  1293\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1294\t    >>> author = Author.objects.filter(name__startswith=Chr(ord('M'))).get()\n  1295\t    >>> print(author.name)\n  1296\t    Margaret Smith\n  1297\t\n  1298\t``Concat``\n  1299\t----------\n  1300\t\n  1301\t.. class:: Concat(*expressions, **extra)\n  1302\t\n  1303\tAccepts a list of at least two text fields or expressions and returns the\n  1304\tconcatenated text. Each argument must be of a text or char type. If you want\n  1305\tto concatenate a ``TextField()`` with a ``CharField()``, then be sure to tell\n  1306\tDjango that the ``output_field`` should be a ``TextField()``. Specifying an\n  1307\t``output_field`` is also required when concatenating a ``Value`` as in the\n  1308\texample below.\n  1309\t\n  1310\tThis function will never have a null result. On backends where a null argument\n  1311\tresults in the entire expression being null, Django will ensure that each null\n  1312\tpart is converted to an empty string first.\n  1313\t\n  1314\tUsage example::\n  1315\t\n  1316\t    >>> # Get the display name as \"name (goes_by)\"\n  1317\t    >>> from django.db.models import CharField, Value as V\n  1318\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Concat\n  1319\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', goes_by='Maggie')\n  1320\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n  1321\t    ...     screen_name=Concat(\n  1322\t    ...         'name', V(' ('), 'goes_by', V(')'),\n  1323\t    ...         output_field=CharField()\n  1324\t    ...     )\n  1325\t    ... ).get()\n  1326\t    >>> print(author.screen_name)\n  1327\t    Margaret Smith (Maggie)\n  1328\t\n  1329\t``Left``\n  1330\t--------\n  1331\t\n  1332\t.. class:: Left(expression, length, **extra)\n  1333\t\n  1334\tReturns the first ``length`` characters of the given text field or expression.\n  1335\t\n  1336\tUsage example::\n  1337\t\n  1338\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Left\n  1339\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1340\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(first_initial=Left('name', 1)).get()\n  1341\t    >>> print(author.first_initial)\n  1342\t    M\n  1343\t\n  1344\t``Length``\n  1345\t----------\n  1346\t\n  1347\t.. class:: Length(expression, **extra)\n  1348\t\n  1349\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the number of characters\n  1350\tthe value has. If the expression is null, then the length will also be null.\n  1351\t\n  1352\tUsage example::\n  1353\t\n  1354\t    >>> # Get the length of the name and goes_by fields\n  1355\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Length\n  1356\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1357\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n  1358\t    ...    name_length=Length('name'),\n  1359\t    ...    goes_by_length=Length('goes_by')).get()\n  1360\t    >>> print(author.name_length, author.goes_by_length)\n  1361\t    (14, None)\n  1362\t\n  1363\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1364\t\n  1365\t    >>> from django.db.models import CharField\n  1366\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Length\n  1367\t    >>> CharField.register_lookup(Length)\n  1368\t    >>> # Get authors whose name is longer than 7 characters\n  1369\t    >>> authors = Author.objects.filter(name__length__gt=7)\n  1370\t\n  1371\t``Lower``\n  1372\t---------\n  1373\t\n  1374\t.. class:: Lower(expression, **extra)\n  1375\t\n  1376\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the lowercase\n  1377\trepresentation.\n  1378\t\n  1379\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1380\t\n  1381\tUsage example::\n  1382\t\n  1383\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Lower\n  1384\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1385\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_lower=Lower('name')).get()\n  1386\t    >>> print(author.name_lower)\n  1387\t    margaret smith\n  1388\t\n  1389\t``LPad``\n  1390\t--------\n  1391\t\n  1392\t.. class:: LPad(expression, length, fill_text=Value(' '), **extra)\n  1393\t\n  1394\tReturns the value of the given text field or expression padded on the left side\n  1395\twith ``fill_text`` so that the resulting value is ``length`` characters long.\n  1396\tThe default ``fill_text`` is a space.\n  1397\t\n  1398\tUsage example::\n  1399\t\n  1400\t    >>> from django.db.models import Value\n  1401\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import LPad\n  1402\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='John', alias='j')\n  1403\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=LPad('name', 8, Value('abc')))\n  1404\t    1\n  1405\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(alias='j').name)\n  1406\t    abcaJohn\n  1407\t\n  1408\t``LTrim``\n  1409\t---------\n  1410\t\n  1411\t.. class:: LTrim(expression, **extra)\n  1412\t\n  1413\tSimilar to :class:`~django.db.models.functions.Trim`, but removes only leading\n  1414\tspaces.\n  1415\t\n  1416\t``MD5``\n  1417\t-------\n  1418\t\n  1419\t.. class:: MD5(expression, **extra)\n  1420\t\n  1421\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the MD5 hash of the\n  1422\tstring.\n  1423\t\n  1424\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1425\t\n  1426\tUsage example::\n  1427\t\n  1428\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import MD5\n  1429\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1430\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_md5=MD5('name')).get()\n  1431\t    >>> print(author.name_md5)\n  1432\t    749fb689816b2db85f5b169c2055b247\n  1433\t\n  1434\t``Ord``\n  1435\t-------\n  1436\t\n  1437\t.. class:: Ord(expression, **extra)\n  1438\t\n  1439\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the Unicode code point\n  1440\tvalue for the first character of that expression. It works similar to Python's\n  1441\t:func:`ord` function, but an exception isn't raised if the expression is more\n  1442\tthan one character long.\n  1443\t\n  1444\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1445\tThe default lookup name is ``ord``.\n  1446\t\n  1447\tUsage example::\n  1448\t\n  1449\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Ord\n  1450\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1451\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_code_point=Ord('name')).get()\n  1452\t    >>> print(author.name_code_point)\n  1453\t    77\n  1454\t\n  1455\t``Repeat``\n  1456\t----------\n  1457\t\n  1458\t.. class:: Repeat(expression, number, **extra)\n  1459\t\n  1460\tReturns the value of the given text field or expression repeated ``number``\n  1461\ttimes.\n  1462\t\n  1463\tUsage example::\n  1464\t\n  1465\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Repeat\n  1466\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='John', alias='j')\n  1467\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=Repeat('name', 3))\n  1468\t    1\n  1469\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(alias='j').name)\n  1470\t    JohnJohnJohn\n  1471\t\n  1472\t``Replace``\n  1473\t-----------\n  1474\t\n  1475\t.. class:: Replace(expression, text, replacement=Value(''), **extra)\n  1476\t\n  1477\tReplaces all occurrences of ``text`` with ``replacement`` in ``expression``.\n  1478\tThe default replacement text is the empty string. The arguments to the function\n  1479\tare case-sensitive.\n  1480\t\n  1481\tUsage example::\n  1482\t\n  1483\t    >>> from django.db.models import Value\n  1484\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Replace\n  1485\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Johnson')\n  1486\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1487\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=Replace('name', Value('Margaret'), Value('Margareth')))\n  1488\t    2\n  1489\t    >>> Author.objects.values('name')\n  1490\t    <QuerySet [{'name': 'Margareth Johnson'}, {'name': 'Margareth Smith'}]>\n  1491\t\n  1492\t``Reverse``\n  1493\t-----------\n  1494\t\n  1495\t.. class:: Reverse(expression, **extra)\n  1496\t\n  1497\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the characters of that\n  1498\texpression in reverse order.\n  1499\t\n  1500\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`. The\n  1501\tdefault lookup name is ``reverse``.\n  1502\t\n  1503\tUsage example::\n  1504\t\n  1505\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Reverse\n  1506\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1507\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(backward=Reverse('name')).get()\n  1508\t    >>> print(author.backward)\n  1509\t    htimS teragraM\n  1510\t\n  1511\t``Right``\n  1512\t---------\n  1513\t\n  1514\t.. class:: Right(expression, length, **extra)\n  1515\t\n  1516\tReturns the last ``length`` characters of the given text field or expression.\n  1517\t\n  1518\tUsage example::\n  1519\t\n  1520\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Right\n  1521\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1522\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(last_letter=Right('name', 1)).get()\n  1523\t    >>> print(author.last_letter)\n  1524\t    h\n  1525\t\n  1526\t``RPad``\n  1527\t--------\n  1528\t\n  1529\t.. class:: RPad(expression, length, fill_text=Value(' '), **extra)\n  1530\t\n  1531\tSimilar to :class:`~django.db.models.functions.LPad`, but pads on the right\n  1532\tside.\n  1533\t\n  1534\t``RTrim``\n  1535\t---------\n  1536\t\n  1537\t.. class:: RTrim(expression, **extra)\n  1538\t\n  1539\tSimilar to :class:`~django.db.models.functions.Trim`, but removes only trailing\n  1540\tspaces.\n  1541\t\n  1542\t``SHA1``, ``SHA224``, ``SHA256``, ``SHA384``, and ``SHA512``\n  1543\t------------------------------------------------------------\n  1544\t\n  1545\t.. class:: SHA1(expression, **extra)\n  1546\t.. class:: SHA224(expression, **extra)\n  1547\t.. class:: SHA256(expression, **extra)\n  1548\t.. class:: SHA384(expression, **extra)\n  1549\t.. class:: SHA512(expression, **extra)\n  1550\t\n  1551\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the particular hash of\n  1552\tthe string.\n  1553\t\n  1554\tThey can also be registered as transforms as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1555\t\n  1556\tUsage example::\n  1557\t\n  1558\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import SHA1\n  1559\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1560\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_sha1=SHA1('name')).get()\n  1561\t    >>> print(author.name_sha1)\n  1562\t    b87efd8a6c991c390be5a68e8a7945a7851c7e5c\n  1563\t\n  1564\t.. admonition:: PostgreSQL\n  1565\t\n  1566\t    The `pgcrypto extension <https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/\n  1567\t    pgcrypto.html>`_ must be installed. You can use the\n  1568\t    :class:`~django.contrib.postgres.operations.CryptoExtension` migration\n  1569\t    operation to install it.\n  1570\t\n  1571\t.. admonition:: Oracle\n  1572\t\n  1573\t    Oracle doesn't support the ``SHA224`` function.\n  1574\t\n  1575\t``StrIndex``\n  1576\t------------\n  1577\t\n  1578\t.. class:: StrIndex(string, substring, **extra)\n  1579\t\n  1580\tReturns a positive integer corresponding to the 1-indexed position of the first\n  1581\toccurrence of ``substring`` inside ``string``, or 0 if ``substring`` is not\n  1582\tfound.\n  1583\t\n  1584\tUsage example::\n  1585\t\n  1586\t    >>> from django.db.models import Value as V\n  1587\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import StrIndex\n  1588\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1589\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Smith, Margaret')\n  1590\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Jackson')\n  1591\t    >>> Author.objects.filter(name='Margaret Jackson').annotate(\n  1592\t    ...     smith_index=StrIndex('name', V('Smith'))\n  1593\t    ... ).get().smith_index\n  1594\t    0\n  1595\t    >>> authors = Author.objects.annotate(\n  1596\t    ...    smith_index=StrIndex('name', V('Smith'))\n  1597\t    ... ).filter(smith_index__gt=0)\n  1598\t    <QuerySet [<Author: Margaret Smith>, <Author: Smith, Margaret>]>\n  1599\t\n  1600\t.. warning::\n  1601\t\n  1602\t    In MySQL, a database table's :ref:`collation<mysql-collation>` determines\n  1603\t    whether string comparisons (such as the ``expression`` and ``substring`` of\n  1604\t    this function) are case-sensitive. Comparisons are case-insensitive by\n  1605\t    default.\n  1606\t\n  1607\t``Substr``\n  1608\t----------\n  1609\t\n  1610\t.. class:: Substr(expression, pos, length=None, **extra)\n  1611\t\n  1612\tReturns a substring of length ``length`` from the field or expression starting\n  1613\tat position ``pos``. The position is 1-indexed, so the position must be greater\n  1614\tthan 0. If ``length`` is ``None``, then the rest of the string will be returned.\n  1615\t\n  1616\tUsage example::\n  1617\t\n  1618\t    >>> # Set the alias to the first 5 characters of the name as lowercase\n  1619\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Lower, Substr\n  1620\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1621\t    >>> Author.objects.update(alias=Lower(Substr('name', 1, 5)))\n  1622\t    1\n  1623\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(name='Margaret Smith').alias)\n  1624\t    marga\n  1625\t\n  1626\t``Trim``\n  1627\t--------\n  1628\t\n  1629\t.. class:: Trim(expression, **extra)\n  1630\t\n  1631\tReturns the value of the given text field or expression with leading and\n  1632\ttrailing spaces removed.\n  1633\t\n  1634\tUsage example::\n  1635\t\n  1636\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Trim\n  1637\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='  John  ', alias='j')\n  1638\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=Trim('name'))\n  1639\t    1\n  1640\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(alias='j').name)\n  1641\t    John\n  1642\t\n  1643\t``Upper``\n  1644\t---------\n  1645\t\n  1646\t.. class:: Upper(expression, **extra)\n  1647\t\n  1648\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the uppercase\n  1649\trepresentation.\n  1650\t\n  1651\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1652\t\n  1653\tUsage example::\n  1654\t\n  1655\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Upper\n  1656\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1657\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_upper=Upper('name')).get()\n  1658\t    >>> print(author.name_upper)\n  1659\t    MARGARET SMITH\n  1660\t\n  1661\t.. _window-functions:\n  1662\t\n  1663\tWindow functions\n  1664\t================\n  1665\t\n  1666\tThere are a number of functions to use in a\n  1667\t:class:`~django.db.models.expressions.Window` expression for computing the rank\n  1668\tof elements or the :class:`Ntile` of some rows.\n  1669\t\n  1670\t``CumeDist``\n  1671\t------------\n  1672\t\n  1673\t.. class:: CumeDist(*expressions, **extra)\n  1674\t\n  1675\tCalculates the cumulative distribution of a value within a window or partition.\n  1676\tThe cumulative distribution is defined as the number of rows preceding or\n  1677\tpeered with the current row divided by the total number of rows in the frame.\n  1678\t\n  1679\t``DenseRank``\n  1680\t-------------\n  1681\t\n  1682\t.. class:: DenseRank(*expressions, **extra)\n  1683\t\n  1684\tEquivalent to :class:`Rank` but does not have gaps.\n  1685\t\n  1686\t``FirstValue``\n  1687\t--------------\n  1688\t\n  1689\t.. class:: FirstValue(expression, **extra)\n  1690\t\n  1691\tReturns the value evaluated at the row that's the first row of the window\n  1692\tframe, or ``None`` if no such value exists.\n  1693\t\n  1694\t``Lag``\n  1695\t-------\n  1696\t\n  1697\t.. class:: Lag(expression, offset=1, default=None, **extra)\n  1698\t\n  1699\tCalculates the value offset by ``offset``, and if no row exists there, returns\n  1700\t``default``.\n  1701\t\n  1702\t``default`` must have the same type as the ``expression``, however, this is\n  1703\tonly validated by the database and not in Python.\n  1704\t\n  1705\t.. admonition:: MariaDB and ``default``\n  1706\t\n  1707\t    MariaDB `doesn't support <https://jira.mariadb.org/browse/MDEV-12981>`_\n  1708\t    the ``default`` parameter.\n  1709\t\n  1710\t``LastValue``\n  1711\t-------------\n  1712\t\n  1713\t.. class:: LastValue(expression, **extra)\n  1714\t\n  1715\tComparable to :class:`FirstValue`, it calculates the last value in a given\n  1716\tframe clause.\n  1717\t\n  1718\t``Lead``\n  1719\t--------\n  1720\t\n  1721\t.. class:: Lead(expression, offset=1, default=None, **extra)\n  1722\t\n  1723\tCalculates the leading value in a given :ref:`frame <window-frames>`. Both\n  1724\t``offset`` and ``default`` are evaluated with respect to the current row.\n  1725\t\n  1726\t``default`` must have the same type as the ``expression``, however, this is\n  1727\tonly validated by the database and not in Python.\n  1728\t\n  1729\t.. admonition:: MariaDB and ``default``\n  1730\t\n  1731\t    MariaDB `doesn't support <https://jira.mariadb.org/browse/MDEV-12981>`_\n  1732\t    the ``default`` parameter.\n  1733\t\n  1734\t``NthValue``\n  1735\t------------\n  1736\t\n  1737\t.. class:: NthValue(expression, nth=1, **extra)\n  1738\t\n  1739\tComputes the row relative to the offset ``nth`` (must be a positive value)\n  1740\twithin the window. Returns ``None`` if no row exists.\n  1741\t\n  1742\tSome databases may handle a nonexistent nth-value differently. For example,\n  1743\tOracle returns an empty string rather than ``None`` for character-based\n  1744\texpressions. Django doesn't do any conversions in these cases.\n  1745\t\n  1746\t``Ntile``\n  1747\t---------\n  1748\t\n  1749\t.. class:: Ntile(num_buckets=1, **extra)\n  1750\t\n  1751\tCalculates a partition for each of the rows in the frame clause, distributing\n  1752\tnumbers as evenly as possible between 1 and ``num_buckets``. If the rows don't\n  1753\tdivide evenly into a number of buckets, one or more buckets will be represented\n  1754\tmore frequently.\n  1755\t\n  1756\t``PercentRank``\n  1757\t---------------\n  1758\t\n  1759\t.. class:: PercentRank(*expressions, **extra)\n  1760\t\n  1761\tComputes the percentile rank of the rows in the frame clause. This\n  1762\tcomputation is equivalent to evaluating::\n  1763\t\n  1764\t    (rank - 1) / (total rows - 1)\n  1765\t\n  1766\tThe following table explains the calculation for the percentile rank of a row:\n  1767\t\n  1768\t===== ===== ==== ============ ============\n  1769\tRow # Value Rank Calculation  Percent Rank\n  1770\t===== ===== ==== ============ ============\n  1771\t1     15    1    (1-1)/(7-1)  0.0000\n  1772\t2     20    2    (2-1)/(7-1)  0.1666\n  1773\t3     20    2    (2-1)/(7-1)  0.1666\n  1774\t4     20    2    (2-1)/(7-1)  0.1666\n  1775\t5     30    5    (5-1)/(7-1)  0.6666\n  1776\t6     30    5    (5-1)/(7-1)  0.6666\n  1777\t7     40    7    (7-1)/(7-1)  1.0000\n  1778\t===== ===== ==== ============ ============\n  1779\t\n  1780\t``Rank``\n  1781\t--------\n  1782\t\n  1783\t.. class:: Rank(*expressions, **extra)\n  1784\t\n  1785\tComparable to ``RowNumber``, this function ranks rows in the window. Set this to a string such as\n  3161\t``\"example.com\"`` for cross-domain cookies, or use ``None`` for a standard\n  3162\tdomain cookie.\n  3163\t\n  3164\tTo use cross-domain cookies with :setting:`CSRF_USE_SESSIONS`, you must include\n  3165\ta leading dot (e.g. ``\".example.com\"``) to accommodate the CSRF middleware's\n  3166\treferer checking.\n  3167\t\n  3168\tBe cautious when updating this setting on a production site. If you update\n  3169\tthis setting to enable cross-domain cookies on a site that previously used\n  3170\tstandard domain cookies, existing user cookies will be set to the old\n  3171\tdomain. This may result in them being unable to log in as long as these cookies\n  3172\tpersist.\n  3173\t\n  3174\tThis setting also affects cookies set by :mod:`django.contrib.messages`.\n  3175\t\n  3176\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY\n  3177\t\n  3178\t``SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY``\n  3179\t---------------------------\n  3180\t\n  3181\tDefault: ``True``\n  3182\t\n  3183\tWhether to use ``HttpOnly`` flag on the session cookie. If this is set to\n  3184\t``True``, client-side JavaScript will not be able to access the session\n  3185\tcookie.\n  3186\t\n  3187\tHttpOnly_ is a flag included in a Set-Cookie HTTP response header. It's part of\n  3188\tthe :rfc:`6265#section-4.1.2.6` standard for cookies and can be a useful way to\n  3189\tmitigate the risk of a client-side script accessing the protected cookie data.\n  3190\t\n  3191\tThis makes it less trivial for an attacker to escalate a cross-site scripting\n  3192\tvulnerability into full hijacking of a user's session. There aren't many good\n  3193\treasons for turning this off. Your code shouldn't read session cookies from\n  3194\tJavaScript.\n  3195\t\n  3196\t.. _HttpOnly: https://owasp.org/www-community/HttpOnly\n  3197\t\n  3198\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_NAME\n  3199\t\n  3200\t``SESSION_COOKIE_NAME``\n  3201\t-----------------------\n  3202\t\n  3203\tDefault: ``'sessionid'``\n  3204\t\n  3205\tThe name of the cookie to use for sessions. This flag prevents the\n  3230\tcookie from being sent in cross-site requests thus preventing CSRF attacks and\n  3231\tmaking some methods of stealing session cookie impossible.\n  3232\t\n  3233\tPossible values for the setting are:\n  3234\t\n  3235\t* ``'Strict'``: prevents the cookie from being sent by the browser to the\n  3236\t  target site in all cross-site browsing context, even when following a regular\n  3237\t  link.\n  3238\t\n  3239\t  For example, for a GitHub-like website this would mean that if a logged-in\n  3240\t  user follows a link to a private GitHub project posted on a corporate\n  3241\t  discussion forum or email, GitHub will not receive the session cookie and the\n  3242\t  user won't be able to access the project. A bank website, however, most\n  3243\t  likely doesn't want to allow any transactional pages to be linked from\n  3244\t  external sites so the ``'Strict'`` flag would be appropriate.\n  3245\t\n  3246\t* ``'Lax'`` (default): provides a balance between security and usability for\n  3247\t  websites that want to maintain user's logged-in session after the user\n  3248\t  arrives from an external link.\n  3249\t\n  3250\t  In the GitHub scenario, the session cookie would be allowed when following a\n  3251\t  regular link from an external website and be blocked in CSRF-prone request\n  3252\t  methods (e.g. ``POST``).\n  3253\t\n  3254\t* ``'None'`` (string): the session cookie will be sent with all same-site and\n  3255\t  cross-site requests.\n  3256\t\n  3257\t* ``False``: disables the flag.\n  3258\t\n  3259\t.. note::\n  3260\t\n  3261\t    Modern browsers provide a more secure default policy for the ``SameSite``\n  3262\t    flag and will assume ``Lax`` for cookies without an explicit value set.\n  3263\t\n  3264\t.. _SameSite: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Set-Cookie/SameSite\n  3265\t\n  3266\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE\n  3267\t\n  3268\t``SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE``\n  3269\t-------------------------\n  3270\t\n  3271\tDefault: ``False``\n  3272\t\n  3273\tWhether to use a secure cookie for the session cookie. See the how-to on\n  3388\t:doc:`managing static files</howto/static-files/index>` for more details about\n  3389\tusage.\n  3390\t\n  3391\t.. warning::\n  3392\t\n  3393\t    This should be an initially empty destination directory for collecting\n  3394\t    your static files from their permanent locations into one directory for\n  3395\t    ease of deployment; it is **not** a place to store your static files\n  3396\t    permanently. You should do that in directories that will be found by\n  3397\t    :doc:`staticfiles</ref/contrib/staticfiles>`’s\n  3398\t    :setting:`finders<STATICFILES_FINDERS>`, which by default, are\n  3399\t    ``'static/'`` app sub-directories and any directories you include in\n  3400\t    :setting:`STATICFILES_DIRS`).\n  3401\t\n  3402\t.. setting:: STATIC_URL\n  3403\t\n  3404\t``STATIC_URL``\n  3405\t--------------\n  3406\t\n  3407\tDefault: ``None``\n  3408\t\n  3409\tURL to use when referring to static files located in :setting:`STATIC_ROOT`.\n  3410\t\n  3411\tExample: ``\"static/\"`` or ``\"http://static.example.com/\"``\n  3412\t\n  3413\tIf not ``None``, this will be used as the base path for\n  3414\t:ref:`asset definitions<form-asset-paths>` (the ``Media`` class) and the\n  3415\t:doc:`staticfiles app</ref/contrib/staticfiles>`.\n  3416\t\n  3417\tIt must end in a slash if set to a non-empty value.\n  3418\t\n  3419\tYou may need to :ref:`configure these files to be served in development\n  3420\t<serving-static-files-in-development>` and will definitely need to do so\n  3421\t:doc:`in production </howto/static-files/deployment>`.\n  3422\t\n  3423\t.. note::\n  3424\t\n  3425\t    If :setting:`STATIC_URL` is a relative path, then it will be prefixed by\n  3426\t    the server-provided value of ``SCRIPT_NAME`` (or ``/`` if not set). This\n  3427\t    makes it easier to serve a Django application in a subpath without adding\n  3428\t    an extra configuration to the settings.\n  3429\t\n  3430\t.. setting:: STATICFILES_DIRS\n  3431\t\n  3432\t``STATICFILES_DIRS``\n  3433\t--------------------\n  3434\t\n  3435\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  3436\t\n  3437\tThis setting defines the additional locations the staticfiles app will traverse\n  3438\tif the ``FileSystemFinder`` finder is enabled, e.g. if you use the\n  3439\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` or :djadmin:`findstatic` management command or use the\n  3440\tstatic file serving view.\n  3441\t\n  3442\tThis should be set to a list of strings that contain full paths to\n  3443\tyour additional files directory(ies) e.g.::\n  3444\t\n  3445\t    STATICFILES_DIRS = [\n  3446\t        \"/home/special.polls.com/polls/static\",\n  3447\t        \"/home/polls.com/polls/static\",\n  3448\t        \"/opt/webfiles/common\",\n  3449\t    ]\n  3450\t\n  3451\tNote that these paths should use Unix-style forward slashes, even on Windows\n  3452\t(e.g. ``\"C:/Users/user/mysite/extra_static_content\"``).\n  3453\t\n  3454\t.. _staticfiles-dirs-prefixes:\n  3455\t\n  3456\tPrefixes (optional)\n  3457\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n  3458\t\n  3459\tIn case you want to refer to files in one of the locations with an additional\n  3460\tnamespace, you can **optionally** provide a prefix as ``(prefix, path)``\n  3461\ttuples, e.g.::\n  3462\t\n  3463\t    STATICFILES_DIRS = [\n  3464\t        # ...\n  3465\t        (\"downloads\", \"/opt/webfiles/stats\"),\n  3466\t    ]\n  3467\t\n  3468\tFor example, assuming you have :setting:`STATIC_URL` set to ``'static/'``, the\n  3469\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` management command would collect the \"stats\" files\n  3470\tin a ``'downloads'`` subdirectory of :setting:`STATIC_ROOT`.\n  3471\t\n  3472\tThis would allow you to refer to the local file\n  3473\t``'/opt/webfiles/stats/polls_20101022.tar.gz'`` with\n  3474\t``'/static/downloads/polls_20101022.tar.gz'`` in your templates, e.g.:\n  3475\t\n  3476\t.. code-block:: html+django\n  3477\t\n  3478\t    <a href=\"{% static 'downloads/polls_20101022.tar.gz' %}\">\n  3479\t\n  3480\t.. setting:: STATICFILES_STORAGE\n  3481\t\n  3482\t``STATICFILES_STORAGE``\n  3483\t-----------------------\n  3484\t\n  3485\tDefault: ``'django.contrib.staticfiles.storage.StaticFilesStorage'``\n  3486\t\n  3487\tThe file storage engine to use when collecting static files with the\n  3488\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` management command.\n  3489\t\n  3490\tA ready-to-use instance of the storage backend defined in this setting\n  3491\tcan be found at ``django.contrib.staticfiles.storage.staticfiles_storage``.\n  3492\t\n  3493\tFor an example, see :ref:`staticfiles-from-cdn`.\n  3494\t\n  3495\t.. setting:: STATICFILES_FINDERS\n  3496\t\n  3497\t``STATICFILES_FINDERS``\n  3498\t-----------------------\n  3499\t\n  3500\tDefault::\n  3501\t\n  3502\t    [\n  3503\t        'django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.FileSystemFinder',\n  3504\t        'django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder',\n  3505\t    ]\n  3506\t\n  3507\tThe list of finder backends that know how to find static files in\n  3508\tvarious locations.\n  3509\t\n  3510\tThe default will find files stored in the :setting:`STATICFILES_DIRS` setting\n  3511\t(using ``django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.FileSystemFinder``) and in a\n  3512\t``static`` subdirectory of each app (using\n  3513\t``django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder``). If multiple\n  3514\tfiles with the same name are present, the first file that is found will be\n  3515\tused.\n  3516\t\n  3517\tOne finder is disabled by default:\n  3518\t``django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.DefaultStorageFinder``. If added to\n  3519\tyour :setting:`STATICFILES_FINDERS` setting, it will look for static files in\n  3520\tthe default file storage as defined by the :setting:`DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE`\n  3521\tsetting.\n  3522\t\n  3523\t.. note::\n  3524\t\n  3525\t    When using the ``AppDirectoriesFinder`` finder, make sure your apps\n  3526\t    can be found by staticfiles by adding the app to the\n  3527\t    :setting:`INSTALLED_APPS` setting of your site.\n  3528\t\n  3529\tStatic file finders are currently considered a private interface, and this\n  3530\tinterface is thus undocumented.\n  3531\t\n  3532\tCore Settings Topical Index\n  3533\t===========================\n  3534\t\n  3535\tCache\n  3536\t-----\n  3537\t* :setting:`CACHES`\n  3538\t* instead\\n  of ``[]``, ``[]``, and ``''`` respectively.\\n\\n* The ``USE_L10N`` setting will be removed.\\n\\n* Support for pytz will be removed. Django will exclusively use\\n  :mod:`zoneinfo` (from the Python standard library) for timezone support.\\n\\n* The ``is_dst`` argument to ``Trunc()`` and its subclasses\\n  (``TruncYear``, ``TruncMonth``, etc.) will be removed. django.utils.timezone.activate()\\n    to set the timezone on entry and restores the previously active timezone\\n    on exit.\\n\\n    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a ``tzinfo`` Conversely, if ``is_dst=False`` the time\n    would be interpreted as 2:30 standard time (equivalent to 3:30 local time).\n\n    The ``is_dst`` parameter has no effect when using non-``pytz`` timezone\n    implementations.\n\n.. function:: make_naive(value, timezone=None)\n","new_text":"    :class:`~datetime.datetime`. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:3","agent_id":"ab68fbeca","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.ReadFile:34","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"   585\t   None of this is true in a time zone aware environment::\n   586\t\n   587\t       >>> import datetime\n   588\t       >>> import pytz\n   589\t       >>> paris_tz = pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Paris\")\n   590\t       >>> new_york_tz = pytz.timezone(\"America/New_York\")\n   591\t       >>> paris = paris_tz.localize(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30))\n   592\t       # This is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   593\t       >>> new_york = new_york_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(new_york_tz))\n   594\t       >>> paris == new_york, paris.date() == new_york.date()\n   595\t       (True, False)\n   596\t       >>> paris - new_york, paris.date() - new_york.date()\n   597\t       (datetime.timedelta(0), datetime.timedelta(1))\n   598\t       >>> paris\n   599\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   600\t       >>> new_york\n   601\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 2, 19, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/New_York' EST-1 day, 19:00:00 STD>)\n   602\t\n   603\t   As this example shows, the same datetime has a different date, depending on\n   604\t   the time zone in which it is represented. But the real problem is more\n   605\t   fundamental.\n   606\t\n   607\t   A datetime represents a **point in time**. It's absolute: it doesn't depend\n   608\t   on anything. On the contrary, a date is a **calendaring concept**. It's a\n   609\t   period of time whose bounds depend on the time zone in which the date is\n   610\t   considered. As you can see, these two concepts are fundamentally different,\n   611\t   and converting a datetime to a date isn't a deterministic operation.\n   612\t\n   613\t   What does this mean in practice?\n   614\t\n   615\t   Generally, you should avoid converting a :class:`~datetime.datetime` to\n   616\t   :class:`~datetime.date`. For instance, you can use the :tfilter:`date`\n   617\t   template filter to only show the date part of a datetime. This filter will\n   618\t   convert the datetime into the current time zone before formatting it,\n   619\t   ensuring the results appear correctly.\n   620\t\n   621\t   If you really need to do the conversion yourself, you must ensure the\n   622\t   datetime is converted to the appropriate time zone first. Usually, this\n   623\t   will be the current timezone::\n   624\t\n   625\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   626\t       >>> timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(\"Asia/Singapore\"))\n   627\t       # For this example, we set the time zone to Singapore, but here's how\n   628\t       # you would obtain the current time zone in the general case.\n   629\t       >>> current_tz = timezone.get_current_timezone()\n   630\t       # Again, this is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   631\t       >>> local = current_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(current_tz))\n   632\t       >>> local\n   633\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 8, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Asia/Singapore' SGT+8:00:00 STD>)\n   634\t       >>> local.date()\n   635\t       datetime.date(2012, 3, 3)\n   636\t\n   637\t#. **I get an error** \"``Are time zone definitions for your database\n   638\t   installed?``\"\n   639\t\n   640\t   If you are using MySQL, see the :ref:`mysql-time-zone-definitions` section\n   641\t   of the MySQL notes for instructions on loading time zone definitions.\n   642\t\n   643\tUsage\n   644\t-----\n   645\t\n   646\t#. **I have a string** ``\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\"`` **and I know it's in the**\n   647\t   ``\"Europe/Helsinki\"`` **time zone. How do I turn that into an aware\n   648\t   datetime?**\n   649\t\n   650\t   This is exactly what pytz_ is for.\n   651\t\n   652\t       >>> from django.utils.dateparse import parse_datetime\n   653\t       >>> naive = parse_datetime(\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\")\n   654\t       >>> import pytz\n   655\t       >>> pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Helsinki\").localize(naive, is_dst=None)\n   656\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 21, 10, 28, 45, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Helsinki' EET+2:00:00 STD>)\n   657\t\n   658\t   Note that ``localize`` is a pytz extension to the :class:`~datetime.tzinfo`\n   659\t   API. Also, you may want to catch ``pytz.InvalidTimeError``. The\n   660\t   documentation of pytz contains `more examples`_. You should review it\n   661\t   before attempting to manipulate aware datetimes.\n   662\t\n   663\t#. **How can I obtain the local time in the current time zone?**\n   664\t\n   665\t   Well, the first question is, do you really need to?\n   666\t\n   667\t   You should only use local time when you're interacting with humans, and the\n   668\t   template layer provides :ref:`filters and tags <time-zones-in-templates>`\n   669\t   to convert datetimes to the time zone of your choice.\n   670\t\n   671\t   Furthermore, Python knows how to compare aware datetimes, taking into\n   672\t   account UTC offsets when necessary. It's much easier (and possibly faster)\n   673\t   to write all your model and view code in UTC. 20, 30, tzinfo=EAT))\\n        Event.objects.create(dt=datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 3, 20, 40, tzinfo=EAT))\\n        result = Event.objects.all().aggregate(Min('dt'), Max('dt'))\\n        self.assertEqual(result, {\\n            'dt__min': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 3, 20, 40, tzinfo=EAT),\\n            'dt__max': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 23, 20, 20, tzinfo=EAT),\\n        })\\n\\n    def test_query_annotation(self):\\n        # Only min and max make sense for datetimes.\\n        morning = Session.objects.create(name='morning')\\n        afternoon = Session.objects.create(name='afternoon')\\n        SessionEvent.objects.create(dt=datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 23, 20, 20, tzinfo=EAT), session=afternoon)\\n        SessionEvent.objects.create(dt=datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, tzinfo=EAT), session=afternoon)\\n        SessionEvent.objects.create(dt=datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 3, 20, 40, tzinfo=EAT), session=morning)\\n        morning_min_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 3, 20, 40, tzinfo=EAT)\\n        afternoon_min_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, tzinfo=EAT)\\n        self.assertQuerysetEqual(\\n            Session.objects.annotate(dt=Min('events__dt')).order_by('dt'),\\n            [morning_min_dt, afternoon_min_dt],\\n            transform=lambda d: d.dt,\\n        )\\n        self.assertQuerysetEqual(\\n            Session.objects.annotate(dt=Min('events__dt')).filter(dt__lt=afternoon_min_dt),\\n            [morning_min_dt],\\n            transform=lambda d: d.dt,\\n        )\\n        self.assertQuerysetEqual(\\n            Session.objects.annotate(dt=Min('events__dt')).filter(dt__gte=afternoon_min_dt),\\n            [afternoon_min_dt],\\n            transform=lambda d: d.dt,\\n        )\\n\\n    @skipUnlessDBFeature('has_zoneinfo_database')\\n    def test_query_datetimes(self):\\n        Event.objects.create(dt=datetime.datetime(2011, 1, 1, 1, timedelta(hours=2))\\n\\n\\n@override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi', USE_TZ=True)\\nclass ForcedTimeZoneDatabaseTests(TransactionTestCase):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Test the TIME_ZONE database configuration parameter.\\n\\n    Since this involves reading and writing to the same database through two\\n    connections, this is a TransactionTestCase.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\n    available_apps = ['timezones']\\n\\n    @classmethod\\n    def setUpClass(cls):\\n        # @skipIfDBFeature and @skipUnlessDBFeature cannot be chained. The\\n        # outermost takes precedence. Handle skipping manually instead.\\n        if connection.features.supports_timezones:\\n            raise SkipTest(\\\"Database has feature(s) supports_timezones\\\")\\n        if not connection.features.test_db_allows_multiple_connections:\\n            raise SkipTest(\\\"Database doesn't support feature(s): test_db_allows_multiple_connections\\\")\\n\\n        super().setUpClass()\\n\\n    def test_read_datetime(self):\\n        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        Event.objects.create(dt=fake_dt)\\n\\n        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\\n            event = Event.objects.get()\\n            dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        self.assertEqual(event.dt, dt)\\n\\n    def test_write_datetime(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\\n            Event.objects.create(dt=dt)\\n\\n        event = Event.objects.get()\\n        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        self.assertEqual(event.dt, fake_dt)\\n\\n\\n@override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi')\\nclass SerializationTests(SimpleTestCase):\\n\\n    # Backend-specific notes:\\n    # - JSON supports only milliseconds, microseconds will be truncated.\\n    # - PyYAML dumps the UTC offset correctly for timezone-aware datetimes.\\n    #   When PyYAML < 5.3 loads this representation, it subtracts the offset\\n    #   and returns a naive datetime object in UTC. PyYAML 5.3+ loads timezones\\n    #   correctly.\\n    # Tests are adapted to take these quirks into account.\\n\\n    def assert_python_contains_datetime(self, objects, dt):\\n        self.assertEqual(objects[0]['fields']['dt'], dt)\\n\\n    def assert_json_contains_datetime(self, json, dt):\\n        self.assertIn('\\\"fields\\\": {\\\"dt\\\": \\\"%s\\\"}' % dt, json)\\n\\n    def assert_xml_contains_datetime(self, xml, dt):\\n        field = parseString(xml).getElementsByTagName('field')[0]\\n        self.assertXMLEqual(field.childNodes[0].wholeText, dt)\\n\\n    def assert_yaml_contains_datetime(self, yaml, dt):\\n        # Depending on the yaml dumper, '!timestamp' might be absent\\n        self.assertRegex(yaml, r\\\"\\\\n  fields: {dt: !(!timestamp)? '%s'}\\\" % re.escape(dt))\\n\\n    def test_naive_datetime(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('python', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_python_contains_datetime(data, dt)\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('python', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_xml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('xml', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        if not isinstance(serializers.get_serializer('yaml'), serializers.BadSerializer):\\n            data = serializers.serialize('yaml', [Event(dt=dt)], default_flow_style=None)\\n            self.assert_yaml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01 13:20:30\\\")\\n            obj = next(serializers.deserialize('yaml', data)).object\\n            self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n    def test_naive_datetime_with_microsecond(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, 405060)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('python', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_python_contains_datetime(data, dt)\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('python', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30.405\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt.replace(microsecond=405000))\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_xml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30.405060\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('xml', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        if not isinstance(serializers.get_serializer('yaml'), serializers.BadSerializer):\\n            data = serializers.serialize('yaml', [Event(dt=dt)], default_flow_style=None)\\n            self.assert_yaml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01 13:20:30.405060\\\")\\n            obj = next(serializers.deserialize('yaml', data)).object\\n            self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n    def test_aware_datetime_with_microsecond(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, 405060, tzinfo=ICT)\\n\\n        data = self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T17:20:30.405+07:00\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt.replace(microsecond=405000))\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n '5.3':\\n                self.assertEqual(obj.dt.replace(tzinfo=UTC), dt)\\n            else:\\n                self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n\\n# RemovedInDjango50Warning: When the deprecation ends, remove setUpClass() and\\n# USE_L10N=False. The tests should remain because format-related settings will\\n# take precedence over locale-dictated formats.\\n@override_settings(DATETIME_FORMAT='c', TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi', USE_L10N=False, USE_TZ=True)\\nclass TemplateTests(SimpleTestCase):\\n    @classmethod\\n    def setUpClass(cls):\\n        with ignore_warnings(category=RemovedInDjango50Warning):\\n            super().setUpClass()\\n\\n    @requires_tz_support\\n    def test_localtime_templatetag_and_filters(self):\\n        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n        Test the {% localtime %} templatetag and related filters.\\n        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n        datetimes = {\\n            'utc': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC),\\n            'eat': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, tzinfo=EAT),\\n            'ict': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=ICT),\\n            'naive': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30),\\n        }\\n        templates = {\\n            'notag': Template(\\\"{% load tz %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}\\\"),\\n            'noarg': Template(\\n                \\\"{% load tz %}{% localtime %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|\\\"\\n                \\\"{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}{% endlocaltime %}\\\"\\n            ),\\n            'on': Template(\\n                \\\"{% load tz %}{% localtime on %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|\\\"\\n                \\\"{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}{% endlocaltime %}\\\"\\n            ),\\n            'off': Template(\\n                \\\"{% load tz %}{% localtime off %}{{ dt }}|{{ dt|localtime }}|\\\"\\n                \\\"{{ dt|utc }}|{{ dt|timezone:ICT }}{% endlocaltime %}\\\"\\n            ),\\n        }\\n\\n        # Transform a list of keys in 'datetimes' to the expected template\\n        # output. This makes the definition of 'results' more readable.\\n        def t(*result):\\n            return '|'.join(datetimes[key].isoformat() for key in result)\\n\\n        # Results for USE_TZ = True\\n\\n        results = {\\n            'utc': {\\n                'notag': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'noarg': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'on': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'off': t('utc', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n            },\\n            'eat': {\\n                'notag': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'noarg': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'on': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'off': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n            },\\n            'ict': {\\n                'notag': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'noarg': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'on': t('eat', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'off': t('ict', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n            },\\n            'naive': {\\n                'notag': t('naive', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'noarg': t('naive', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'on': t('naive', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n                'off': t('naive', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict'),\\n            }\\n        }\\n\\n        for k1, dt in datetimes.items():\\n            for k2, tpl in templates.items():\\n                ctx = Context({'dt': dt, 'ICT': ICT})\\n                actual = tpl.render(ctx)\\n                expected = results[k1][k2]\\n                self.assertEqual(actual, expected, '%s / %s: %r != %r' % (k1, k2, actual, expected))\\n\\n        # Changes for USE_TZ = False\\n\\n        results['utc']['notag'] = t('utc', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict')\\n        results['ict']['notag'] = t('ict', 'eat', 'utc', 'ict')\\n\\n        with self.settings(USE_TZ=False):\\n            for k1, dt in datetimes.items():\\n                for k2, tpl in templates.items():\\n                    ctx = Context({'dt': dt, 'ICT': ICT})\\n                    actual = tpl.render(ctx)\\n                    expected = results[k1][k2]\\n                    self.assertEqual(actual, expected, '%s / %s: %r != %r' % (k1, k2, actual, expected))\\n\\n    def test_localtime_filters_with_iana(self):\\n        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n        Test the |localtime, |utc, and |timezone filters with iana zones.\\n        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n        # Use an IANA timezone as local time\\n        tpl = Template(\\\"{% load tz %}{{ dt|localtime }}|{{ dt|utc }}\\\")\\n        ctx = Context({'dt': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 12, 20, 30)})\\n\\n        with self.settings(TIME_ZONE='Europe/Paris'):\\n            self.assertEqual(tpl.render(ctx), \\\"2011-09-01T12:20:30+02:00|2011-09-01T10:20:30+00:00\\\")\\n\\n        # Use an IANA timezone as argument\\n        for tz in get_timezones('Europe/Paris'):\\n            with self.subTest(repr(tz)):\\n                tpl = Template(\\\"{% load tz %}{{ dt|timezone:tz }}\\\")\\n                ctx = Context({\\n                    'dt': datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30),\\n                    'tz': zoneinfo.ZoneInfo('Europe/Paris') if zoneinfo else None,\\n                })\\n                self.assertEqual(tpl.render(ctx), \\\"2011-09-01T12:20:30+02:00\\\")\\n\\n    def test_localtime_templatetag_invalid_argument(self):\\n        with self.assertRaises(TemplateSyntaxError):\\n            Template(\\\"{% load tz %}{% localtime foo %}{% endlocaltime %}\\\").render()\\n\\n    def test_localtime_filters_do_not_raise_exceptions(self):\\n        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n        Test the |localtime, |utc, and |timezone filters on Here's what was changed:\n\n### Key Changes:\n\n1. **Import zoneinfo at module level** - Uses stdlib `zoneinfo` for Python 3.9+ (or `backports.zoneinfo` for older Python)\n\n2. **Removed unconditional `import pytz`** - pytz is now only imported conditionally when:\n   - `USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ=True` setting is enabled\n   - A pytz timezone object needs to be detected via `_is_pytz_zone()`\n\n3. **Changed `utc` to `datetime.timezone.utc`** - `utc = timezone.utc` instead of `pytz.utc`\n\n4. **Use `zoneinfo.ZoneInfo` for timezone construction**:\n   - `get_default_timezone()` returns `zoneinfo.ZoneInfo(settings.TIME_ZONE)`\n   - Special case: UTC timezone returns `datetime.timezone.utc` for backwards compatibility\n   - `activate()` with string timezone name creates `zoneinfo.ZoneInfo` instances\n\n5. **Added `USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ` setting support** - When True, falls back to pytz behavior\n\n6. **`is_dst` parameter on `make_aware()`**:\n   - Changed default from `None` to `Ellipsis` (sentinel value)\n   - Emits `RemovedInDjango50Warning` when explicitly passed\n   - Does NOT warn when using the default (no argument passed)\n\n7. **Lazy pytz imports** - `_is_pytz_zone()` and `_datetime_ambiguous_or_imaginary()` now import pytz lazily only when needed\n\nAll 25 tests in `utils_tests.test_timezone` pass successfully."}}}}}
